title: Emit telemetry events for Optimization Suggestions funnel (UI + API)
preview:
We need a closed-loop telemetry funnel for the new Console “Optimization suggestions” beta (batching/caching only). This ticket covers emitting analytics events from the Suggestions UI and adding lightweight server-side logging for Suggestions API usage so we can (a) measure feature adoption, (b) understand where users drop, and (c) debug “why is this empty / why is this estimate weird” reports without relying on screenshots.

Scope (beta):
- UI events from the Suggestions list view and details drawer (impressions → expand → dismiss → apply click / deep link).
- API-side events for /optimize/suggestions list + get-by-id calls (success/failure + latency bucket + response shape fingerprints, but NO request/response bodies).
- Ensure events are org/project scoped and do not include PII (no prompt text, no user-entered labels).
- Provide a stable event taxonomy and properties that can support the funnel dashboard spec.

Out of scope:
- Experiment framework / A/B allocations (can be added later).
- Per-token “savings realized” attribution (requires separate billing/usage reconciliation).

Owners / reviewers:
- Primary: Nadia Rahman (cost attribution pipeline owner)
- Telemetry platform review: Logan Wright
- Metrics ingestion: Caleb Johnson
- Console UI integration: Nora Ahmed (consult)

References:
- Funnel dashboard spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/suggestions-beta-funnel-dashboard-spec.json
- Event taxonomy draft: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/suggestions-events-taxonomy.json
- Suggestions UX PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/optimization-suggestions-console-ux-beta-prd.json

Event taxonomy (proposed)
Event names are prefixed to avoid collisions and to keep dashboards simple.

UI events (client):
1) optimize_suggestions_impression
- When: Suggestions page renders with at least one row (after successful load).
- Notes: For empty/error states, emit separate events below.

2) optimize_suggestions_empty_state
- When: Page renders an empty state.
- Properties include empty_state_reason (see below).

3) optimize_suggestions_row_expand
- When: user opens details drawer from a suggestion row.

4) optimize_suggestions_row_dismiss
- When: user dismisses a suggestion (explicit dismiss action).

5) optimize_suggestions_apply_click
- When: user clicks “Apply” / “Enable” CTA OR clicks deep link to config page to implement recommendation.
- Notes: This is not “applied successfully”; it’s an intent/action click.

6) optimize_suggestions_docs_click
- When: user clicks “How estimates work” or other docs/help link from the Suggestions view.

API events (server):
7) optimize_suggestions_api_request
- When: /optimize/suggestions (list) and /optimize/suggestions/{id} called.
- Includes http_status, route (list|get), latency_ms_bucket, result_count_bucket (list), stale_as_of_present, and error_class (if 4xx/5xx).

Common required properties
All events:
- event_version: "1"
- timestamp_ms: client/server generated
- org_id: Redwood org identifier
- project_id: Redwood project identifier (if present; some org-level views may omit)
- environment: "prod" | "staging"
- actor_type: "user" | "service" (UI events use user; server uses service)
- surface: "console"
- feature: "optimize_suggestions"
- feature_flag_enabled: "true"|"false" (for UI events; server can infer based on authz path)

UI event properties:
- page: "optimize/suggestions"
- ui_variant: "beta"
- table_sort: e.g. "savings_desc" | "risk_asc" | "latency_impact_desc"
- filters_applied: comma-separated key/value pairs (e.g. "risk=low,model=llama-3.1-70b,route=/v1/chat/completions") with allowlisted keys only
- suggestions_visible_count_bucket: "0" | "1-5" | "6-20" | "21-50" | "50+"
- empty_state_reason (for optimize_suggestions_empty_state only): "insufficient_telemetry" | "no_suggestions" | "not_enabled" | "rbac_denied" | "api_error" | "unknown"

Suggestion-specific properties (for expand/dismiss/apply):
- suggestion_id: stable suggestion identifier returned by API
- suggestion_type: "batching" | "caching"
- model_family: allowlisted (e.g. "llama" | "mistral" | "qwen" | "other")
- model_id: Redwood model identifier string (allowlisted format; do not include user-defined aliases)
- route_id: normalized route identifier (NOT full URL with query params)
- region: e.g. "us-east" | "eu-west"
- risk_level: "low" | "medium" | "high"
- confidence_level: "low" | "medium" | "high" (if shown in UI)
- estimated_savings_percent_bucket: "<5" | "5-10" | "10-25" | "25-50" | ">50"
- estimated_savings_usd_per_day_bucket: "<10" | "10-50" | "50-200" | "200-1000" | ">1000"
- estimated_p95_latency_delta_ms_bucket: "<-50" | "-50-0" | "0-50" | "50-150" | ">150"

API request properties:
- endpoint: "/optimize/suggestions" | "/optimize/suggestions/{id}"
- http_status: numeric
- latency_ms_bucket: "<50" | "50-100" | "100-250" | "250-500" | "500-1000" | ">1000"
- result_count_bucket (list): "0" | "1-5" | "6-20" | "21-50" | "50+"
- rbac_decision: "allow" | "deny"
- error_class (optional): "timeout" | "db" | "upstream" | "validation" | "unknown"

Privacy / compliance notes
- Do not send prompt text, completions, customer route names that could contain user IDs/emails, or any free-form text fields.
- Filter values must be allowlisted. For any non-allowlisted filter, emit "other".
- suggestion_id is OK (internal stable identifier) and is necessary for join/debug.
- user_id: do NOT include in event payload for beta. If needed later, we can add pseudonymous actor_id via telemetry platform guidance.

Implementation notes
- UI: use the existing Console analytics client. Events should be debounced so impressions emit once per page load, not per re-render.
- API: emit structured logs that are ingested into metrics (count, latency) and optionally into the event stream. Keep payload minimal.
- Correlation: include request_id in API logs (already standard) and optionally propagate to UI via response header for troubleshooting.

Open questions (resolve during implementation)
- Should “drawer open” fire when user clicks row OR when the drawer actually loads details? Proposed: fire on user action (click) + include drawer_load_result="success|error" in a follow-up event if we need it.
- Should apply click distinguish “enable batching” vs “enable caching”? Proposed: use suggestion_type + cta_target.

CTA targets (apply click only)
- cta_target: "config_batching" | "config_caching" | "rollout_policy" | "docs" | "other"

Links to dashboards and verification are in Acceptance Criteria.

- http_status: numeric
- latency_ms_bucket: "<50" | "50-100" | "100-250" | "250-500" | "500-1000" | ">1000"
- result_count_bucket (list): "0" | "1-5" | "6-20" | "21-50" | "50+"
- rbac_decision: "allow" | "deny"
- error_class (optional): "timeout" | "db" | "upstream" | "validation" | "unknown"

Privacy / compliance notes
- Do not send prompt text, completions, customer route names that could contain user IDs/emails, or any free-form text fields.
- Filter values must be allowlisted. For any non-allowlisted filter, emit "other".
- suggestion_id is OK (internal stable identifier) and is necessary for join/debug.
- user_id: do NOT include in event payload for beta. If needed later, we can add pseudonymous actor_id via telemetry platform guidance.

Implementation notes
- UI: use the existing Console analytics client. Events should be debounced so impressions emit once per page load, not per re-render.
- API: emit structured logs that are ingested into metrics (count, latency) and optionally into the event stream. Keep payload minimal.
- Correlation: include request_id in API logs (already standard) and optionally propagate to UI via response header for troubleshooting.

Open questions (resolve during implementation)
- Should “drawer open” fire when user clicks row OR when the drawer actually loads details? Proposed: fire on user action (click) + include drawer_load_result="success|error" in a follow-up event if we need it.
- Should apply click distinguish “enable batching” vs “enable caching”? Proposed: use suggestion_type + cta_target.

CTA targets (apply click only)
- cta_target: "config_batching" | "config_caching" | "rollout_policy" | "docs" | "other"

Links to dashboards and verification are in Acceptance Criteria.

title: Top cost drivers service: compute ‘what changed’ deltas + explainability strings
preview:
Build the backend computation for Usage Insights “Top cost drivers” and “What changed?” views.

We need deterministic, non-misleading explanations for cost changes for a given scope (workspace/project, time range, filters), primarily broken down by (route, model bucket), with additional drivers (tokens in/out, cache hit rate, retries, fallbacks, streaming overhead, context length distribution, latency-correlated spend).

Deliverables:
- Heuristic definitions + guardrails (thresholds, confidence, suppression rules)
- Driver ranking algorithm (contribution to $ delta)
- Explainability strings (customer-facing copy templates + evidence metrics)
- Experiments/feature flags for tuning thresholds & copy
- Unit tests + fixtures for common scenarios

Primary consumer: Usage Insights Query API (ENG-3814) and Console UI cards (DES-913 / ENG-3815).

Links:
- Brainstorm: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/console-and-tooling/top-cost-drivers-heuristics-brainstorm.json
- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/in-console-usage-insights-prd.json
- ADR (route normalization/attribution): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json

title: Add unit/integration tests for streaming token metering (partials + disconnects)
preview:
Background
We’ve had multiple investigations (Finance + Support) where billed tokens appear to be double-counted or inconsistent for streaming responses, especially when:
- the client disconnects mid-stream
- the server retries/fallbacks mid-stream
- partial chunks are emitted and then the request terminates with a timeout/cancel

This ticket adds regression coverage so metering invariants are enforced in CI/staging. Goal is to prevent a recurrence of the double-counting bugs seen in the retry/fallback path and to make the semantics explicit for RevRec.

Scope
1) Unit tests at the token meter boundary:
- Streamed chunk accounting matches final “billed_tokens” record
- Partial stream + client disconnect does NOT bill completion tokens that were never emitted/committed
- Server-side retry/fallback within a request does NOT double count prompt tokens
- Tool/function-call deltas do not cause token attribution to shift across segments unexpectedly

2) Integration tests in staging (billing hooks enabled):
- E2E streaming request with deterministic content; verify usage_event emitted exactly once (idempotency)
- E2E streaming request where client cancels after N chunks; verify billed tokens == sum of emitted tokens (plus prompt tokens once)
- E2E streaming request with injected transient failure triggering server retry; verify billed tokens are not duplicated

Non-goals
- Changing the billing logic itself (covered by ENG-4430 / PRs). This ticket is test-only unless we find a gap that requires a small fix.

Notes
- All tests must run without relying on external model providers. Use the local deterministic tokenizer + stubbed streaming generator.
- Where exact token counts differ by tokenizer/model, assert invariants (monotonicity, “once per request”, and “no tokens after disconnect”) + validate against a golden trace for one canonical model.

title: Telemetry input contracts + data quality checks for Standard Cost Model v1
preview:
## Context
We’re standardizing cost computation across Console + Optimize. The Cost Model API v1 needs consistent telemetry inputs (units, semantics, and availability guarantees) so that:
- Console cost breakdowns are stable and comparable over time.
- Optimize what-if projections have the right batch/cache/quant signals.
- Finance reporting can reconcile predicted/request-attributed cost vs observed spend with known/controlled drift.

Today we have multiple partially-overlapping sources for token metering and runtime metrics (prefill/decode split, KV cache hits, batch sizes), with naming drift and unclear null/unknown semantics.

This ticket defines **telemetry input contracts** (schemas + semantics + required SLIs) and implements **data quality checks** so Cost Model API can treat inputs deterministically.

## Scope (v1)
### Telemetry inputs to formalize (contracted fields)
1) **Token metering (request-level)**
- `request_id` (string, globally unique within env)
- `tenant_id` (string)
- `workspace_id` (string) if applicable
- `deployment_mode` (hosted | dedicated | private)
- `model_id` (catalog stable id)
- `model_version` (semver-ish or catalog version string)
- `quant_profile_id` (stable ID; required when quant != fp16)
- `region` (cloud region string)
- `route_id` (Console route / API key route / internal route)
- `timestamp_start_ms`, `timestamp_end_ms`

Token counts **must** be split and attributed:
- `tokens_input_total` (prompt tokens actually processed, after any prefix-cache reuse is applied)
- `tokens_output_total`
- `tokens_prefill` (tokens processed in prefill stage)
- `tokens_decode` (tokens processed in decode stage)

Required invariants:
- `tokens_prefill + tokens_decode >= tokens_input_total + tokens_output_total` is allowed if runtime counts include special/system tokens; if so, runtime must also provide `token_counting_mode` so cost model can normalize.
- If prefill/decode split is unavailable, emit `tokens_prefill = -1`, `tokens_decode = -1` (NOT null), and set `token_split_status = missing`.

2) **KV cache / prefix cache (request-level + aggregate)**
We need enough to model cache value and to avoid over-attributing prefill.
- `kv_cache_enabled` (true/false as string in metrics pipeline)
- `kv_cache_hit_tokens` (tokens skipped due to cache hits)
- `kv_cache_miss_tokens`
- `kv_cache_hit_rate` (0..1) if computed, else -1
- `cache_scope` (request | tenant | shared)
- `cache_keyspace` (model_version + quant_profile + tokenizer_hash)

Semantics:
- `kv_cache_hit_tokens` counts tokens *not* recomputed in prefill due to prefix/KV reuse.
- For v1 cost breakdown taxonomy, cache hits should be represented as a **delta** in prefill compute cost (not as negative tokens).

3) **Batching / scheduling stats (request-level)**
These drive batch efficiency modeling and explain cost/latency variance.
- `batch_id` (string; stable per runtime scheduling window)
- `batch_size_effective` (int)
- `batch_size_max` (int)
- `queue_time_ms` (time waiting before entering GPU execution)
- `gpu_time_ms` (wall time of GPU execution for request)
- `continuous_batching_enabled` (true/false)
- `microbatch_count` (int; -1 if unknown)

Semantics:
- `batch_size_effective` should reflect the concurrency level during the request’s decode steps; for prefill-only requests, it can be prefill batch size.
- `gpu_time_ms` should be inclusive of kernel execution and exclude known host-side overhead if separable; if not separable, set `gpu_time_includes_host_overhead = true`.

4) **Runtime identifiers required for cost mapping**
- `runtime_engine` (e.g., redwood-runtime)
- `runtime_version`
- `kernel_profile` (opaque string, used to map to perf tables)
- `hardware_sku` (e.g., A100-80GB, H100-SXM)

### Data quality checks to implement
Checks must run in telemetry ingestion (preferred) and be queryable by the cost-model service.

A) **Schema presence + type checks**
- Hard fail for missing: `request_id`, `tenant_id`, `model_id`, `timestamp_start_ms`, `timestamp_end_ms`, `tokens_input_total`, `tokens_output_total`.
- Soft fail (flag) for missing: `quant_profile_id` (only if quant != fp16), `hardware_sku`, `kernel_profile`, `batch_size_effective`.

B) **Range and sanity checks**
- Tokens must be >= 0; allow -1 only for explicitly “unknown” fields noted above.
- `timestamp_end_ms >= timestamp_start_ms`.
- `queue_time_ms` and `gpu_time_ms` must be >= 0.
- `kv_cache_hit_rate` must be -1 or within [0, 1].

C) **Consistency checks**
- If `kv_cache_enabled=false`, require `kv_cache_hit_tokens=0` and `kv_cache_miss_tokens>=0` (or both -1 if cache metrics missing).
- If `continuous_batching_enabled=false`, `microbatch_count` should be 1 or -1.

D) **Completeness SLIs (pipeline health)**
Emit per env/region/model:
- `% requests with prefill/decode split present`
- `% requests with kv_cache metrics present`
- `% requests with batch metrics present`
- Telemetry lag p50/p95 (event time to availability)

These SLIs are used by Cost Model API to set confidence/quality flags and by SRE for alerting.

## Deliverables
- Confluence-style contract doc (embedded here for now; publish link once posted): field names, units, unknown semantics, invariants.
- Metric dictionary updates in telemetry pipeline (names/units).
- A “contract validator” job that:
  - samples N requests per env/region/model per hour
  - computes violation counts
  - exports to Grafana + stores a daily report in analytics
- A compatibility shim for old metric names (2-week transition window) and a sunset plan.

## Out of scope
- Changing billing system of record.
- Full customer-facing explanations; internal correctness first.

## Acceptance criteria
- Token metering fields and semantics are documented and approved by Runtime (Omar) + Cost Model API lead (Ethan).
- Runtime + telemetry exporters emit the contracted fields for at least: hosted/us-east-1 + dedicated/us-west-2 in staging.
- Contract validator runs hourly in staging and daily in prod with dashboards showing pass/fail rates.
- Cost Model API v1 can compute a cost estimate without falling back to legacy fields for >= 95% of requests in staging for the top 5 models.
- Unknown/missing metrics are represented using agreed sentinel values (-1) and surfaced via `input_quality_flags` in the service (service implementation tracked in ENG-4821/4822).

## Implementation notes
- Prefer to normalize field naming at ingestion rather than in cost-model service.
- Provide a “version” tag for the telemetry contract, e.g. `cost_model_input_contract_version=v1`, to support future v2 additions.
- Keep cardinality bounded: do NOT add raw per-kernel counters; only ship request-level aggregates plus stable IDs.

## References / links
- Confluence PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/standard-cost-model-prd.json
- Design spec: sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/cost-model-api-v1-design-spec.json
- Runtime inputs doc (to align names): sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/benchmarking-and-perf-suite/cost-model-inputs-from-runtime-metrics.json
- Drift notes: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/cost-drift-analysis-notes.json
- Related tickets: ENG-4822 (schema), ENG-4828 (runtime instrumentation), ENG-4827 (drift SLI), ENG-4832 (billing hooks alignment)

## Open questions
1) Prefill/decode split definition: do we include “speculative decoding” tokens in decode or a separate bucket? (Tentative: include in decode; add `decode_mode` later in v2.)
2) Cache warmness: Optimize wants a “warm-rate” parameter. For v1, we’ll infer from observed hit_rate and expose a confidence flag; explicit warm-rate input deferred.
3) GPU time attribution: runtime reports per-request gpu_time_ms; need to confirm whether it’s proportional attribution from batch or measured from instrumentation.

## Updates / comments (internal)
- 2026-01-16 (Logan Wright): Drafted initial field list based on current token-metering + runtime exports. Need Omar to confirm prefill/decode semantics.
- 2026-01-22 (Nadia Rahman): Proposed sentinel strategy (-1) for unknowns to avoid null-handling differences across languages. Asked Console to confirm they can display “unknown” in breakdown.
- 2026-01-28 (Sofia Ivanova): Batch metrics: `batch_size_effective` is stable; `microbatch_count` is not always available for older runtime versions. OK to allow -1.
- 2026-02-03 (Omar El-Sayed): Prefill/decode split is accurate only after runtime-1.18 patch; prior versions sometimes double-count prefill for short prompts. Add validator check comparing split sum vs totals + version gating.
- 2026-02-10 (Thomas Baird): Validator job must sample; do not attempt to validate all requests due to cost. Suggested stratified sampling by (model_id, region, deployment_mode).
- 2026-02-14 (Ethan Park): Cost Model API will expose `input_quality_flags` and `contract_version` in responses; ensure telemetry has consistent tags so service can detect missingness without extra joins.
- 2026-02-18 (Caleb Johnson): Implemented ingestion-side renames for legacy fields; pending review. Need to confirm final field names with API guidelines (naming: snake_case vs camelCase). Proposed: keep snake_case in telemetry store; service maps to API schema.
- 2026-02-21 (Allison Grant): Please ensure lag SLI is measured on event_time vs ingestion_time, not wall-clock, to avoid noisy alerts during backfills.

title: Usage Insights: cardinality guardrails + sampling for route/model breakdowns
preview:
Problem
Usage Insights requires grouping usage/cost by route + model. Without guardrails, the route dimension can explode (e.g., untemplated IDs, user-defined paths, OpenAI-compat proxies), driving:
- warehouse costs (scan + group-by)
- slow/timeout queries for 7–30d ranges
- noisy/misleading “top drivers” (long tail dominates)
- ingestion issues (high-cardinality tags)

This ticket implements guardrails across ingestion/aggregation/query to keep cardinality bounded while preserving useful reporting.

Goals
1) Prevent route dimension explosion in the aggregated dataset and query service.
2) Handle untemplated or unknown paths safely (bucket + visibility).
3) Cap query complexity so the console remains interactive (and export remains reliable).
4) Add alerts so we detect regressions early (new route patterns, normalization failures, abusive queries, etc.).

Non-goals
- Changing the canonical route normalization rules themselves (owned by ENG-3813 / ADR).
- Building per-customer custom dimensions beyond the fixed set.

Proposed guardrails (implementation)
A) Ingestion + normalization hardening
- Enforce use of normalized_route from the shared library (ENG-3813) at the point of metering event creation (ENG-3822).
- Introduce a dedicated field: route_group (string) used for analytics, which is:
  - the normalized templated route if normalization succeeds (e.g., "/v1/files/{file_id}")
  - otherwise one of:
    - "/__unknown_route" (missing/empty)
    - "/__untemplated" (detected dynamic segment w/o template)
    - "/__invalid" (malformed)
- Add boolean flags on event -> aggregation input:
  - route_is_templated
  - route_is_openai_compat (best-effort; based on gateway route map)

Notes
- We must keep raw route_path out of the aggregation key. Raw route is allowed only in debug logs (sampled) and never in the warehouse grouping key.

B) Aggregation dataset constraints (ENG-3812 integration)
- Aggregate key uses route_group, not raw path.
- Additional constraints at aggregation time:
  - Cap distinct route_group per workspace+day at N (proposal: N=2,000). If exceeded, automatically bucket long-tail routes into "/__other_routes" with route_overflow_count incremented.
  - For each workspace+day+model_family, optionally store TopK routes (proposal: K=200) + “other” bucket.
- Emit dataset-level metrics:
  - distinct_route_group_count{workspace_id, bucket=hour|day}
  - route_overflow_triggered_count
  - route_unknown_count, route_untemplated_count
  - percent_routes_untemplated

Rationale
The Console breakdown is a “top costs” UI; TopK + other maintains accurate totals while preventing a pathological group-by.

C) Query service guardrails (ENG-3814 integration)
Hard limits (server-side; applies to console + API + export initiation):
- time range max:
  - interactive endpoints: 31 days
  - export endpoints: 90 days (async only) with explicit warnings and rate limiting
- max group-by dimensions per request: 2 (route_group, model_family/model_version_bucket). Additional dimensions allowed only if they are low-cardinality and explicitly whitelisted (deployment_mode, region) and only one of them can be added at a time.
- max page size: 500 rows (default 100)
- max “include breakdown details” toggles: reject requests that request per-route per-model per-region per-project simultaneously.

Adaptive behavior
- If a request hits a complexity threshold (estimated by planner), return 422 with a structured error explaining how to narrow (shorter range, drop dimension, filter to a model/route first).
- For console UI: we should fail gracefully with a “Too much data” banner and suggested filters, not a generic error.

Query complexity estimator (v1)
- Complexity score = (days * dims_multiplier * estimated_distinct_routes * estimated_distinct_models)
- Use recent stats table (daily distinct counts) to estimate; fall back to conservative defaults if missing.

D) Sampling (debug-only)
We still need visibility into what is causing route explosions.
- Add a sampled debug log stream (e.g., 0.1% per workspace, capped) that logs raw route_path + normalized_route + reason for bucketing.
- The sampled logs are for internal debugging only and must not be exposed in customer-facing exports.

E) Alerting + dashboards (SRE/Observability)
Add alerts (paging vs ticket) with thresholds tuned during beta:
- distinct_route_group_count spike (e.g., >2x weekly baseline) for a workspace or globally
- percent_routes_untemplated > 5% for >30 minutes (global) OR >10% for a specific workspace
- route_overflow_triggered_count > 0 for any workspace in the last hour (ticket during beta; page only if sustained)
- query_rejected_complexity_count spikes
- p95 query latency correlated with high distinct route counts (for diagnosing)

Links / references
- ADR: Route normalization and attribution semantics: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json
- Query service doc: sources/confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/usage-insights-query-service.json
- Data freshness/quality plan: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/usage-insights-data-freshness-and-quality.json
- Related support pain: SUP-24827 (missing route breakdown for OpenAI-compat), SUP-24863 (timeouts for 30-day range)

Acceptance criteria
- Aggregated dataset key is route_group (templated) and does not include raw path.
- Distinct route groups are capped (TopK + “other” bucketing) without changing totals.
- Query API rejects overly complex requests deterministically with a user-actionable error.
- Console can render the route/model breakdown table for 30 days for a typical enterprise workspace with p95 < SLO target (see ENG-3821) and without timeouts.
- Alerts exist for route cardinality spikes, untemplated routes, overflow bucketing, and complexity rejections.
- Documentation snippet for internal teams: how to debug route explosions using sampled logs + metrics (add to runbook or internal notes).

Open questions
- What should N (cap per workspace+day) be for GA? Proposal starts at 2,000; verify against top beta accounts.
- Should "/__other_routes" be shown as “Other” in the UI (preferred) or hidden? PM to confirm.
- For OpenAI-compat endpoints, do we want a separate normalization map in gateway to avoid "/__untemplated"? Likely yes, but may be a follow-up.

Rollout plan
- Gate overflow bucketing behind a server-side flag: usage_insights_route_cap_enabled.
- Beta: enable for internal + allowlist accounts first; watch distinct_route_group_count and overflow.
- GA: enable globally once thresholds are stable and we have validated totals vs billing (ENG-3817).
text:
Problem
Usage Insights requires grouping usage/cost by route + model. Without guardrails, the route dimension can explode (e.g., untemplated IDs, user-defined paths, OpenAI-compat proxies), driving:
- warehouse costs (scan + group-by)
- slow/timeout queries for 7–30d ranges
- noisy/misleading “top drivers” (long tail dominates)
- ingestion issues (high-cardinality tags)

This ticket implements guardrails across ingestion/aggregation/query to keep cardinality bounded while preserving useful reporting.

Goals
1) Prevent route dimension explosion in the aggregated dataset and query service.
2) Handle untemplated or unknown paths safely (bucket + visibility).
3) Cap query complexity so the console remains interactive (and export remains reliable).
4) Add alerts so we detect regressions early (new route patterns, normalization failures, abusive queries, etc.).

Non-goals
- Changing the canonical route normalization rules themselves (owned by ENG-3813 / ADR).
- Building per-customer custom dimensions beyond the fixed set.

Proposed guardrails (implementation)
A) Ingestion + normalization hardening
- Enforce use of normalized_route from the shared library (ENG-3813) at the point of metering event creation (ENG-3822).
- Introduce a dedicated field: route_group (string) used for analytics, which is:
  - the normalized templated route if normalization succeeds (e.g., "/v1/files/{file_id}")
  - otherwise one of:
    - "/__unknown_route" (missing/empty)
    - "/__untemplated" (detected dynamic segment w/o template)
    - "/__invalid" (malformed)
- Add boolean flags on event -> aggregation input:
  - route_is_templated
  - route_is_openai_compat (best-effort; based on gateway route map)

Notes
- We must keep raw route_path out of the aggregation key. Raw route is allowed only in debug logs (sampled) and never in the warehouse grouping key.

B) Aggregation dataset constraints (ENG-3812 integration)
- Aggregate key uses route_group, not raw path.
- Additional constraints at aggregation time:
  - Cap distinct route_group per workspace+day at N (proposal: N=2,000). If exceeded, automatically bucket long-tail routes into "/__other_routes" with route_overflow_count incremented.
  - For each workspace+day+model_family, optionally store TopK routes (proposal: K=200) + “other” bucket.
- Emit dataset-level metrics:
  - distinct_route_group_count{workspace_id, bucket=hour|day}
  - route_overflow_triggered_count
  - route_unknown_count, route_untemplated_count
  - percent_routes_untemplated

Rationale
The Console breakdown is a “top costs” UI; TopK + other maintains accurate totals while preventing a pathological group-by.

C) Query service guardrails (ENG-3814 integration)
Hard limits (server-side; applies to console + API + export initiation):
- time range max:
  - interactive endpoints: 31 days
  - export endpoints: 90 days (async only) with explicit warnings and rate limiting
- max group-by dimensions per request: 2 (route_group, model_family/model_version_bucket). Additional dimensions allowed only if they are low-cardinality and explicitly whitelisted (deployment_mode, region) and only one of them can be added at a time.
- max page size: 500 rows (default 100)
- max “include breakdown details” toggles: reject requests that request per-route per-model per-region per-project simultaneously.

Adaptive behavior
- If a request hits a complexity threshold (estimated by planner), return 422 with a structured error explaining how to narrow (shorter range, drop dimension, filter to a model/route first).
- For console UI: we should fail gracefully with a “Too much data” banner and suggested filters, not a generic error.

Query complexity estimator (v1)
- Complexity score = (days * dims_multiplier * estimated_distinct_routes * estimated_distinct_models)
- Use recent stats table (daily distinct counts) to estimate; fall back to conservative defaults if missing.

D) Sampling (debug-only)
We still need visibility into what is causing route explosions.
- Add a sampled debug log stream (e.g., 0.1% per workspace, capped) that logs raw route_path + normalized_route + reason for bucketing.
- The sampled logs are for internal debugging only and must not be exposed in customer-facing exports.

E) Alerting + dashboards (SRE/Observability)
Add alerts (paging vs ticket) with thresholds tuned during beta:
- distinct_route_group_count spike (e.g., >2x weekly baseline) for a workspace or globally
- percent_routes_untemplated > 5% for >30 minutes (global) OR >10% for a specific workspace
- route_overflow_triggered_count > 0 for any workspace in the last hour (ticket during beta; page only if sustained)
- query_rejected_complexity_count spikes
- p95 query latency correlated with high distinct route counts (for diagnosing)

Links / references
- ADR: Route normalization and attribution semantics: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json
- Query service doc: sources/confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/usage-insights-query-service.json
- Data freshness/quality plan: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/usage-insights-data-freshness-and-quality.json
- Related support pain: SUP-24827 (missing route breakdown for OpenAI-compat), SUP-24863 (timeouts for 30-day range)

Acceptance criteria

- Distinct route groups are capped (TopK + “other” bucketing) without changing totals.
- Query API rejects overly complex requests deterministically with a user-actionable error.
- Console can render the route/model breakdown table for 30 days for a typical enterprise workspace with p95 < SLO target (see ENG-3821) and without timeouts.
- Alerts exist for route cardinality spikes, untemplated routes, overflow bucketing, and complexity rejections.
- Documentation snippet for internal teams: how to debug route explosions using sampled logs + metrics (add to runbook or internal notes).

Open questions
- What should N (cap per workspace+day) be for GA? Proposal starts at 2,000; verify against top beta accounts.
- Should "/__other_routes" be shown as “Other” in the UI (preferred) or hidden? PM to confirm.
- For OpenAI-compat endpoints, do we want a separate normalization map in gateway to avoid "/__untemplated"? Likely yes, but may be a follow-up.

Rollout plan
- Gate overflow bucketing behind a server-side flag: usage_insights_route_cap_enabled.
- Beta: enable for internal + allowlist accounts first; watch distinct_route_group_count and overflow.
- GA: enable globally once thresholds are stable and we have validated totals vs billing (ENG-3817).

title: Instrumentation: add/verify normalized route tag on metering events at ingestion
preview:
Usage Insights requires a stable `route` dimension for cost attribution by API route + model. Today a non-trivial subset of metering events arrive without a reliable route tag (notably streaming endpoints and some OpenAI-compat paths), which leads to “Unknown route” buckets and mismatches vs expectations.

This ticket adds/verified route tags in metering events at ingestion:
- Prefer emitted route fields from gateway when present.
- If missing, derive from request metadata available at ingestion (HTTP method + raw path), then normalize/canonicalize via the shared route normalization library.
- Record provenance (emitted vs derived) to support debugging and rollout.

This is intended to unblock accurate aggregations in ENG-3812 and reduce support issues like missing route breakdowns and unexplained “unknown route” spend.

title: Shadow traffic telemetry events v1 (shadow request/response pair + scoring refs) with cardinality + PII constraints
preview:
Goal
Ship v1 telemetry events and ingestion logic for shadow traffic pairs so we can compute quality deltas (control vs shadow) and attach them to rollouts/experiments without blowing up metrics cardinality or leaking sensitive prompt data.

Background
Shadow traffic duplicates a sampled subset of production requests to one or more “shadow targets” while returning the control response to the end user. We need structured telemetry that:
- correlates control + shadow results via stable fingerprinting and bucketing
- captures minimal request/response metadata required for latency/cost/error and quality scoring
- references (not embeds) redacted artifacts for later scoring
- enforces strict cardinality limits across Hosted/Dedicated/Private

Primary stakeholders
- Telemetry/Analytics: Logan Wright (lead), Nadia Rahman (assignee), Caleb Johnson
- Routing: Ravi Menon / Mina Haddad
- Applied ML/Evals: Jonas Weber / Selene Huang
- SRE: Allison Grant / Sean Gallagher
- Security: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei

Links
- ADR (data model + cardinality): sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-shadow-metrics-data-model-and-cardinality.json
- Retention/redaction policy: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/shadow-traffic-data-retention-and-redaction.json
- Event schema draft (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/shadow-traffic-event-schema-draft.json
- Cardinality/dimensions review sheet: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/shadow-traffic-metrics-dimensions-sheet.json
- Related PR (expected): sources/github/redwood/pr-24872-shadow-telemetry-events-and-ingestion.json

Scope (v1)
1) New telemetry events
Introduce two primary events (names per observability standard; final naming to match ADR):
- shadow_pair_v1: single row per (control_response, shadow_response) attempt
- shadow_scoring_result_v1: row per scoring job completion (rubric version pinned), keyed back to shadow_pair_v1

2) Ingestion logic + storage
- Ingest events into analytics pipeline with explicit schema enforcement + reject/soft-drop for invalid/high-cardinality payloads
- Persist to shadow_pairs table (hot, short retention) and scoring_results table
- Provide materialized aggregates per (tenant, route, experiment_id, variant_id, time_window) for Console dashboards

3) Dimension constraints (hard requirements)
- Must not emit raw prompt text or raw model outputs in telemetry events
- Must not include user identifiers (email, phone, IP, device id) as dimensions
- Must cap cardinality for route_id / experiment_id / model_id / region dimensions (per ADR)
- Must bucket or drop free-form tags/headers (only allow allowlisted header keys)

4) PII handling requirements
- Deterministic request_fingerprint is salted and HMACed; never reversible without tenant-specific secrets
- Any content references must point to redacted artifacts (separate store) and be optional
- Explicit “pii_redaction_applied=true/false” and “artifact_capture_mode=none|metadata_only|redacted_text” fields for auditability

Out of scope (v1)
- Customer-defined arbitrary dimensions
- Long-term storage of prompt/response content
- Full segmentation across arbitrary request attributes (we will keep segments limited + controlled)

Proposed schema (v1)
Event: shadow_pair_v1
Required fields:
- ts
- tenant_id
- env: hosted|dedicated|private
- request_type: chat|completion|embeddings|rerank
- route_id
- shadow_experiment_id (nullable for “policy-only” shadowing, but preferred)
- rollout_id (nullable)
- control: model_id, model_version (or runtime profile), quant_profile, region
- shadow: target_id (variant), model_id, model_version, quant_profile, region
- request_fingerprint (HMAC)
- bucket_id (0..N-1), sample_rate (basis points)
- outcome_control: ok|error|timeout|canceled
- outcome_shadow: ok|error|timeout|canceled|skipped
- latency_ms_control, latency_ms_shadow (if attempted)
- tokens_in, tokens_out_control, tokens_out_shadow (nullable depending on request_type)
- cache_hit_control/cache_hit_shadow (boolean, nullable)
- error_class_control/error_class_shadow (enum; no raw error strings)
- artifact_ref_control/artifact_ref_shadow (opaque IDs; may be empty)
- pii_redaction_applied (boolean)

Event: shadow_scoring_result_v1
Required fields:
- ts
- tenant_id
- shadow_experiment_id
- request_fingerprint
- rubric_id, rubric_version
- scorer: model_graded|heuristic|hybrid
- score_status: ok|error|skipped
- metrics: flattened key/value pairs restricted to a small allowlist (see “Metric allowlist”)
- aggregate_group: (optional) “default” only in v1 to avoid high-cardinality segment keys

Metric allowlist (initial)
- format_adherence_pass (0/1)
- refusal (0/1)
- safety_flag (0/1)
- instruction_following_score (0..1)
- empty_answer (0/1)
- json_parseable (0/1)
Note: metrics must be emitted as fixed columns in storage; do not allow arbitrary metric names in v1.

Cardinality guardrails (must-have)
- Drop any dimension values exceeding max length (e.g., 128 bytes) with reason=dimension_too_long
- Enforce allowlist for headers captured into telemetry (“x-redwood-request-class”, “x-redwood-use-case-id” if approved); default capture none
- Enforce max unique values per hour for route_id and experiment_id per tenant; if exceeded, degrade to route_id=OTHER and emit counter for drops
- Do not include prompt_id, user_id, session_id, request_id as metric labels (they can exist only as opaque artifact refs for sampled examples)

Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Events emitted from data plane for shadowed traffic with stable correlation between control + shadow
- [ ] Ingestion validates schema; invalid events are counted and soft-dropped (no pipeline instability)
- [ ] Verified no raw prompt/output text in event payloads end-to-end
- [ ] Cardinality in staging stays within agreed limits at 5% sampling for internal dogfood tenant
- [ ] Dashboards can compute: shadow attempt rate, shadow success rate, delta latency, delta tokens, scoring lag (even if scoring pipeline not fully rolled out)
- [ ] Security review sign-off on PII handling + retention references

Testing plan
- Unit tests: schema validation, allowlist enforcement, HMAC fingerprint deterministic behavior (given fixed secret)
- Integration tests: emit shadow_pair_v1 from staging router → ingestion → table row + aggregate
- Load test validation: simulate worst-case route fanout + many experiments; verify drops degrade gracefully

Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag: shadow_telemetry_v1
- Stage 1: internal tenant only (dogfood), 1% sampling, no artifact refs
- Stage 2: internal tenant, 5% sampling, artifact refs enabled (redacted)
- Stage 3: beta Dedicated tenants (per PM beta plan), 1–2% sampling default

Risks / open questions
- Streaming: if shadowing ends early, do we emit partial token counts? Proposal: emit tokens_out_shadow=null + outcome_shadow=canceled/timeout and rely on attempt metrics.
- Multi-shadow targets: v1 supports emitting one event per shadow target (fanout). Need to confirm how to cap targets to protect ingestion.
- Private deployments: confirm whether events go to customer-controlled sink; ensure schema identical but configurable exporters.

Owner notes
This issue owns the telemetry contract and ingestion enforcement. Artifact capture/redaction is owned by ENG-5411; we will only emit artifact refs once those invariants are met.

tags: Shadow traffic tool for eval metrics

title: Add/adjust dashboard panels for KV cache compaction (fragmentation, bytes moved, evictions, p99 correlation)
preview:
Goal
Add/adjust the standard runtime/SRE dashboards to support safe rollout + ongoing operations of KV cache compaction and tier-aware eviction. This ticket is explicitly about panels and dashboard ergonomics (not changing metric emission).

Why
We need to (a) see fragmentation behavior over time, (b) understand compaction cost (bytes moved/time) and eviction rate, and (c) correlate compaction/eviction activity to tail latency (p95/p99) during mixed-length bursts. During canary, oncall needs a single dashboard that answers: “Are we compacting because we’re fragmented?”, “Are we compacting too aggressively (latency spikes)?”, “Are we evicting at the right time/tier?”, and “Should we rollback/flip kill-switch?”.

Scope
1) New dashboard (or new section on existing Runtime / Allocator Health dashboard): “KV Cache Compaction”.
2) Required panels (minimum viable for canary):
   - Fragmentation ratio over time (overall + per-pool breakdown) with annotations for deploys/flag flips.
   - KV arena free/used bytes + largest contiguous free span (or equivalent allocator health signal if that’s what allocator exposes).
   - Compaction bytes moved (rate + total) and compaction time (p50/p95) with budget/limit overlay.
   - Eviction count/rate, eviction reasons (pressure/threshold/budget exhaustion) and tier breakdown (Hosted vs Dedicated, and priority tiers if exported).
   - Tail latency correlation: overlay (a) decode p99 latency and (b) compaction bytes moved rate, plus exemplars linking to traces when compaction spans are present.
   - Tail latency correlation: overlay eviction rate vs p99, to catch “evict storms” that coincide with latency spikes.
3) Dashboard filters and grouping (consistent across panels): region, cluster/pool, model, tier, GPU type, runtime version/build, canary cohort.
4) Add links from the compaction runbook + rollout checklist to the dashboard.

Non-goals
- Creating new alerts in this ticket (those are tracked in SRE guardrail work); we will add “suggested thresholds” annotations only if already ratified by SRE.
- Redesigning metric naming or cardinality; any changes discovered here should be follow-ups to ENG-4822.

Acceptance criteria
- Oncall can open a single dashboard and, within ~2 minutes, determine whether tail spikes correlate with: fragmentation increasing, compaction being too active (bytes moved/time), or eviction surges.
- Dashboard contains the panels listed above, with the standard filters (region/pool/model/tier/version).
- At least one panel uses exemplars/trace links (via compaction spans) to jump to a representative trace around a spike.
- Dashboard is placed in the standard SRE folder and referenced in the compaction runbook.

Notes / open questions
- Metric naming: follow the Confluence metrics standard; if emitted metric names differ from the standard, we should document the delta and file a tight follow-up to ENG-4822.
- Cardinality: for tier and model breakdowns, keep defaults sane (top-N by traffic) to avoid slow dashboard loads; add “advanced breakdown” panels if needed.
- p99 source of truth: use the same latency metric SRE uses for Hosted API tail latency guardrails to avoid mismatches during rollback decisions.

Proposed dashboard structure (draft)
A) Health summary
- Fragmentation ratio (time series) + threshold band
- Largest contiguous free span (time series) / allocator health score (if available)
B) Compaction activity
- Bytes moved rate (stacked by reason/phase if available)
- Compaction time ms (p50/p95) + budget (configured)
C) Eviction activity
- Evictions/sec (stacked by tier)
- Eviction reason counts (pressure vs policy)
D) Correlation
- Decode p99 latency overlay with compaction bytes moved rate
- Decode p99 latency overlay with eviction rate
- Event annotations for feature flag changes / deploys

Implementation plan
- Build the dashboard off existing runtime metrics dashboards and import into the standard folder.
- Wire trace exemplars using compaction span IDs where available (PR-19405).
- Review with SRE (Sean/Allison) and Runtime (Omar/Sofia) before canary expansion.

Risks
- Slow dashboards due to high-cardinality labels; mitigate by limiting breakdown panels and using template variables.
- Confusion during incident response if multiple latency metrics exist; mitigate by clearly labeling the latency metric and linking to SRE guardrails.

Follow-ups (if needed)
- Add “deploy / flag flip annotations” integration if not already present in our dashboard framework.
- Add per-model default views for known-problem models/pools once we have canary data.
text:
Goal
Add/adjust the standard runtime/SRE dashboards to support safe rollout + ongoing operations of KV cache compaction and tier-aware eviction. This ticket is explicitly about panels and dashboard ergonomics (not changing metric emission).

Why
We need to (a) see fragmentation behavior over time, (b) understand compaction cost (bytes moved/time) and eviction rate, and (c) correlate compaction/eviction activity to tail latency (p95/p99) during mixed-length bursts. During canary, oncall needs a single dashboard that answers: “Are we compacting because we’re fragmented?”, “Are we compacting too aggressively (latency spikes)?”, “Are we evicting at the right time/tier?”, and “Should we rollback/flip kill-switch?”.

Scope
1) New dashboard (or new section on existing Runtime / Allocator Health dashboard): “KV Cache Compaction”.
2) Required panels (minimum viable for canary):
   - Fragmentation ratio over time (overall + per-pool breakdown) with annotations for deploys/flag flips.
   - KV arena free/used bytes + largest contiguous free span (or equivalent allocator health signal if that’s what allocator exposes).
   - Compaction bytes moved (rate + total) and compaction time (p50/p95) with budget/limit overlay.
   - Eviction count/rate, eviction reasons (pressure/threshold/budget exhaustion) and tier breakdown (Hosted vs Dedicated, and priority tiers if exported).
   - Tail latency correlation: overlay (a) decode p99 latency and (b) compaction bytes moved rate, plus exemplars linking to traces when compaction spans are present.
   - Tail latency correlation: overlay eviction rate vs p99, to catch “evict storms” that coincide with latency spikes.
3) Dashboard filters and grouping (consistent across panels): region, cluster/pool, model, tier, GPU type, runtime version/build, canary cohort.
4) Add links from the compaction runbook + rollout checklist to the dashboard.

Non-goals
- Creating new alerts in this ticket (those are tracked in SRE guardrail work); we will add “suggested thresholds” annotations only if already ratified by SRE.
- Redesigning metric naming or cardinality; any changes discovered here should be follow-ups to ENG-4822.

Acceptance criteria
- Oncall can open a single dashboard and, within ~2 minutes, determine whether tail spikes correlate with: fragmentation increasing, compaction being too active (bytes moved/time), or eviction surges.
- Dashboard contains the panels listed above, with the standard filters (region/pool/model/tier/version).
- At least one panel uses exemplars/trace links (via compaction spans) to jump to a representative trace around a spike.
- Dashboard is placed in the standard SRE folder and referenced in the compaction runbook.

Notes / open questions
- Metric naming: follow the Confluence metrics standard; if emitted metric names differ from the standard, we should document the delta and file a tight follow-up to ENG-4822.
- Cardinality: for tier and model breakdowns, keep defaults sane (top-N by traffic) to avoid slow dashboard loads; add “advanced breakdown” panels if needed.
- p99 source of truth: use the same latency metric SRE uses for Hosted API tail latency guardrails to avoid mismatches during rollback decisions.

Proposed dashboard structure (draft)
A) Health summary
- Fragmentation ratio (time series) + threshold band
- Largest contiguous free span (time series) / allocator health score (if available)
B) Compaction activity
- Bytes moved rate (stacked by reason/phase if available)
- Compaction time ms (p50/p95) + budget (configured)
C) Eviction activity
- Evictions/sec (stacked by tier)
- Eviction reason counts (pressure vs policy)
D) Correlation
- Decode p99 latency overlay with compaction bytes moved rate
- Decode p99 latency overlay with eviction rate
- Event annotations for feature flag changes / deploys

Implementation plan
- Build the dashboard off existing runtime metrics dashboards and import into the standard folder.
- Wire trace exemplars using compaction span IDs where available (PR-19405).
- Review with SRE (Sean/Allison) and Runtime (Omar/Sofia) before canary expansion.

Risks
- Slow dashboards due to high-cardinality labels; mitigate by limiting breakdown panels and using template variables.
- Confusion during incident response if multiple latency metrics exist; mitigate by clearly labeling the latency metric and linking to SRE guardrails.

Follow-ups (if needed)
- Add “deploy / flag flip annotations” integration if not already present in our dashboard framework.
- Add per-model default views for known-problem models/pools once we have canary data.

title: Route normalization library (templating IDs) shared by Gateway + Telemetry
preview:
Problem
- Usage Insights requires cost/usage breakdown by API route. Today we emit inconsistent route strings depending on where the event is produced (gateway vs downstream services) and whether a request hit OpenAI-compat vs Redwood-native endpoints.
- Unnormalized paths with IDs (e.g. /v1/workspaces/ws_abc123/projects/prj_9f.../keys/key_...) explode cardinality and make aggregations expensive/unreliable.

Goal
- Ship a single, shared route normalization implementation used by both:
  1) API Gateway request metering/telemetry emitters
  2) Telemetry ingestion/aggregation jobs (as a fallback / validation layer)
- Normalize routes by templating path params (IDs) and standardizing prefixes so aggregation keys are stable.
- Provide tests + a migration/rollout plan to avoid breaking billing reconciliation and existing dashboards.

Non-goals (Phase 1)
- Not a general-purpose URL parser for arbitrary customer-defined routes.
- Not auto-discovering templates from traffic (we will use a controlled template set + a conservative heuristic fallback).
- Not changing billing semantics; only adding/standardizing dimensions used for analytics.

Proposed output fields (event tags)
- route_raw: exact request path from gateway (no query string); retained temporarily for debugging.
- route_template: normalized template used for analytics (e.g. /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/api-keys/{key_id}).
- route_group: canonical grouping (mostly equals route_template; may collapse minor variants like trailing slash).
- route_source: gateway | ingestion (for auditing/coverage metrics).

Normalization rules (Phase 1)
1) Strip query string and fragment.
   - /v1/chat/completions?stream=true -> /v1/chat/completions
2) Remove trailing slash (except root).
   - /v1/embeddings/ -> /v1/embeddings
3) Prefer exact match against a maintained template allowlist for Redwood + OpenAI-compat.
   Examples (expected route_template):
   - /v1/chat/completions -> /v1/chat/completions
   - /v1/embeddings -> /v1/embeddings
   - /v1/rerank -> /v1/rerank
   - /openai/v1/chat/completions -> /openai/v1/chat/completions
   - /openai/v1/embeddings -> /openai/v1/embeddings
4) For templated routes with IDs, replace segments in known positions.
   - /v1/workspaces/ws_2kQ1/projects/prj_A91/deployments/dpl_77/logs -> /v1/workspaces/{workspace_id}/projects/{project_id}/deployments/{deployment_id}/logs
   - /v1/keys/key_abc123 -> /v1/keys/{key_id}
5) Conservative heuristic fallback for unknown routes:
   - If a segment matches common ID patterns (uuid v4, base58-ish tokens >= 16 chars, ws_/prj_/dpl_ prefixes), replace with {id}.
   - If heuristic would replace >2 segments OR the result would be too generic, return /unknown (and keep route_raw for debugging).

Rationale
- Using an allowlist first prevents over-templating and avoids grouping distinct endpoints under the same template.
- Heuristic fallback covers missed templates while limiting cardinality blowups.
- Keeping route_raw temporarily unblocks debugging and customer support cases (e.g. “missing OpenAI route breakdown”) without trusting it for aggregation keys.

Implementation plan
- Create shared package: go module "github.com/redwoodinference/platform/route-normalization" (exact path TBD by repo conventions).
- Expose stable API:
  - Normalize(path string) -> {route_template, route_group, normalization_reason}
  - NormalizeFromURL(url string) -> same, with query stripping
  - Metrics helpers: normalization coverage counters (matched_allowlist, matched_template, heuristic, unknown)
- Add template registry (versioned): templates.go + templates_test.go
  - Explicit test fixtures for every supported route.
- Integrate in Gateway emitter:
  - Emit route_template + route_group on every metering event.
  - Emit route_raw behind a temporary feature flag (default on in beta environments; off in GA unless requested).
- Integrate in Telemetry ingestion:
  - If route_template missing (older agents/regions), compute it at ingestion and set route_source=ingestion.
  - If both present, compute and compare; log mismatch rate with sampling.

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Allowlist exact matches (Redwood + OpenAI-compat)
  - ID templating for all “ID-bearing” endpoints
  - Query stripping / trailing slash
  - Heuristic behavior (replace 1–2 segments only)
  - Negative tests: do NOT template common nouns (e.g. /v1/models/llama-3.1-70b stays literal if in allowlist)
- Integration tests:
  - Gateway event emission contains stable route_template for representative traffic
  - Ingestion fallback produces identical templates for the same request
- Regression tests:
  - Fixtures sourced from recent gateway logs to ensure no surprises with real traffic patterns

Rollout / migration plan
1) Phase A (1–2 days): library lands + gateway emits route_template + route_group in parallel; ingestion fallback enabled.
2) Phase B (1 week): monitor normalization coverage + mismatch rate dashboards.
   - Target: >= 99% matched_allowlist/template/heuristic (non-unknown) for /v1/* and /openai/v1/*.
   - Mismatch target: < 0.5% sampled mismatches (excluding known legacy paths).
3) Phase C: switch Usage Insights aggregation (ENG-3812) to key on route_group.
4) Phase D: deprecate raw route usage in analytics; keep route_raw only for debug sampling, gated by env + retention.

Risks / open questions
- OpenAI-compat routing: ensure we treat /openai/v1/* as first-class templates; do not collapse into /v1/*.
- Streaming: stream=true is a query param; we strip it so route_template is stable. Streaming overhead will be attributed via separate dimension(s) (not via route).
- Cardinality: heuristic fallback must remain conservative. If unknown traffic increases, we add templates explicitly rather than broadening heuristics.
- Backfill: we will not backfill historical data in Phase 1; insights will show normalized routes from rollout forward. (Backfill plan tracked separately.)

Acceptance criteria
- Shared library merged with full test suite.
- Gateway emits route_template + route_group for 100% of metering events in at least one canary region.
- Ingestion fallback enabled and mismatch rate is within target.
- Template registry includes all current public API endpoints used for Usage Insights (chat, completions, embeddings, rerank + auth/admin endpoints that generate meaningful spend).

Links
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-route-normalization-and-attribution.json
- Slack thread (edge cases): sources/slack/eng-platform/1737468804.json
- Related: ENG-3822 (route tag instrumentation), ENG-3812 (aggregation dataset keyed by route/model)

title: Status page metrics backfill job (historical component health for incident summaries + trends)
preview:
## Context
We’re improving status page transparency by moving to a more granular component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability) and publishing higher-quality post-incident summaries quickly.

A recurring gap: we can draft incident summaries from the incident timeline, but we don’t have reliable historical component health rollups (error rate + latency) for the impacted components over the incident window and for pre/post comparisons. This is slowing Comms Leads down and causing “hand-wavy” impact statements.

This ticket adds a backfill job that populates historical component health metrics so:
- post-incident summaries can include concrete impact metrics (e.g., peak error rate, p95 latency, duration)
- we can trend component health over time and audit status accuracy

## Goals
1) Backfill component-level health rollups for a defined history window (initial proposal: 90 days; stretch: 180 days) for all public status components and regions.
2) Produce a dataset that is stable and queryable by:
   - incident summary generator (ENG-4823)
   - console linking / incident details view (Console integration spec)
   - weekly status accuracy audit job (pr-19278)
3) Ensure runs are idempotent and safe to rerun (late-arriving metrics, label fixes, re-computes).

## Non-goals
- Recomputing internal SLO burn-rate history (that lives in the SLO toolkit). This is a rollup for customer-facing reporting, not SLO accounting.
- Real-time status signal computation (covered by observability-pack anti-flap logic + alerts).

## Proposed outputs (rollups)
Rollups keyed by: component_id, region, capability (where applicable), time_bucket.

Time buckets:
- 1m or 5m rollups for incident windows (needed for peak calculations)
- 1h rollups for general querying
- 1d rollups for long-term trends (cheaper storage)

Metrics (minimum viable):
- request_count
- error_count + error_rate
- latency_p50 / latency_p95 (or p90+p99 depending on what’s stable in Mimir)
- streaming_disconnect_rate (streaming capability only)
- “data_quality_flags” (string field) when we detect missing labels / sparse data

## Data sources
Primary: Mimir/Prometheus metrics that already carry component tags (or can be mapped):
- API gateway metrics w/ component tags (see PR: redwood/pr-19502)
- Region-aware streaming error/disconnect metrics (see PR: redwood/pr-19518)
- Component registry source-of-truth (ADR-014; schema in observability-pack/pr-19371)

Secondary (fallback): service-level metrics mapped via the component mapping layer (ENG-4830) when direct component tags are absent.

## Where the data lives
Write to BigQuery dataset used by Observability + incident tooling (current preference from Allison/Aisha):
- table: status_component_health_rollups
- partition: date(time_bucket)
- clustering: component_id, region, capability

If BigQuery is blocked for cost/latency, alternate is ClickHouse (but that complicates integrations). Assume BigQuery for v1.

## Backfill approach
- Implement as a scheduled batch job (Airflow/Prefect—use whatever we’re already running for observability jobs; align with Caleb on platform conventions).
- Backfill window start/end controlled via config so we can do staged runs (e.g., 7d test, then 30d, then 90d).
- Query PromQL range queries in bounded chunks (e.g., 6h chunks) to avoid hammering Mimir and to respect query limits.
- Normalize labels to the public taxonomy:
  - For metrics missing component_id, use mapping rules from ENG-4830 (service->component) and tag the resulting rows with data_quality_flags=missing_component_label.
  - For known historical label changes (e.g., embeddings misclassification fixed in redwood/pr-19563), run a corrective recompute for affected time ranges.
- Idempotency:
  - Use MERGE semantics in BigQuery by (component_id, region, capability, time_bucket)
  - Keep a run_log table with job_run_id, window, success/failure, row counts, and a hash of the registry version used

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Backfill completes successfully for at least 30 days in staging, and 90 days in prod without exceeding Mimir query budget.
- [ ] For a known incident (e.g., 2025-11-07 US-East streaming degradation), we can query rollups and get:
      - peak error rate / disconnect rate
      - p95 latency delta vs baseline
      - duration above threshold
- [ ] ENG-4823 can consume these rollups without bespoke per-incident PromQL.
- [ ] Data quality flags appear for cases with missing labels, and the count of flagged rows is tracked in a dashboard.

## Monitoring
- Job success/failure + runtime metrics
- Mimir query volume + rate limiting errors
- Backfill coverage dashboard: % of components w/ rollups per day, broken down by region/capability

## Risks / open questions
- Cost: BigQuery storage and scan costs if we store 1m rollups for 90d for all components/regions. Mitigation: store 5m rollups for long window; keep 1m only for last 14d.
- Label completeness: older metrics may not have component_id tags. Mitigation: mapping fallback + clear data_quality_flags.
- Historical correctness: instrumentation bugs (e.g., embeddings vs chat classification) may require targeted recompute windows.

## Implementation tasks (proposed)
1) Finalize schema + dataset location (sync w/ Allison Grant + Aisha Bello)
2) Implement backfill runner w/ config-driven windows + chunking
3) Implement label normalization + mapping fallback integration (ENG-4830)
4) BigQuery write path w/ MERGE + run logs
5) Dashboards for coverage + data quality
6) Dry run in staging, then prod ramp (7d -> 30d -> 90d)

## Links
- Project brief: Status page transparency upgrades (Confluence)
- Component registry schema: observability-pack/pr-19371
- Component tags on gateway metrics: redwood/pr-19502
- Region-aware streaming metrics: redwood/pr-19518
- Incident timeline export hook: redwood/pr-19540
- Misclassified embeddings hotfix: redwood/pr-19563

- [ ] For a known incident (e.g., 2025-11-07 US-East streaming degradation), we can query rollups and get:
      - peak error rate / disconnect rate
      - p95 latency delta vs baseline
      - duration above threshold
- [ ] ENG-4823 can consume these rollups without bespoke per-incident PromQL.
- [ ] Data quality flags appear for cases with missing labels, and the count of flagged rows is tracked in a dashboard.

## Monitoring
- Job success/failure + runtime metrics
- Mimir query volume + rate limiting errors
- Backfill coverage dashboard: % of components w/ rollups per day, broken down by region/capability

## Risks / open questions
- Cost: BigQuery storage and scan costs if we store 1m rollups for 90d for all components/regions. Mitigation: store 5m rollups for long window; keep 1m only for last 14d.
- Label completeness: older metrics may not have component_id tags. Mitigation: mapping fallback + clear data_quality_flags.
- Historical correctness: instrumentation bugs (e.g., embeddings vs chat classification) may require targeted recompute windows.

## Implementation tasks (proposed)
1) Finalize schema + dataset location (sync w/ Allison Grant + Aisha Bello)
2) Implement backfill runner w/ config-driven windows + chunking
3) Implement label normalization + mapping fallback integration (ENG-4830)
4) BigQuery write path w/ MERGE + run logs
5) Dashboards for coverage + data quality
6) Dry run in staging, then prod ramp (7d -> 30d -> 90d)

## Links
- Project brief: Status page transparency upgrades (Confluence)
- Component registry schema: observability-pack/pr-19371
- Component tags on gateway metrics: redwood/pr-19502
- Region-aware streaming metrics: redwood/pr-19518
- Incident timeline export hook: redwood/pr-19540
- Misclassified embeddings hotfix: redwood/pr-19563

title: Add runtime golden-signal recording rules (rate, errors, latency, saturation, queues) for Private minimum pack
preview:
Goal
Ship a bounded-cardinality set of Prometheus recording rules that compute the runtime “golden signals” for Redwood Private installs. These rules are the stable building blocks used by the minimum pack dashboards + alerts, so they must be:
- cheap to evaluate
- consistent naming
- backward-compatible where possible
- safe for customer Prometheus (bounded label cardinality; no per-request / unbounded labels)

Scope
Recording rules for:
1) Request rate (RPS / req/s) and token throughput (tok/s)
2) Error rate (5xx + runtime-level errors)
3) Latency (p50/p95/p99 via histogram quantiles) for end-to-end and model runtime segments where available
4) Saturation (GPU utilization, memory, CPU throttling, request concurrency / in-flight, KV cache pressure if exposed)
5) Queueing / backpressure (queue depth, queue wait time, dropped/limited requests)

Non-goals
- Defining final alert thresholds (tracked under ENG-19147/ENG-19148)
- High-cardinality per-model/per-route/per-tenant breakdowns in v1 (we’ll support a small curated set of dimensions; the rest stays aggregated)

Design notes / conventions
- Naming: `redwood:runtime:<signal>_<unit>:rate{window="5m"}` style for rates, and `redwood:runtime:<signal>_<unit>:p95{window="5m"}` for quantiles.
- Windows: default 5m for rates/quantiles (dashboards can derive 1m/15m as needed, but alerts should use stable windows).
- Aggregation: default aggregates across pod/instance; keep only bounded labels.
- Label constraints (per Private standards + SRE feedback):
  - Allowed: `cluster`, `namespace`, `service`, `deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private), `component` (gateway/router/runtime), `model_family` (bounded set), `route_group` (bounded set, optional)
  - Not allowed in recording rules: raw `route`, raw `model` (full name), `tenant_id`, `request_id`, `user_id`, arbitrary `endpoint`
- Histogram handling: prefer `*_bucket` histograms for request latency and queue wait latency. Where older metrics only have summaries, keep a temporary compatibility rule with deprecation note.

Planned rule groups (v1)
A) Runtime request + tokens
- `redwood:runtime:requests_per_second:rate5m`
  - sum(rate(redwood_runtime_requests_total[5m]))
- `redwood:runtime:request_errors_per_second:rate5m`
  - sum(rate(redwood_runtime_request_errors_total[5m]))
- `redwood:runtime:tokens_per_second:rate5m`
  - sum(rate(redwood_runtime_tokens_total[5m]))

B) Runtime latency
- `redwood:runtime:request_latency_seconds:p50_5m`
- `redwood:runtime:request_latency_seconds:p95_5m`
- `redwood:runtime:request_latency_seconds:p99_5m`
  - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le) (rate(redwood_runtime_request_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])))

C) Queueing / backpressure
- `redwood:runtime:queue_depth:avg`
  - avg(redwood_runtime_queue_depth)
- `redwood:runtime:queue_wait_latency_seconds:p95_5m`
  - histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le) (rate(redwood_runtime_queue_wait_latency_seconds_bucket[5m])))
- `redwood:runtime:rate_limited_requests_per_second:rate5m`
  - sum(rate(redwood_gateway_rate_limited_requests_total[5m]))

D) Saturation / resource pressure
- `redwood:runtime:gpu_utilization_ratio:avg`
  - avg(redwood_runtime_gpu_utilization_ratio)
- `redwood:runtime:gpu_memory_used_ratio:avg`
  - avg(redwood_runtime_gpu_memory_used_ratio)
- `redwood:runtime:cpu_throttling_ratio:rate5m`
  - sum(rate(container_cpu_cfs_throttled_periods_total{container!="",pod=~"redwood-runtime-.*"}[5m])) / sum(rate(container_cpu_cfs_periods_total{container!="",pod=~"redwood-runtime-.*"}[5m]))
  - Note: requires kube-state/cAdvisor metrics availability; rule should be gated/optional if customers disable these.

E) Availability / restarts (used by dashboard panels; alert rules live elsewhere)
- `redwood:runtime:pod_restarts_per_second:rate15m`
  - sum(rate(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{pod=~"redwood-runtime-.*"}[15m]))

Implementation details
- Location: `observability-pack/prometheus/rules/private/runtime_golden_signals.rules.yaml`
- Rule group name: `redwood.private.runtime.golden_signals`
- Interval: 30s evaluation (validated cost in staging; ok under typical private footprints)
- Gating:
  - Rules that depend on Kubernetes/cAdvisor metrics are placed into a separate group `redwood.private.runtime.k8s_deps` and can be disabled in Helm values (some private customers do not run full kube-state-metrics).
- Backward compatibility:
  - Temporary compatibility rules added for older metric names until `private-2025.10` (documented in comments + release notes).

Acceptance criteria
- Recording rules exist in observability-pack and pass `promtool check rules` in CI.
- Rule output series cardinality bounded; no unbounded labels included.
- Dashboards/alerts can depend on the recorded series without requiring pod-level queries.
- Rule evaluation cost acceptable (validated by SRE in staging private cluster; no >10% Prometheus CPU regression from baseline).
- Documented series names and intended usage in the minimum pack doc set (link added to PR description).

Validation plan
- Local: promtool rule check + unit tests for histogram_quantile (use synthetic buckets).
- Staging: deploy minimum pack in private reference cluster, generate load, verify:
  - request/tokens rate tracks load generator within tolerance
  - latency quantiles move appropriately under injected slowness
  - queue panels populate when forcing backpressure (reduced runtime replicas)
  - no explosion in series count when changing route/model (labels should remain stable)

Progress / updates
- 2025-06-11: Drafted rule naming + label policy; aligned with Allison Grant on standards and with Thomas Baird on label constraints (no raw `route`/`model`).
- 2025-06-20: Implemented initial rule file with request/tokens/latency/queue depth; flagged k8s-dependent rules behind values toggle.
- 2025-07-02: Adjusted histogram_quantile aggregation to `sum by (le)` only; removed `pod` label from all recorded series (dashboards can still drill down using raw metrics when enabled).
- 2025-07-10: Added compatibility mapping for older runtime metric names (pre-standardization) and documented deprecation timeline.
- 2025-08-12: Merged + validated in staging; series count stable across 24h soak; handed off series names to dashboard/alert owners.

Links
- Standards: Confluence: Metrics & alerting standards for Private (label cardinality + severity taxonomy)
- Related PR: `sources/github/observability-pack/pr-18356-add-private-prometheus-alert-rules-latency-and-availability.json` (contains the initial recording rules + rationale)
- Dependent work:
  - ENG-19145 (runtime dashboard uses these recording rules)
  - ENG-19147/ENG-19148 (alerts reference these recorded series)

Open questions / follow-ups
- Add optional per-`model_family` breakdown once model family mapping is available everywhere (blocked on final instrumentation coverage).
- Decide whether to ship a “top-K routes” debug dashboard panel using raw metrics behind an opt-in flag (to avoid cardinality blowups by default).

title: Support log export to Splunk HEC for Private installs (documented + tested)
preview:
## Context
Private customers frequently mandate forwarding platform logs to Splunk (Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud) via HTTP Event Collector (HEC). Today we have ad-hoc collector configs in PS engagements, which creates support risk and inconsistent outcomes.

This ticket adds a **supported, documented, and validated** Splunk HEC log export path for Redwood Private (VPC + on-prem, including air-gapped constraints).

Scope aligns with the Private observability minimum pack:
- Control plane + data plane operational logs (NOT audit logs; audit logs are handled separately).
- Default collector is the minimum-pack log collector (Vector-based) shipped via `redwood-helm-charts`.

## Goal
Provide a turnkey Splunk HEC sink configuration that:
1) is configurable via Helm values (no editing manifests by hand),
2) uses Kubernetes Secret(s) for tokens,
3) is safe by default (redaction guidance, no request bodies),
4) has a smoke/e2e test (CI + documented manual test),
5) is documented in Private Day-2 runbooks + known-issues.

## Non-goals (v1)
- Building a Splunk TA/app or maintaining customer dashboards inside Splunk.
- Full audit log export (handled via Security/audit logging docs).
- Advanced Splunk routing logic per-namespace/index beyond a small set of supported knobs.

## Proposed implementation
### Collector
Use the minimum-pack collector (Vector) with a Splunk HEC sink.
- Input: Kubernetes container logs (CRI) + Redwood structured JSON logs where present.
- Transform:
  - Parse JSON if `message` is JSON.
  - Normalize fields (timestamp, level, service, pod, namespace, request_id if present).
  - Apply redaction transform rules (align with ENG-19157).

### Supported configuration knobs (Helm values)
Expose a `logging.export.splunkHec` block:
- `enabled` (default false)
- `endpoint` (e.g. `https://splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector/event`)
- `tokenSecretRef` (name + key) for HEC token
- `index` (optional)
- `sourcetype` (default `redwood:private`)
- `source` (default `redwood-private`)
- `tls.verify` (default true)
- `tls.caBundleSecretRef` (optional; common for on-prem Splunk with private CA)
- `batch.maxBytes`, `batch.timeoutSecs`
- `retry.maxBackoffSecs`, `request.timeoutSecs`

### Secret management
- HEC token must be provided by customer in a Secret.
- Docs must include both:
  1) creating Secret via `kubectl create secret generic ...` and
  2) referencing an existing external-secrets/ESO-managed Secret.

### Network policy considerations
Document required egress from collector namespace to Splunk HEC endpoint. Provide a short allowlist example for clusters with default-deny egress.

## Helm values example (target docs snippet)
```yaml
redwood:
  logging:
    collector:
      enabled: true

    export:
      splunkHec:
        enabled: true
        endpoint: "https://splunk.example.com:8088/services/collector/event"
        tokenSecretRef:
          name: "splunk-hec"
          key: "token"
        index: "redwood_private"
        sourcetype: "redwood:private"
        source: "redwood-private"
        tls:
          verify: true
          caBundleSecretRef:
            name: "splunk-ca"
            key: "ca.crt"
        batch:
          maxBytes: 1048576
          timeoutSecs: 2
        request:
          timeoutSecs: 10
        retry:
          maxBackoffSecs: 60
```

Secret creation example:
```bash
kubectl -n redwood-observability create secret generic splunk-hec \
  --from-literal=token='${SPLUNK_HEC_TOKEN}'
```

## Validation / testing plan
### Automated (CI)
Add to the private observability smoke-test harness:
- Deploy collector with Splunk HEC export enabled.
- Spin up a minimal HEC-compatible endpoint in-cluster (test fixture) that:
  - returns 200,
  - validates token header,
  - counts received events.
- Assert that >N events arrive within 2 minutes after generating known logs.

### Manual validation (runbook)
Provide steps for customers/Support:
1) Enable `logging.export.splunkHec.enabled=true`.
2) Confirm collector is healthy (pods ready, no restart loop).
3) Confirm Vector sink metrics show non-zero sent events and zero/low errors.
4) In Splunk, search for `sourcetype=redwood:private source=redwood-private`.

## Operational notes / pitfalls to document
- HEC 401 due to token mismatch / wrong endpoint path (`/services/collector/event` vs `/services/collector`).
- TLS issues with private CA (solution: provide `caBundleSecretRef`).
- Backpressure: if Splunk is slow/unreachable, collector will buffer up to configured limits; document defaults and how to tune without destabilizing the cluster.
- PII/secrets: no request/response bodies; ensure redaction transform is on by default and describe how to verify.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Helm chart supports Splunk HEC export via values only.
- [ ] Token sourced from Secret; no tokens in ConfigMaps or logs.
- [ ] Docs added: install/config, troubleshooting, and FAQ/known-issues entry.
- [ ] Smoke test covers successful send + basic auth failure case.
- [ ] Security review sign-off for log fields + redaction defaults (ENG-19157 dependency).

## Links / references
- Private minimum pack initiative: PM-842
- Collector defaults: ENG-19149
- Helm wiring: ENG-19152
- Security review: ENG-19157

Owner notes:
- Primary eng: Diego Santos
- SME for customer constraints: Colin O'Donnell
- SRE validation: Rafael Mendes
- Security reviewer: Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei
