title: Support log export to Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for Private observability minimum pack
preview:
## Summary
Add a supported, documented, and tested configuration for exporting Redwood Private operational logs (control plane + data plane) to Elasticsearch or OpenSearch.

This is part of the Private Observability Minimum Pack (v1). The goal is to provide a turnkey baseline that works in typical customer environments while keeping defaults safe (no PII/secrets) and bounded (no unbounded cardinality and no unbounded buffering).

## Scope
In-scope:
- Vector-based log forwarder sink configuration for:
  - Elasticsearch 8.x (with TLS, basic auth and/or API key)
  - OpenSearch 2.x (compat mode settings)
- Helm values and docs for enabling the sink in Private installs
- Tested examples for common customer permutations:
  - Elastic Cloud-style endpoint with TLS and API key
  - Self-managed Elasticsearch with basic auth and custom CA bundle
  - Self-managed OpenSearch with basic auth
- Day-2 troubleshooting guidance for:
  - auth failures (401/403)
  - TLS/CA issues
  - index mapping errors
  - backpressure / buffer growth

Out of scope (v1):
- Shipping an Elasticsearch/OpenSearch cluster
- Multi-cluster federation or cross-cluster search
- Customer-specific index lifecycle policies beyond a recommended baseline

## Requirements / Acceptance Criteria
- Docs:
  - A single documented path via `redwood-helm-charts` values to enable Elasticsearch/OpenSearch export
  - Explicit statement that export is disabled by default
  - Explicit list of required customer-provided inputs (endpoint, auth, TLS settings, index/prefix)
  - Redaction and retention notes aligned with Security guidance
- Implementation:
  - Vector sink supports both Elasticsearch and OpenSearch endpoints
  - Supports TLS verification with optional custom CA bundle
  - Supports basic auth and API key auth
  - Index naming convention is deterministic and documented
  - Includes a recommended template/mapping approach (or safe defaults) to prevent field explosion
- Testing:
  - CI / automated test that deploys a minimal log forwarder config and successfully indexes events into a local OpenSearch container
  - Manual verification checklist for Elasticsearch 8.x (cannot rely on internet access; use a local container for functional test)
- Operational safety:
  - Batching + retry configuration prevents hot loops
  - Bounded buffering with documented drop/pressure behavior
  - Does not include request payloads, raw prompts, or secrets; confirms structured log fields only

## Proposed Approach
### 1) Sink implementation (Vector)
Add a `sinks.elasticsearch` (Vector) configuration block that is parameterized by Helm values.

Key behavior:
- Index naming:
  - Default: `redwood-private-logs-%Y.%m.%d`
  - Allow override via `observability.logs.export.elasticsearch.indexPrefix`
- Dataset separation (optional but recommended):
  - Control plane vs data plane separation using `index` based on `kubernetes.namespace` or `service` field
- TLS:
  - `tls.verify_certificate` default true
  - support `tls.ca_file` via mounted secret/configmap
- Auth:
  - Basic auth: username/password from Kubernetes Secret
  - API key: from Kubernetes Secret
- OpenSearch compatibility:
  - Document that OpenSearch endpoints are supported; avoid features known to diverge (e.g., data streams) in v1

### 2) Helm wiring
Add new values under `observability.logs.export.elasticsearch`:
- `enabled` (default false)
- `endpoint` (e.g., https://opensearch.logging.svc:9200)
- `auth.type` (basic | apiKey)
- `auth.secretRef` (name + keys; documented)
- `tls.caSecretRef` (optional)
- `indexPrefix` (optional)
- `bulk.batch.maxBytes`, `bulk.batch.timeoutSecs` (documented defaults)
- `request.concurrency`, `request.timeoutSecs`, `retry.maxDurationSecs` (documented defaults)
- `buffer.maxSizeMb` (bounded)

### 3) Documentation
Publish examples and copy/paste snippets for:
- Elasticsearch with API key
- OpenSearch with basic auth
- Custom CA bundle
- Minimal RBAC needed (none beyond existing collector permissions)

### 4) Validation
- Add a lightweight integration test:
  - Start OpenSearch (docker) in CI job
  - Deploy log forwarder config
  - Send a known log line via a test pod
  - Assert it appears in OpenSearch index

## Notes / Decisions
- Export disabled by default to avoid surprising data egress in private environments.
- We will not log request/response bodies. Structured fields are limited to operational metadata (request_id, route, model where permitted by cardinality policy, error_code, latency_ms, pod/node identifiers). Any tenant identifier must be hashed/anonymized if present.
- We will not introduce dynamic index names based on tenant/model to avoid index explosion.

## Risks
- Mapping conflicts / field explosion if customers inject arbitrary JSON logs into the same index.
  - Mitigation: recommend a dedicated index prefix and keep Redwood logs structured and stable.
- Backpressure when Elasticsearch/OpenSearch is degraded.
  - Mitigation: bounded buffer + documented drop policy; emit self-metrics for dropped events.
- TLS/auth misconfiguration is common.
  - Mitigation: include troubleshooting section and preflight guidance in runbook.

## Deliverables
- Helm chart values + templates for Elasticsearch/OpenSearch export
- Vector sink config with tested defaults
- Docs + runbook section
- CI validation for OpenSearch

title: Standardize Private metric names/labels, cap cardinality, and define migrations
preview:
Private installs need a bounded, consistent metric surface that is safe for customer-managed Prometheus (especially on-prem) and stable across upgrades.

This ticket standardizes:
- Metric naming (prefixes, units, suffixes, histogram conventions)
- Label keys and allowed values (to avoid unbounded cardinality)
- Cross-service consistency (same label keys mean the same thing)
- Backwards-compatibility plan for any renamed/removed metrics (including recording rules and dashboards)

Primary problems observed in early Private staging and customer escalations:
- High-cardinality labels (route/model/tenant/request_id) causing TSDB churn and slow Grafana queries
- Inconsistent label sets across gateway/router/runtime making dashboards brittle
- Metric renames between builds without migration notes, leading to blank panels after upgrades

Scope (v1): control plane services, API gateway, routing/policy engine, serving runtime pods, and installer/upgrade controller metrics exported to Prometheus.

Non-goals: defining customer-specific dimensions (e.g., per-tenant metrics) as first-class labels in the minimum pack; those should be addressed via logs/traces or optional, explicitly gated metrics.

title: Benchmark results service + cache layer for marketing site (versioned JSON/CSV, low-latency, resilient)
preview:
Context
The public benchmark landing page + interactive table will pull benchmark result artifacts (versioned JSON/CSV) generated by benchmark-lab. During launch we expect large, spiky, mostly-read traffic (including bots) and we need the results payloads to be served reliably with low latency, without leaking any internal identifiers or creating a thundering-herd effect on storage.

Problem
Directly fetching artifacts from object storage (or via an un-cached internal endpoint) risks:
- cache-miss storms on launch day and on asset refresh
- uneven latency by region
- accidental exposure of non-public data if an artifact is misconfigured
- operational fragility (no clear rollback/version pinning)

Goal
Ship a lightweight “benchmark results service” with an aggressive caching strategy and explicit versioning controls, so marketing site can reliably load results and render the table/filters without timeouts.

Non-goals
- building a full analytics/BI layer (this is a read-optimized distribution layer)
- creating new benchmark computations (artifact export owned by ENG-18477)
- changing methodology or results selection logic (owned by Applied ML + PMM)

Requirements (must have)
1) Read-only API that serves benchmark artifacts for the marketing site:
   - GET /public/benchmarks/index.json (list of available versions, latest pointer, schema_version)
   - GET /public/benchmarks/{version}/results.json
   - GET /public/benchmarks/{version}/results.csv (optional if we choose to gate)
   - GET /public/benchmarks/{version}/changelog.txt (human-readable)
2) Strong caching:
   - CDN cacheable responses with long TTL for versioned assets (e.g., 24h+) and immutable cache headers
   - short TTL (e.g., 60–300s) for index.json “latest” pointer
   - origin-side cache to protect storage and smooth spikes
3) Version pinning + rollback:
   - marketing site can pin to a specific {version} in config
   - “latest” pointer can be advanced safely after validation + legal approval
   - quick rollback by reverting “latest” pointer (no redeploy required)
4) Safe-public guarantee:
   - only serve artifacts from a known public bucket/prefix
   - enforce schema_version + required fields checks before serving “latest”
   - no auth required for public endpoints, but strict allowlist of paths
5) Observability:
   - dashboards/alerts for p95 latency, 5xx, cache hit ratio, origin fetch rate
   - structured logs with request id + path + cache status

Security/compliance requirements
- Confirm redaction pipeline is applied upstream (ENG-18477) and add “deny by default” on any artifact that is not explicitly marked publishable.
- Security review for:
  - public endpoints exposure
  - request logging (avoid query params containing user identifiers)
  - any headers that might leak internal routing details

Proposed architecture
- Small stateless service (“bench-results”) deployed in the same cluster as marketing site backend (or edge-adjacent), fronted by CDN.
- Origin storage: public artifact bucket/prefix (write side handled by export pipeline).
- Two-layer caching:
  1) CDN cache: immutable for versioned paths; short TTL for index.json
  2) In-service cache: LRU in-memory + optional shared cache (Redis) for index.json and hottest results.json
- Validation gate for “latest”:
  - on startup and every N minutes, fetch index.json and validate that latest points to a version that passes:
    - schema_version supported
    - required files exist
    - file sizes within bounds

Implementation plan
Phase 1 (launch blocker)
- Implement endpoints + CDN headers + origin fetch with timeouts and retries.
- Add in-memory cache with singleflight/dedup to prevent concurrent origin fetch for the same key.
- Add health checks and basic metrics (request count, latency, 4xx/5xx, cache hit/miss).
- Add allowlist validation: only serve {version} that matches regex and exists in index.

Phase 2 (hardening)
- Add Redis (or shared cache) if needed based on load test.
- Add size guardrails (fail closed if results.json exceeds X MB).
- Add circuit breaker for origin errors (serve last-known-good for index.json).

Acceptance criteria
- Marketing site interactive table loads results.json in <300ms p95 (edge cache warm) and <800ms p95 (cache cold) from NA/EU.
- No more than 1 origin fetch per asset per cache TTL under steady state (dedup working).
- “latest” pointer updates propagate globally within 5 minutes.
- A rollback to previous version requires only changing index.json, not a code deploy.
- Security sign-off from Naomi Feldman (or delegate) that public endpoints + logs are safe.

Testing
- Load test with simulated launch traffic (spiky burst; 10–50k req/min) verifying cache hit ratio and origin protection.
- Chaos test: origin returns 500/timeout; service continues serving last-known-good “latest” for index for up to 15 minutes.
- Contract tests against exported artifact schema (schema_version, required fields).

Links / related work
- Core product PR adding internal benchmark results endpoint (reference/precedent): sources/github/redwood/pr-27841-add-internal-benchmark-results-api-endpoint.json
- Artifact export pipeline: ENG-18477 (versioned JSON/CSV + redaction)
- Launch-week monitoring/guardrails: ENG-18610
- Interactive page requirements + analytics: PM-826

Rollout notes
- Default marketing site config pins to a fixed version for launch; switch to “latest” only after we have confidence in index validation.
- Warm cache job (optional): periodically fetch latest results.json from each region to reduce cold-start.

Open questions
- Do we expose CSV publicly by default or keep behind an explicit “download CSV” click that still uses the same endpoint? (PMM + Legal preference)
- Should we sign assets (e.g., signed URLs) even if public? Current view: unnecessary if bucket is public-read and paths are allowlisted.

Owner
- Felix (implementation), Logan (telemetry/review), Sean (SRE review), Naomi (security review).

title: Observability dashboards + alerts for PLG signal router (lag, HubSpot failures, task routing volume)
preview:
We have the signal aggregation + HubSpot routing service in flight for the PLG-to-sales pilot. Before we expand cohort/regions we need production-grade observability: a single dashboard AEs/ops can reference for volume + health, plus actionable alerts for the oncall rotation.

This ticket covers:
- Dashboard(s) for pipeline health and throughput
- Alerting for event lag, HubSpot API errors/429s, and task creation anomalies
- Oncall handoff notes/runbook snippets (what to check first, how to backfill/retry)

Why now: We’ve already seen HubSpot 429s during early testing and we need to avoid silent drops/missed follow-up tasks during the NA pilot.

title: PLG signal quality: noise reduction + suppression rules (MVP)
preview:
Context
We’re seeing early pilot feedback that routed PLG signals can be noisy (false positives) and/or “spammy” (re-notifying on the same underlying behavior). This ticket defines and implements the first set of suppression + quality guardrails in the scoring/routing pipeline.

Primary goals
1) Reduce false positives and duplicate notifications without losing legitimate “sales-worthy” accounts.
2) Provide deterministic and auditable suppression behavior (why a signal did/didn’t route).
3) Ensure internal/test orgs never route into HubSpot.

Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Building a full review queue UI (we’ll use HubSpot views + internal logs for now).
- Fully automated outbound sequencing.

Problem statement
During pilot week 1, AEs flagged at least one high-friction false positive (Quartz Commerce) and multiple “repeat pings” driven by daily aggregation windows re-surfacing the same issue. We need suppression windows + minimum confidence thresholds + allow/deny lists to make the system usable.

Definitions (working)
- “Signal” = scored, deduped record representing an account-level sales-worthy event derived from product telemetry and/or intent.
- “Routing” = creating/updating HubSpot company properties + timeline note + follow-up task.
- “Suppression” = preventing routing for a period or condition, while still recording the signal internally for analysis.

Proposed guardrails (MVP)
A) Internal/test org filtering (hard deny)
- Denylist sources (union):
  1. org.is_internal flag (from auth/billing)
  2. email domain matches internal domains (redwoodinference.com, redwood.ai) for the org owner/admin
  3. known test workspace prefixes: “test-”, “dev-”, “sandbox-”, “demo-”
  4. explicit denylist table maintained by Growth Ops (for one-offs)
- If denylisted: signal is written to analytics with status=suppressed and suppression_reason=internal_org (do not route to HubSpot).

B) Allowlist (override)
- Explicit allowlist table (Growth Ops + Sales Ops) to override denylist for:
  - integration testing against a controlled HubSpot sandbox company
  - internal dogfooding accounts where we intentionally want to test routing
- Allowlist should require: org_id + environment (prod/sandbox) + expiry_date.

C) Suppression windows (dedupe + re-notification control)
- Per org_id + signal_type suppression TTL:
  - Default: 14 days
  - Reliability signals (429_rate_high, error_rate_high): 7 days (more time-sensitive)
  - Compliance intent (security_docs_views, vpc_private_docs_interest): 21 days (intent tends to cluster; reduce spam)
- Additionally, apply “cooldown” on task creation:
  - If there is an open HubSpot task from the router for the same org_id within the suppression TTL, do not create a new task; instead append a timeline note and update the “latest_signal_*” properties.

D) Minimum confidence thresholds
- Introduce signal.confidence in [0,1] (already produced by scoring service in Phase 1).
- Route only if:
  - score >= routing_score_threshold (existing) AND
  - confidence >= 0.70 (default) AND
  - at least one “non-vanity” feature is present for intent signals (see E)
- Thresholds are config-driven by signal_type (YAML/env config).

E) “Vanity intent” suppression
- Docs/pricing intent signals must have at least one high-intent action in the window:
  - security faq page view
  - dedicated/private docs view
  - DPA/SOC2 request click
  - “contact sales” click
- Pure “pricing page view” or “docs homepage view” alone should not route.

F) Minimum volume requirements (scale signals)
- For scale-based triggers, require sustained usage not a one-day spike:
  - tokens_7d >= X AND tokens_7d / tokens_prev_7d >= Y OR spend_velocity_7d >= Z
  - If only a single-day spike: record but suppress with suppression_reason=insufficient_sustain
- X/Y/Z are owned by PM-482, but implementation here should be generic (takes fields from aggregator output).

G) Auditability
- For every candidate signal evaluated for routing, persist:
  - evaluation timestamp
  - route_decision: routed | suppressed
  - suppression_reason (enum)
  - suppression_ttl_days applied
  - matching_suppression_key (org_id + signal_type)
- Include these in structured logs for debugging and in analytics table for tuning.

Implementation plan
1) Add suppression evaluator module in scoring/routing service:
- Inputs: org_id, signal_type, score, confidence, feature_flags, last_routed_at, open_task_exists, allowlist/denylist lookups
- Output: route_decision + suppression metadata

2) Data stores
- allowlist/denylist stored as small tables in the analytics DB (managed via dbt seed or a simple admin script).
- suppression state stored in existing “plg_signal_state” table keyed by (org_id, signal_type) with last_routed_at + last_routed_signal_id.

3) HubSpot interaction changes
- If suppressed: no HubSpot write.
- If routed but task suppressed due to open task: only update company properties + timeline note.

4) Config
- Per-signal-type config for:
  - confidence threshold
  - suppression TTL
  - minimum volume/sustain checks enabled
  - vanity intent rules enabled

Acceptance criteria
- Internal/test orgs never create HubSpot tasks/notes from the router.
- For a given org_id + signal_type, we do not create more than one router task within the suppression TTL.
- When suppression triggers, the system records suppression_reason and does not fail the pipeline.
- Quartz Commerce false-positive class is mitigated by vanity-intent suppression and/or confidence threshold (verify against pilot cohort re-run).
- Dashboards can show: routed_count, suppressed_count by reason, and top suppressed orgs (for tuning).

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - denylist/allowlist precedence
  - suppression TTL behavior
  - open-task suppression
  - vanity intent rules
  - sustained usage requirement
- Integration test (staging):
  - use sandbox HubSpot company via allowlist, verify no duplicate tasks within TTL

Rollout
- Gate behind feature flag: PLG_SIGNAL_SUPPRESSION_V1
- Pilot: NA cohort only (as per PM-496)
- Review after 1 week: meeting booked rate, AE feedback, suppression breakdown

Risks / open questions
- Risk: Over-suppression reduces recall and slows conversions.
  - Mitigation: start with conservative TTLs and log suppressed signals for tuning.
- Risk: Allowlist/denylist changes become ad hoc.
  - Mitigation: require expiry_date and weekly review with Sales Ops.
- Open: Should compliance intent suppression TTL differ by region (EMEA residency signals)? Track but keep global defaults for MVP.

Links
- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/plg-to-sales-signal-routing-prd.json
- ADR schema v1: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-usage-intent-signals-schema-v1.json
- HubSpot routing ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-hubspot-routing-and-ownership-rules.json
- Pilot feedback false positive: sources/slack/sales/1746468910-false-positive-example-quartz-commerce.json
- Support escalation re unwanted outreach: sources/jira/customer-support/SUP-3392-customer-complaint-unwanted-sales-outreach-triggered-by-signal.json

Outcome
Implemented suppression + quality guardrails and shipped behind PLG_SIGNAL_SUPPRESSION_V1. Follow-up: incorporate suppression metrics into router dashboards (ENG-7824) and iterate thresholds with Growth/Sales Ops based on pilot week 2 data.
text:
Context
We’re seeing early pilot feedback that routed PLG signals can be noisy (false positives) and/or “spammy” (re-notifying on the same underlying behavior). This ticket defines and implements the first set of suppression + quality guardrails in the scoring/routing pipeline.

Primary goals
1) Reduce false positives and duplicate notifications without losing legitimate “sales-worthy” accounts.
2) Provide deterministic and auditable suppression behavior (why a signal did/didn’t route).
3) Ensure internal/test orgs never route into HubSpot.

Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Building a full review queue UI (we’ll use HubSpot views + internal logs for now).
- Fully automated outbound sequencing.

Problem statement
During pilot week 1, AEs flagged at least one high-friction false positive (Quartz Commerce) and multiple “repeat pings” driven by daily aggregation windows re-surfacing the same issue. We need suppression windows + minimum confidence thresholds + allow/deny lists to make the system usable.

Definitions (working)
- “Signal” = scored, deduped record representing an account-level sales-worthy event derived from product telemetry and/or intent.
- “Routing” = creating/updating HubSpot company properties + timeline note + follow-up task.
- “Suppression” = preventing routing for a period or condition, while still recording the signal internally for analysis.

Proposed guardrails (MVP)
A) Internal/test org filtering (hard deny)
- Denylist sources (union):
  1. org.is_internal flag (from auth/billing)
  2. email domain matches internal domains (redwoodinference.com, redwood.ai) for the org owner/admin
  3. known test workspace prefixes: “test-”, “dev-”, “sandbox-”, “demo-”
  4. explicit denylist table maintained by Growth Ops (for one-offs)
- If denylisted: signal is written to analytics with status=suppressed and suppression_reason=internal_org (do not route to HubSpot).

B) Allowlist (override)
- Explicit allowlist table (Growth Ops + Sales Ops) to override denylist for:
  - integration testing against a controlled HubSpot sandbox company
  - internal dogfooding accounts where we intentionally want to test routing
- Allowlist should require: org_id + environment (prod/sandbox) + expiry_date.

C) Suppression windows (dedupe + re-notification control)
- Per org_id + signal_type suppression TTL:
  - Default: 14 days
  - Reliability signals (429_rate_high, error_rate_high): 7 days (more time-sensitive)
  - Compliance intent (security_docs_views, vpc_private_docs_interest): 21 days (intent tends to cluster; reduce spam)
- Additionally, apply “cooldown” on task creation:
  - If there is an open HubSpot task from the router for the same org_id within the suppression TTL, do not create a new task; instead append a timeline note and update the “latest_signal_*” properties.

D) Minimum confidence thresholds
- Introduce signal.confidence in [0,1] (already produced by scoring service in Phase 1).
- Route only if:
  - score >= routing_score_threshold (existing) AND
  - confidence >= 0.70 (default) AND
  - at least one “non-vanity” feature is present for intent signals (see E)
- Thresholds are config-driven by signal_type (YAML/env config).

E) “Vanity intent” suppression
- Docs/pricing intent signals must have at least one high-intent action in the window:
  - security faq page view
  - dedicated/private docs view
  - DPA/SOC2 request click
  - “contact sales” click
- Pure “pricing page view” or “docs homepage view” alone should not route.

F) Minimum volume requirements (scale signals)
- For scale-based triggers, require sustained usage not a one-day spike:
  - tokens_7d >= X AND tokens_7d / tokens_prev_7d >= Y OR spend_velocity_7d >= Z
  - If only a single-day spike: record but suppress with suppression_reason=insufficient_sustain
- X/Y/Z are owned by PM-482, but implementation here should be generic (takes fields from aggregator output).

G) Auditability
- For every candidate signal evaluated for routing, persist:
  - evaluation timestamp
  - route_decision: routed | suppressed
  - suppression_reason (enum)
  - suppression_ttl_days applied
  - matching_suppression_key (org_id + signal_type)
- Include these in structured logs for debugging and in analytics table for tuning.

Implementation plan
1) Add suppression evaluator module in scoring/routing service:
- Inputs: org_id, signal_type, score, confidence, feature_flags, last_routed_at, open_task_exists, allowlist/denylist lookups
- Output: route_decision + suppression metadata

2) Data stores
- allowlist/denylist stored as small tables in the analytics DB (managed via dbt seed or a simple admin script).
- suppression state stored in existing “plg_signal_state” table keyed by (org_id, signal_type) with last_routed_at + last_routed_signal_id.

3) HubSpot interaction changes
- If suppressed: no HubSpot write.
- If routed but task suppressed due to open task: only update company properties + timeline note.

4) Config
- Per-signal-type config for:
  - confidence threshold
  - suppression TTL
  - minimum volume/sustain checks enabled
  - vanity intent rules enabled

Acceptance criteria
- Internal/test orgs never create HubSpot tasks/notes from the router.
- For a given org_id + signal_type, we do not create more than one router task within the suppression TTL.
- When suppression triggers, the system records suppression_reason and does not fail the pipeline.
- Quartz Commerce false-positive class is mitigated by vanity-intent suppression and/or confidence threshold (verify against pilot cohort re-run).
- Dashboards can show: routed_count, suppressed_count by reason, and top suppressed orgs (for tuning).

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - denylist/allowlist precedence
  - suppression TTL behavior
  - open-task suppression
  - vanity intent rules
  - sustained usage requirement
- Integration test (staging):
  - use sandbox HubSpot company via allowlist, verify no duplicate tasks within TTL

Rollout
- Gate behind feature flag: PLG_SIGNAL_SUPPRESSION_V1
- Pilot: NA cohort only (as per PM-496)
- Review after 1 week: meeting booked rate, AE feedback, suppression breakdown

Risks / open questions
- Risk: Over-suppression reduces recall and slows conversions.
  - Mitigation: start with conservative TTLs and log suppressed signals for tuning.
- Risk: Allowlist/denylist changes become ad hoc.
  - Mitigation: require expiry_date and weekly review with Sales Ops.
- Open: Should compliance intent suppression TTL differ by region (EMEA residency signals)? Track but keep global defaults for MVP.

Links
- PRD: sources/confluence/product-docs/requirements/plg-to-sales-signal-routing-prd.json
- ADR schema v1: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-usage-intent-signals-schema-v1.json
- HubSpot routing ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-hubspot-routing-and-ownership-rules.json
- Pilot feedback false positive: sources/slack/sales/1746468910-false-positive-example-quartz-commerce.json
- Support escalation re unwanted outreach: sources/jira/customer-support/SUP-3392-customer-complaint-unwanted-sales-outreach-triggered-by-signal.json

Outcome
Implemented suppression + quality guardrails and shipped behind PLG_SIGNAL_SUPPRESSION_V1. Follow-up: incorporate suppression metrics into router dashboards (ENG-7824) and iterate thresholds with Growth/Sales Ops based on pilot week 2 data.

title: Create Private Grafana dashboard: Runtime Overview (golden signals + GPU + batching/KV cache)
preview:
## Summary
Build the primary Grafana dashboard shipped in the Private Observability Minimum Pack to monitor the Redwood serving runtime in customer clusters.

This dashboard should be the first stop for:
- Customer platform teams operating Redwood Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Redwood Support/SRE during escalations

## Goals
1) Provide a single “Runtime Overview” dashboard that surfaces the highest-impact failure modes:
- Latency regressions (p50/p95/p99) and tail amplification
- Error rates / non-2xx responses / model execution errors
- Saturation signals: GPU utilization, GPU memory pressure, CPU/mem, queue depth, request concurrency
- Throughput signals: requests/sec, tokens/sec, decode vs prefill time, output tokens per request
- Batching + caching behavior: batch sizes, continuous batching effectiveness, KV cache hit rate/evictions (where available)

2) Work in Private constraints:
- Bounded metric cardinality (no unbounded per-tenant/per-route/per-model breakdowns by default)
- No reliance on external hosted console services
- Packaged and versioned with the offline/air-gapped bundle

3) Align with Private metrics/alerting standards:
- Naming conventions and labels per: `metrics-and-alerting-standards-for-private`
- Prefer recording rules from ENG-19144 when possible

## Non-goals (v1)
- Full per-tenant attribution dashboards (cardinality risk)
- Advanced tracing views (v1 may not include OTLP traces)
- Customer-specific panel customization beyond supported variables

## Dashboard placement / packaging
- Grafana folder: `Redwood Private / Runtime`
- Dashboard title: `Runtime Overview (Private)`
- Dashboard slug: `redwood-private-runtime-overview`
- Dashboard UID: `rw-priv-runtime-overview` (stable across upgrades)
- Shipped via observability-pack dashboard bundle and wired into `redwood-helm-charts` values.

Primary packaging PR: `sources/github/observability-pack/pr-18341-add-private-minimum-pack-dashboard-bundle.json`
Helm wiring PR: `sources/github/redwood-helm-charts/pr-18405-wire-observability-pack-into-private-values.json`

## Templating variables (must be safe)
Required:
- `cluster` (optional; some installs may not set)
- `namespace` (default `redwood`)
- `workload` / `app` (runtime deployment label; use bounded set)

Optional (disabled by default / limited):
- `model_family` (only if label is capped; do not use raw `model` if it’s unbounded)
- `route_class` (e.g., chat/completions/embeddings; avoid full route string)

## Panels (v1)
### Row 1: Topline status
- Requests/sec (RPS) + tokens/sec
- Error rate (%)
- p95 and p99 latency (end-to-end)
- Active requests / in-flight

### Row 2: Latency breakdown
- Prefill latency p95 (if available)
- Decode latency per token p95 (if available)
- Queue wait time p95 (proxy for backpressure)
- Tail latency heatmap (optional if histogram buckets available and not too expensive)

### Row 3: Throughput & token economics
- Input tokens/sec vs output tokens/sec
- Tokens/request (p50/p95)
- Streaming vs non-streaming request mix (if metric exists)

### Row 4: Batching & scheduling
- Continuous batching: avg batch size, max batch size (p95)
- Batch fill rate / batch utilization (if exposed)
- Scheduler queue depth
- Dropped / timed out queue events

### Row 5: KV cache & memory behavior
- KV cache hit rate (if runtime reports)
- KV cache evictions / churn
- GPU memory allocated vs total
- OOM events / CUDA errors (counter)

### Row 6: GPU fleet utilization
- GPU SM utilization (avg/p95)
- GPU memory utilization
- GPU temperature / throttling flags (only if node exporter/DCGM is present; panel should hide gracefully)
- Per-pod GPU allocation and restarts

### Row 7: Pod health / restarts
- Pod restart rate
- OOMKilled count (from kube-state-metrics)
- CPU throttling / memory RSS for runtime pods

## Query guidelines
- Prefer recording rules from ENG-19144 for golden signals to keep dashboards fast and consistent.
- Avoid wide queries over high-cardinality labels; use `sum by (namespace, workload)` style aggregations.
- Panels must render with a default 6h time range in <5s on a typical Private Prometheus (baseline sizing doc).

## Required metrics (inventory)
Must exist (or degrade gracefully):
- `redwood_runtime_requests_total` (or recorded `rw:runtime:rps`)
- `redwood_runtime_request_errors_total` (or `rw:runtime:error_rate`)
- `redwood_runtime_request_duration_seconds_*` histogram (or recorded p95/p99)
- `redwood_runtime_tokens_total` split input/output if possible
- `redwood_runtime_queue_depth` or equivalent
- `redwood_runtime_batch_size` (gauge or summary)
- GPU metrics: prefer DCGM if included; otherwise show “not available” state

Related normalization work:
- `sources/github/redwood/pr-18503-standardize-metric-labels-and-reduce-cardinality-for-private.json`

## UX / Support requirements
- Each row includes 1–2 short annotations on interpretation (“what good looks like”).
- Add links in dashboard description:
  - Day-2 runbook: `private-observability-day2-runbook`
  - Known issues: `private-observability-known-issues`
- Panel titles must be explicit about units (ms, req/s, tok/s).
- Include a small “Last deploy / build info” panel if we expose build labels (optional).

## Acceptance criteria
- Dashboard is included in the Private minimum pack bundle and enabled by default.
- Renders without errors in a reference VPC cluster and on-prem-like air-gapped validation environment.
- No panels require unbounded labels (`tenant_id`, raw `route`, raw `model`) by default.
- Panels auto-hide or show “metric not found” when optional collectors (DCGM) are absent.
- Review/approval from SRE (noise/perf), Telemetry (queries/recording rules), and Private Deployments (packaging).

## Implementation notes / decisions
- GPU panels: use DCGM metrics when present. If DCGM is not installed, GPU section shows a text panel with setup pointer instead of empty graphs.
- KV cache: we currently expose limited signals; shipped v1 with hit rate + evictions if available; otherwise omit and track parity gap in `private-install-observability-parity-gaps`.
- Model breakdown: removed raw `model` label usage per cardinality thread; we use `model_family` only when provided and capped.

## Links
- Bundle PR: observability-pack PR-18341
- Alert rules (context): observability-pack PR-18356, PR-18372
- Helm wiring: redwood-helm-charts PR-18405
- Metric standardization: redwood PR-18503
- Structured logging fields (future correlation): redwood PR-18521

## Open follow-ups
- Add “prefill vs decode” breakdown once runtime exports stable histograms in Private build (tracked separately).
- Confirm whether DCGM exporter will be part of minimum pack defaults or documented optional add-on (Product decision / ADR).

title: Private: baseline log collector config (structured parsing, buffering/backpressure, safe defaults)
preview:
## Context
Private installs (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) need a supported, repeatable log pipeline so customers and Redwood Support/SRE can debug health/perf issues without bespoke integrations.

This ticket implements the **baseline log collector** configuration shipped with the Private installer/Helm charts. Focus is on:
- parsing Redwood’s **structured JSON logs** reliably
- **bounded buffering** and sensible backpressure behavior when sinks are unavailable
- safe defaults (no secrets/PII leakage, predictable resource use)
- packaging friendliness for offline/on-prem

Scope includes collector config + Helm values + minimal docs for operators. Export-specific “recipes” (Splunk HEC / Elasticsearch) are tracked in ENG-19150/ENG-19151.

## Decision (v1)
Use **Fluent Bit** as the default log collector for Private.
Rationale:
- widely deployed in customer K8s environments (low friction)
- solid backpressure + buffering controls
- supports Loki, Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, and HTTP outputs; OTLP can be supported via http output/bridge where required
- straightforward air-gapped packaging (single image + config)

(See ADR-022 for overall log pipeline defaults and supported sinks.)

## What we’re shipping
### 1) Collector deployment shape
- `DaemonSet` in `redwood-observability` namespace (configurable)
- Tails container logs from:
  - control plane services (gateway, policy/routing, auth/RBAC integration points)
  - data plane (serving runtime pods)
  - installer/upgrade controller
- Adds Kubernetes metadata (pod, namespace, container) for triage

### 2) Structured parsing expectations
All Redwood components are expected to log **single-line JSON** records.
Collector handles:
- JSON parsing with time extraction (RFC3339 / epoch ms)
- tolerant parsing: if JSON parse fails, forward as raw with `parse_error=true` (prevents silent loss)

Expected common fields (non-exhaustive):
- `ts`, `level`, `msg`
- `service`, `component`, `version`
- `request_id` (required for supportability)
- `trace_id` / `span_id` (when present)
- `route` and `model` (if present; *do not promote to labels*)
- `tenant_id_hash` (hashed value only; no raw tenant IDs)

### 3) Safe defaults (redaction + label/cardinality control)
**Redaction / drop rules (collector-side defense-in-depth):**
- Drop keys if present: `authorization`, `cookie`, `set-cookie`, `x-api-key`, `api_key`, `token`, `id_token`, `refresh_token`, `client_secret`.
- Drop any field matching `*_secret` and `*_password` (case-insensitive).
- Ensure we never forward request/response bodies even if an upstream service accidentally logs them: drop keys `prompt`, `messages`, `completion`, `request_body`, `response_body`.

**Label discipline:**
- For Loki (and any label-based sinks), only label on low-cardinality dimensions:
  - `namespace`, `pod`, `container`, `service`, `level`, `cluster`
- Keep `model`, `route`, and `request_id` as *log fields* (not labels) to avoid runaway cardinality.

### 4) Buffering/backpressure behavior
Goal: avoid collector OOM / noisy neighbor scenarios and keep cluster stable if the sink is down.

Defaults (configurable via Helm values):
- Memory buffer limits per input (`Mem_Buf_Limit`) set conservatively
- Enable filesystem buffering where available:
  - `storage.type=filesystem`
  - default to `emptyDir` with size cap; allow PVC override for durability
- Flush interval: 5s (tunable)
- Retry with backoff; bounded total retries. After limits are hit, drop oldest buffered chunks (documented) rather than crash-loop.

Operational note: we optimize for **cluster health** over perfect log durability in v1, but provide a PVC-backed option for customers that require higher durability.

### 5) Outputs / sinks (baseline)
Supported sink stubs are included behind feature flags; v1 default is:
- **Loki in-cluster** if `observability.loki.enabled=true`
- otherwise: collector is deployed but output defaults to `stdout` (so customers relying on existing node-level logging can still collect), with docs on enabling a supported sink

Export targets are implemented/documented in follow-on tickets:
- Splunk HEC (ENG-19150)
- Elasticsearch/OpenSearch (ENG-19151)

### 6) Metrics for the collector
Expose Fluent Bit metrics endpoint for Prometheus scraping:
- input records / bytes
- output errors
- retries
- dropped records
- buffer usage

These metrics will be wired into minimum pack dashboards/alerts separately, but this ticket ensures the endpoint is enabled and labeled consistently.

## Helm values (proposed)
`observability.logCollector.enabled`: true|false
`observability.logCollector.type`: `fluent-bit`
`observability.logCollector.resources.requests/limits`
`observability.logCollector.buffer.type`: `memory|filesystem`
`observability.logCollector.buffer.storageClassName` (optional)
`observability.logCollector.outputs`: `loki|splunkHec|elasticsearch|stdout|otlpHttp`
`observability.logCollector.redaction.enabled`: true
`observability.logCollector.redaction.dropKeys`: override list

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Fluent Bit DaemonSet deploys via `redwood-helm-charts` with default values in both VPC and on-prem reference clusters
- [ ] Redwood service logs appear in Loki when enabled; dashboards can query by `service` + `level` + `pod`
- [ ] Backpressure validation: when Loki is unreachable for 30 minutes, collector does not OOM and does not crash-loop; dropped-record behavior is observable in metrics
- [ ] Redaction validation: synthetic logs containing `authorization`/`token`/`prompt` fields are forwarded with those fields removed
- [ ] Air-gapped packaging: collector image + config artifacts included in offline bundle manifest (wiring may be completed under ENG-19154)
- [ ] Security review sign-off on the default redaction policy (ENG-19157)

## Risks / open questions
- Some customers run restrictive NetworkPolicies; we may need a dedicated doc snippet + installer preflight hint (ENG-19153) to ensure egress to Loki/Splunk/ES endpoints.
- Loki label strategy must remain stable across upgrades to avoid broken queries.
- OTLP export for logs varies by customer; keep as “supported but not default” for v1 unless Product requires it.

## Implementation notes
- Config delivered as a versioned ConfigMap rendered by Helm to keep bundle packaging deterministic.
- Avoid per-tenant indexing/labels; keep `tenant_id_hash` as a field only.
- Prefer predictable keys (`service`, `component`, `cluster`) to keep customer-side parsing simple.

## Links
- ADR-022: Log pipeline defaults (Private)
- Related: ENG-19150 (Splunk HEC export), ENG-19151 (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch export)
- Wiring: ENG-19152 (Helm charts wire-in)
- Security: ENG-19157 (redaction/retention review)
text:
## Context
Private installs (VPC + on-prem/air-gapped) need a supported, repeatable log pipeline so customers and Redwood Support/SRE can debug health/perf issues without bespoke integrations.

This ticket implements the **baseline log collector** configuration shipped with the Private installer/Helm charts. Focus is on:
- parsing Redwood’s **structured JSON logs** reliably
- **bounded buffering** and sensible backpressure behavior when sinks are unavailable
- safe defaults (no secrets/PII leakage, predictable resource use)
- packaging friendliness for offline/on-prem

Scope includes collector config + Helm values + minimal docs for operators. Export-specific “recipes” (Splunk HEC / Elasticsearch) are tracked in ENG-19150/ENG-19151.

## Decision (v1)
Use **Fluent Bit** as the default log collector for Private.
Rationale:
- widely deployed in customer K8s environments (low friction)
- solid backpressure + buffering controls
- supports Loki, Splunk HEC, Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, and HTTP outputs; OTLP can be supported via http output/bridge where required
- straightforward air-gapped packaging (single image + config)

(See ADR-022 for overall log pipeline defaults and supported sinks.)

## What we’re shipping
### 1) Collector deployment shape
- `DaemonSet` in `redwood-observability` namespace (configurable)
- Tails container logs from:
  - control plane services (gateway, policy/routing, auth/RBAC integration points)
  - data plane (serving runtime pods)
  - installer/upgrade controller
- Adds Kubernetes metadata (pod, namespace, container) for triage

### 2) Structured parsing expectations
All Redwood components are expected to log **single-line JSON** records.
Collector handles:
- JSON parsing with time extraction (RFC3339 / epoch ms)
- tolerant parsing: if JSON parse fails, forward as raw with `parse_error=true` (prevents silent loss)

Expected common fields (non-exhaustive):
- `ts`, `level`, `msg`
- `service`, `component`, `version`
- `request_id` (required for supportability)
- `trace_id` / `span_id` (when present)
- `route` and `model` (if present; *do not promote to labels*)
- `tenant_id_hash` (hashed value only; no raw tenant IDs)

### 3) Safe defaults (redaction + label/cardinality control)
**Redaction / drop rules (collector-side defense-in-depth):**
- Drop keys if present: `authorization`, `cookie`, `set-cookie`, `x-api-key`, `api_key`, `token`, `id_token`, `refresh_token`, `client_secret`.
- Drop any field matching `*_secret` and `*_password` (case-insensitive).
- Ensure we never forward request/response bodies even if an upstream service accidentally logs them: drop keys `prompt`, `messages`, `completion`, `request_body`, `response_body`.

**Label discipline:**
- For Loki (and any label-based sinks), only label on low-cardinality dimensions:
  - `namespace`, `pod`, `container`, `service`, `level`, `cluster`
- Keep `model`, `route`, and `request_id` as *log fields* (not labels) to avoid runaway cardinality.

### 4) Buffering/backpressure behavior
Goal: avoid collector OOM / noisy neighbor scenarios and keep cluster stable if the sink is down.

Defaults (configurable via Helm values):
- Memory buffer limits per input (`Mem_Buf_Limit`) set conservatively
- Enable filesystem buffering where available:
  - `storage.type=filesystem`
  - default to `emptyDir` with size cap; allow PVC override for durability
- Flush interval: 5s (tunable)
- Retry with backoff; bounded total retries. After limits are hit, drop oldest buffered chunks (documented) rather than crash-loop.

Operational note: we optimize for **cluster health** over perfect log durability in v1, but provide a PVC-backed option for customers that require higher durability.

### 5) Outputs / sinks (baseline)
Supported sink stubs are included behind feature flags; v1 default is:
- **Loki in-cluster** if `observability.loki.enabled=true`
- otherwise: collector is deployed but output defaults to `stdout` (so customers relying on existing node-level logging can still collect), with docs on enabling a supported sink

Export targets are implemented/documented in follow-on tickets:
- Splunk HEC (ENG-19150)
- Elasticsearch/OpenSearch (ENG-19151)

### 6) Metrics for the collector
Expose Fluent Bit metrics endpoint for Prometheus scraping:
- input records / bytes
- output errors
- retries
- dropped records
- buffer usage

These metrics will be wired into minimum pack dashboards/alerts separately, but this ticket ensures the endpoint is enabled and labeled consistently.

## Helm values (proposed)
`observability.logCollector.enabled`: true|false
`observability.logCollector.type`: `fluent-bit`
`observability.logCollector.resources.requests/limits`
`observability.logCollector.buffer.type`: `memory|filesystem`
`observability.logCollector.buffer.storageClassName` (optional)
`observability.logCollector.outputs`: `loki|splunkHec|elasticsearch|stdout|otlpHttp`
`observability.logCollector.redaction.enabled`: true
`observability.logCollector.redaction.dropKeys`: override list

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Fluent Bit DaemonSet deploys via `redwood-helm-charts` with default values in both VPC and on-prem reference clusters
- [ ] Redwood service logs appear in Loki when enabled; dashboards can query by `service` + `level` + `pod`
- [ ] Backpressure validation: when Loki is unreachable for 30 minutes, collector does not OOM and does not crash-loop; dropped-record behavior is observable in metrics
- [ ] Redaction validation: synthetic logs containing `authorization`/`token`/`prompt` fields are forwarded with those fields removed
- [ ] Air-gapped packaging: collector image + config artifacts included in offline bundle manifest (wiring may be completed under ENG-19154)
- [ ] Security review sign-off on the default redaction policy (ENG-19157)

## Risks / open questions
- Some customers run restrictive NetworkPolicies; we may need a dedicated doc snippet + installer preflight hint (ENG-19153) to ensure egress to Loki/Splunk/ES endpoints.
- Loki label strategy must remain stable across upgrades to avoid broken queries.
- OTLP export for logs varies by customer; keep as “supported but not default” for v1 unless Product requires it.

## Implementation notes
- Config delivered as a versioned ConfigMap rendered by Helm to keep bundle packaging deterministic.
- Avoid per-tenant indexing/labels; keep `tenant_id_hash` as a field only.
- Prefer predictable keys (`service`, `component`, `cluster`) to keep customer-side parsing simple.

## Links
- ADR-022: Log pipeline defaults (Private)
- Related: ENG-19150 (Splunk HEC export), ENG-19151 (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch export)
- Wiring: ENG-19152 (Helm charts wire-in)
- Security: ENG-19157 (redaction/retention review)

title: Inventory existing metrics/dashboards used in Hosted and identify what can ship in Private minimum observability pack
preview:
Goal: compile an inventory of the metrics + Grafana dashboards currently used in Hosted/Dedicated and determine what we can safely and supportably ship as part of the Private observability minimum pack.

Why: Private installs cannot rely on Redwood’s internal telemetry stack assumptions (Mimir, centralized logs, internal label conventions, multi-tenant routing). We need a bounded-cardinality, documented set of signals that works on customer-managed Prometheus/Grafana (and air-gapped packaging), with minimal bespoke setup.

Scope:
- Review existing Hosted/Dedicated Grafana dashboards and panels used by SRE/oncall for inference runtime + control plane.
- Identify required underlying Prometheus metrics (including histograms/summaries) and current label sets.
- Note gaps for Private (missing instrumentation, high-cardinality labels, dependencies on internal data sources).
- Recommend a v1 dashboard set for Private minimum pack, plus required metric normalization/recording rules.

Out of scope:
- Building new dashboards in this ticket (tracked separately).
- Log pipeline defaults (tracked separately).

Deliverable:
- Inventory doc (dashboard -> panels -> metrics -> labels -> notes)
- Proposed “ship/not ship” decision per dashboard with rationale
- Follow-up tasks (instrumentation gaps, label reductions, recording rules) filed/linked

title: HubSpot integration: company lookup + signal property upserts + notes/tasks with retry/rate-limit handling
preview:
Context
We’re implementing the “PLG-to-sales signal routing” pipeline. Scoring will output qualified signals (company/org-level) and we need to route them into HubSpot reliably without spamming reps or breaching rate limits.

This ticket covers the HubSpot integration service responsibilities:
- resolve a Redwood org/account to a HubSpot Company
- upsert Company properties (current signal + rollups)
- append an Activity/Timeline note describing the signal event
- create a follow-up Task with deterministic ownership rules
- implement retries + rate-limit handling + audit logs so we can prove what happened and re-drive safely

Goals / Requirements
1) Company lookup
- Primary key: Redwood org_id (preferred) mapped to a HubSpot custom property (e.g., redwood_org_id).
- Secondary: company domain (for legacy self-serve orgs where org_id isn’t backfilled yet).
- If multiple companies match domain, select the one with an exact domain match and highest “last activity”; otherwise fail and audit with reason=ambiguous_company_match.
- Create vs update: MVP = do not create new companies automatically. If not found, log/audit and exit gracefully (so we don’t pollute CRM). We can add create-on-miss in a later phase if Sales Ops requests.

2) Property upserts (Company)
Upsert the canonical set of PLG signal properties (defined by ADR schema v1 / PM-482). For MVP we will include:
- redwood_plg_signal_last_seen_at (ISO timestamp)
- redwood_plg_signal_last_type (enum string)
- redwood_plg_signal_last_score (integer as string)
- redwood_plg_signal_last_window (e.g., “7d”, “30d”)
- redwood_plg_signal_types_30d (comma-separated set)
- redwood_plg_signal_score_30d (integer rollup)
- redwood_plg_signal_suppressed_until (ISO timestamp; optional)
- redwood_plg_signal_source (e.g., “router-v1”)

Rules:
- All updates must be idempotent (safe to replay). If the incoming signal has a signal_id and occurs_at, only update “last_*” fields if occurs_at is newer than current value.
- Do not overwrite existing owner (HubSpot ownerId) unless explicitly told by routing rules (see below). Owner preservation is critical for CS alignment and existing pipeline.

3) Activity note (timeline/engagement)
For each qualified signal routed, append a short, standardized note to the Company timeline:
- Title: “PLG Signal: <signal_type> (score <score>)”
- Body includes: signal_id, occurs_at, top contributing metrics (e.g., tokens_7d, spend_velocity, 429_rate), recommended next step (“Dedicated eval”, “Security review”, etc.), and link back to internal dashboard (if available).
- Note content must not include raw prompts, user messages, API keys, or end-user PII. Stick to aggregate usage + intent flags.

4) Task creation
Create a follow-up Task for the Company when:
- signal qualifies AND
- not currently suppressed AND
- no identical task exists in the last N days (dedupe window; default 14d) AND
- no open task already exists with the same signal_type tag (to reduce spam)

Task fields:
- Subject: “Follow up: PLG signal – <signal_type>”
- Body: include a short talk track and recommended discovery questions; include the same signal summary as the note.
- Due date: default +1 business day for threshold/reliability signals; same-day for compliance/private intent; configurable via routing config.
- Task association: to Company; optionally associate to primary Contact if available later (MVP company-only).

Ownership rules (implementation hooks; detailed in ADR routing doc)
- Preserve existing Company owner if set (ownerId present) unless the record is explicitly assigned to “Unassigned / Round Robin” bucket.
- If no owner:
  - map region based on billing country / inferred region / existing routing table
  - apply round-robin within region pool (Sales Ops maintained list)
- If Company is an existing customer with a CSM owner property set, ensure we notify CS (separate ticket) and avoid assigning to SDR-only pools.

Reliability / Rate limiting
- Use HubSpot public APIs with a client wrapper that supports:
  - automatic retries on 429 and transient 5xx
  - exponential backoff with jitter, obey Retry-After when present
  - bounded retry attempts (max 6) and total time budget per job item
- Implement per-portal rate limiting via a token bucket in the worker (configurable). The router should queue work and drain at a safe rate instead of bursting.
- Idempotency keys:
  - For notes/tasks: include signal_id in the body and store last_routed_signal_id + last_routed_at in our audit log so we can avoid duplicates.
  - If HubSpot supports external IDs for tasks/engagements in our chosen endpoints, set it; otherwise implement application-level dedupe by searching recent engagements for signal_id.

Audit logging / Observability
- Every attempted route writes an audit record (success or failure) with:
  - redwood_org_id, hubspot_company_id (if found), signal_id, signal_type, occurs_at
  - actions attempted: [company_update, note_create, task_create]
  - outcome: success|suppressed|deduped|failed
  - error_code + error_detail (sanitized)
  - hubspot_request_ids when available
- Logs must be structured and queryable (Datadog), and audit records must be persisted (analytics table or durable store used by signal-router service).
- Emit metrics counters: hubspot_requests_total, hubspot_429_total, hubspot_5xx_total, hubspot_task_created_total, hubspot_task_deduped_total, hubspot_company_not_found_total.

Security / Privacy
- Use a HubSpot private app token or OAuth app credentials stored in secrets manager (per #eng-platform guidance). No tokens in logs.
- Scopes: restrict to the minimum needed (crm.objects.companies.read/write, crm.objects.tasks.write, timeline/engagement write).
- Data minimization: do not send user identifiers, raw IPs, raw URLs with query params, or any prompt/content. Only aggregate metrics + high-level intent categories.
- Retention: audit table retention must match PLG signal retention policy (see data-handling doc); ensure delete requests can be honored by keying on redwood_org_id.

Implementation notes
- Create a HubSpotClient wrapper module used by the router worker:
  - request() method with retry policy, backoff, and response classification
  - typed helpers: findCompanyByOrgId(), findCompanyByDomain(), updateCompanyProperties(), createCompanyNote(), createCompanyTask()
- Maintain a config file for:
  - dedupe windows
  - suppression windows
  - routing pools (region -> owner IDs)
  - task templates by signal_type

Testing
- Unit tests:
  - retry behavior (429 + Retry-After, 5xx)
  - idempotency rules (replay same signal does not create second task)
  - owner preservation logic
- Integration tests (HubSpot sandbox portal):
  - company lookup by org_id and by domain
  - property upsert end-to-end
  - task creation + note creation

Acceptance Criteria
- Given a qualified signal with a known HubSpot company, the integration updates the expected properties, creates a timeline note, and creates exactly one task assigned per routing rules.
- Replaying the same signal (same signal_id) does not create duplicate tasks/notes.
- When HubSpot returns 429, the job retries and completes successfully without exceeding configured throughput; failures are audited.
- All routed actions are audit-logged with enough context to debug and re-drive.
- Security review sign-off: Dr. Aisha Rahman / Marcus Lin confirm fields sent to HubSpot comply with PII minimization.

Rollout Plan
- Deploy behind feature flag: hubspot_routing_enabled=false by default.
- Dry-run mode (log/audit only, no writes) for 48 hours in NA pilot cohort.
- Enable writes for a limited cohort (per PM-496). Weekly review of volume/noise and adjust dedupe/suppression.

Links / Related
- PM-482 (signal definitions + success metrics)
- ENG-7732 (daily aggregation job)
- ADR: usage/intent signals schema v1
- ADR: HubSpot routing + ownership rules

Outcome / Ship notes
- Implemented HubSpot client wrapper with retry/backoff + structured audit logs.
- Integrated into signal-router worker with safe throttling and dedupe checks.
- Verified against sandbox portal and NA pilot cohort before enabling production writes.

title: Audit exporter observability: metrics, tracing, structured logs + dashboards/alerts
preview:
Goal
Ship production-grade observability for the audit-log-exporter service powering the SIEM-ready audit export beta:
- Metrics (Prometheus) suitable for SLO computation and alerting
- Traces (OpenTelemetry) for adapter debugging + config sync issues
- Structured logs (JSON) that are actionable and safe (PII-minimized)
- Grafana dashboards + alert rules aligned with SRE SLO doc

Context
Exporter delivers canonical audit events to Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 with at-least-once semantics. During beta we need rapid triage on:
- Lag spikes (downstream throttling, backpressure, exporter stuck)
- Delivery failures (auth, throttling, bad requests, regressions)
- DLQ growth and replay needs
- Config sync failures from control plane

Non-goals
- Per-tenant/destination metrics in production Prometheus (cardinality). Per-destination details must come from traces/logs and the internal status endpoint.
- Customer-facing metrics export for beta.

Acceptance criteria
1) Metrics
- Export lag observable and alertable (global + beta tenant tier).
- Delivery success and failures broken down by adapter + error_class (auth/throttle/network/timeout/bad_request/server_error/unknown).
- Signals for config sync health, checkpoint staleness, DLQ activity, and pause/backpressure state.
- Label/cardinality policy enforced (no tenant_id/workspace_id/destination_id/event_id/raw hostnames in metric labels).

2) Tracing
- End-to-end spans covering: config sync, filter evaluation, adapter send attempts/retries, checkpoint commit.
- Trace attributes are low-card only (adapter, outcome, error_class, deployment_mode, region). Anything tenant/destination-specific must be in logs (hashed) and must not be added as attributes.

3) Structured logs
- JSON logs with consistent keys: timestamp, level, message, adapter, error_class, outcome, retry_count, batch_size_events, duration_ms, checkpoint_age_seconds, trace_id/span_id.
- Redaction rules applied (no tokens/credentials, no raw URLs, no request bodies containing event payloads).
- Include destination_hash (short stable hash) ONLY in logs (not metrics) to enable per-destination debugging during beta.

4) Dashboards + alerts
- Grafana dashboards: Overview + Adapter deep dive + Queue/backpressure.
- Alert rules align with SRE doc: lag runaway, config sync failure, auto-pause triggered, DLQ growth, delivery success rate burn (non-retryable gated).
- Paging vs ticket-only classification matches SRE guidance.

Implementation notes / decisions
- Metrics naming follows `audit_exporter_*` prefix and Prometheus conventions. Where both histogram + gauge are desired, we prefer a histogram for analysis and a derived/max gauge for cheaper alert queries.
- Lag definition: measured on successful delivery as end-to-end (event timestamp to destination acceptance). For “stuck” detection, checkpoint_age_seconds is the fallback even when successful deliveries are sparse.
- Error classification is performed at adapter boundary with a controlled vocabulary. We bucket HTTP status to classes (auth/throttle/etc.) and avoid labeling raw status codes.
- Cardinality constraints (beta):
  - Allowed labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, outcome, error_class, reason (controlled).
  - Disallowed labels: tenant_id, destination_id, config_id, event_id, request_id, full endpoint/host, Splunk index/sourcetype, S3 bucket name, Datadog tags.

Shipped metrics (high-level)
- Lag: audit_exporter_export_lag_seconds (histogram), audit_exporter_lag_max_seconds (gauge)
- Throughput: audit_exporter_events_received_total, audit_exporter_events_exported_total
- Filtering/drop: audit_exporter_events_dropped_total{reason=...}
- Delivery: audit_exporter_batches_attempted_total, audit_exporter_batches_succeeded_total, audit_exporter_adapter_requests_total, audit_exporter_adapter_request_duration_seconds
- Errors/retries: audit_exporter_adapter_errors_total{error_class=...}, audit_exporter_retry_total{reason=...}
- DLQ: audit_exporter_dlq_enqueued_total, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog
- Checkpointing: audit_exporter_checkpoint_age_seconds
- Config sync: audit_exporter_config_sync_success_total, audit_exporter_config_sync_errors_total
- Pausing/backpressure: audit_exporter_paused{pause_reason=...}, audit_exporter_queue_depth

Dashboards/alerts
- Dashboards + alert rules landed in observability-pack and are referenced from the SRE SLO page.
- Alert gating logic implemented as per SRE guidance:
  - Prefer paging on global/fast-burn and Redwood-owned issues (config sync broken, exporter not making progress across multiple tenants, auto-pause triggered for beta cohort).
  - Auth misconfig spikes generate ticket-only alerts unless multi-tenant and correlated to a regression.

Rollout
- Metrics + logs shipped behind the exporter deployment (no feature flag). Cardinality protections are in code review checklist.
- Dashboards/alerts enabled for beta environments first, with alert routing to SRE primary and Observability secondary during beta weeks.

Risks / mitigations
- Risk: accidental high-card labels (tenant/destination) explode Prometheus.
  - Mitigation: explicit lint/checklist in code review; tests asserting label sets; keep per-destination only in logs.
- Risk: logs leak secrets (Splunk HEC token, Datadog API key, S3 role ARN details).
  - Mitigation: never log headers; log only credential reference IDs; apply structured redaction helpers.

Follow-ups (not blocking Done)
- Evaluate whether we need a dedicated internal metrics pipeline for destination_hash for beta debugging (currently logs+traces are sufficient).
- Add a small “known error” playbook panel in Grafana (Datadog 403 key scope, Splunk 429 throttle) linking to runbook sections.
text:
Goal
Ship production-grade observability for the audit-log-exporter service powering the SIEM-ready audit export beta:
- Metrics (Prometheus) suitable for SLO computation and alerting
- Traces (OpenTelemetry) for adapter debugging + config sync issues
- Structured logs (JSON) that are actionable and safe (PII-minimized)
- Grafana dashboards + alert rules aligned with SRE SLO doc

Context
Exporter delivers canonical audit events to Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, and S3 with at-least-once semantics. During beta we need rapid triage on:
- Lag spikes (downstream throttling, backpressure, exporter stuck)
- Delivery failures (auth, throttling, bad requests, regressions)
- DLQ growth and replay needs
- Config sync failures from control plane

Non-goals
- Per-tenant/destination metrics in production Prometheus (cardinality). Per-destination details must come from traces/logs and the internal status endpoint.
- Customer-facing metrics export for beta.

Acceptance criteria
1) Metrics
- Export lag observable and alertable (global + beta tenant tier).
- Delivery success and failures broken down by adapter + error_class (auth/throttle/network/timeout/bad_request/server_error/unknown).
- Signals for config sync health, checkpoint staleness, DLQ activity, and pause/backpressure state.
- Label/cardinality policy enforced (no tenant_id/workspace_id/destination_id/event_id/raw hostnames in metric labels).

2) Tracing
- End-to-end spans covering: config sync, filter evaluation, adapter send attempts/retries, checkpoint commit.
- Trace attributes are low-card only (adapter, outcome, error_class, deployment_mode, region). Anything tenant/destination-specific must be in logs (hashed) and must not be added as attributes.

3) Structured logs
- JSON logs with consistent keys: timestamp, level, message, adapter, error_class, outcome, retry_count, batch_size_events, duration_ms, checkpoint_age_seconds, trace_id/span_id.
- Redaction rules applied (no tokens/credentials, no raw URLs, no request bodies containing event payloads).
- Include destination_hash (short stable hash) ONLY in logs (not metrics) to enable per-destination debugging during beta.

4) Dashboards + alerts
- Grafana dashboards: Overview + Adapter deep dive + Queue/backpressure.
- Alert rules align with SRE doc: lag runaway, config sync failure, auto-pause triggered, DLQ growth, delivery success rate burn (non-retryable gated).
- Paging vs ticket-only classification matches SRE guidance.

Implementation notes / decisions
- Metrics naming follows `audit_exporter_*` prefix and Prometheus conventions. Where both histogram + gauge are desired, we prefer a histogram for analysis and a derived/max gauge for cheaper alert queries.
- Lag definition: measured on successful delivery as end-to-end (event timestamp to destination acceptance). For “stuck” detection, checkpoint_age_seconds is the fallback even when successful deliveries are sparse.
- Error classification is performed at adapter boundary with a controlled vocabulary. We bucket HTTP status to classes (auth/throttle/etc.) and avoid labeling raw status codes.
- Cardinality constraints (beta):
  - Allowed labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, outcome, error_class, reason (controlled).
  - Disallowed labels: tenant_id, destination_id, config_id, event_id, request_id, full endpoint/host, Splunk index/sourcetype, S3 bucket name, Datadog tags.

Shipped metrics (high-level)
- Lag: audit_exporter_export_lag_seconds (histogram), audit_exporter_lag_max_seconds (gauge)
- Throughput: audit_exporter_events_received_total, audit_exporter_events_exported_total
- Filtering/drop: audit_exporter_events_dropped_total{reason=...}
- Delivery: audit_exporter_batches_attempted_total, audit_exporter_batches_succeeded_total, audit_exporter_adapter_requests_total, audit_exporter_adapter_request_duration_seconds
- Errors/retries: audit_exporter_adapter_errors_total{error_class=...}, audit_exporter_retry_total{reason=...}
- DLQ: audit_exporter_dlq_enqueued_total, audit_exporter_dlq_backlog
- Checkpointing: audit_exporter_checkpoint_age_seconds
- Config sync: audit_exporter_config_sync_success_total, audit_exporter_config_sync_errors_total
- Pausing/backpressure: audit_exporter_paused{pause_reason=...}, audit_exporter_queue_depth

Dashboards/alerts
- Dashboards + alert rules landed in observability-pack and are referenced from the SRE SLO page.
- Alert gating logic implemented as per SRE guidance:
  - Prefer paging on global/fast-burn and Redwood-owned issues (config sync broken, exporter not making progress across multiple tenants, auto-pause triggered for beta cohort).
  - Auth misconfig spikes generate ticket-only alerts unless multi-tenant and correlated to a regression.

Rollout
- Metrics + logs shipped behind the exporter deployment (no feature flag). Cardinality protections are in code review checklist.
- Dashboards/alerts enabled for beta environments first, with alert routing to SRE primary and Observability secondary during beta weeks.

Risks / mitigations
- Risk: accidental high-card labels (tenant/destination) explode Prometheus.
  - Mitigation: explicit lint/checklist in code review; tests asserting label sets; keep per-destination only in logs.
- Risk: logs leak secrets (Splunk HEC token, Datadog API key, S3 role ARN details).
  - Mitigation: never log headers; log only credential reference IDs; apply structured redaction helpers.

Follow-ups (not blocking Done)
- Evaluate whether we need a dedicated internal metrics pipeline for destination_hash for beta debugging (currently logs+traces are sufficient).
- Add a small “known error” playbook panel in Grafana (Datadog 403 key scope, Splunk 429 throttle) linking to runbook sections.

title: Onboarding KPIs dashboard + regression alerts (TTFS/TTFP + checklist funnel)
preview:
Build the canonical onboarding KPI dashboard and alerting for the guided onboarding / first production launch checklist initiative.

We need end-to-end visibility from signup -> first successful API call (TTFS) -> first production traffic (TTFP), plus the step completion funnel for the in-console checklist. This dashboard will be the source of truth for rollout go/no-go and ongoing regression monitoring.

Primary users:
- Product/DevEx: Nikhil Sharma, Marissa Cole, Alex Martinez, Chris Osei
- Telemetry/Data Eng: Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman, Mei Lin
- SRE/Observability review: Allison Grant, Aisha Bello
- Support enablement: Dev Patel (to correlate top onboarding ticket spikes)

References:
- Confluence spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/onboarding-kpis-dashboard-spec.json
- Metric definition ticket: PM-751 (TTFS/TTFP exact logic + cohorting)
- Event schema + ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/api-standards/onboarding-event-schema-v1.json + sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-2025-02-onboarding-checklist-eventing.json
- Backfill: ENG-5844 (pre/post comparison for existing tenants)

Scope (MVP):
1) Dashboard (packaged) with core KPIs and funnel
- TTFS (p50/p90) and distribution: signup_at -> first_success_at
- TTFP (p50/p90) and distribution: signup_at -> first_prod_traffic_at
- Funnel: % of tenants completing each checklist step (and time-to-step)
- Breakdown/filters:
  - cohort: checklist feature flag on/off, dogfood vs external, created_at week
  - SDK / integration: python | typescript | go | openai-compat | unknown (user-agent tags)
  - region
  - account type/tier (self-serve vs sales-assisted where available)
- Drilldowns:
  - top drop-off steps
  - step transition timing (median time from prior step)
  - sample tenant list (internal-only) for debugging

2) Alerts
- Regression: TTFS p50 worsens > X% week-over-week (or absolute threshold) for checklist-on cohort
- Regression: funnel completion to “First successful request” step drops by > X points vs trailing 7d baseline
- Regression: TTFP p50 worsens for checklist-on cohort (guardrail for rollout)
- Data freshness: onboarding events dataset lag > 90 minutes
- Data quality: schema version unknown / required fields missing rate > threshold

3) Data quality checks / monitors
- Event volume sanity checks by day + by sdk
- Duplicate idempotency key rate (indicator of gateway retries/misconfig)
- Out-of-order event rate (should be mitigated by monotonic transitions in progress service; still track)
- Cardinality guardrails for high-cardinality fields (e.g., raw user_agent, prompt hashes) to prevent cost blow-ups

Out of scope (MVP):
- Automated root-cause detection / recommended remediation
- Per-customer support dashboards in Console

Notes:
- TTFS/TTFP must match PM-751 definitions exactly; do not “approximate” in dashboard queries.
- Ensure we can segment checklist-on vs checklist-off cohorts for the rollout plan (PM-768).
- This should be ship-ready for Phase 1 internal dogfood and Phase 2 cohort rollout monitoring.

title: Backfill baseline TTFS/TTFP + onboarding funnel for existing tenants (pre-checklist comparison)
preview:
Goal
Backfill baseline Time-to-First-Success (TTFS) and Time-to-First-Production-Traffic (TTFP) for existing tenants so we can compare pre/post Console checklist rollout and quantify funnel improvements.

Why this exists
The checklist + new eventing will only be present after rollout. We need a comparable baseline for tenants created before the checklist launch to:
- validate metric definitions (PM-751)
- build a pre/post comparison cohort
- sanity-check telemetry quality (e.g., avoid false improvements because we only measure new tenants)

Scope (MVP)
1) Compute TTFS + TTFP for tenants created prior to checklist event emission.
2) Backfill an approximate onboarding funnel (step completion proxy) using existing control plane + gateway + usage/metering signals.
3) Publish results into analytics datasets that the onboarding KPI dashboard can query.
4) Add data quality checks + freshness monitoring for the backfill job outputs.

Non-goals
- Perfect parity with the post-launch event-driven funnel (this is a proxy baseline).
- Reconstructing step-by-step UI interactions in Console.
- Backfilling for deleted/merged tenants beyond standard retention windows.

Definitions (align with PM-751)
- Tenant created_at: control plane tenant creation timestamp.
- TTFS: first successful (2xx) inference response for the tenant in any region, excluding internal dogfood tenants and known synthetic load tests.
- TTFP: first sustained production-like traffic moment. Proxy definition for baseline: first 30-minute window with >= N successful requests AND >= M total tokens, where N/M are agreed with PM-751 (see Open Questions). If those thresholds change, keep job parameterized and re-runnable.

Data sources (proposed)
- control_plane.tenants (created_at, plan/tier flags, region/home shard)
- gateway.request_logs or gateway_events (status_code, route, model, streaming flag, timestamp, tenant_id)
- metering.usage_hourly or usage_tokens_daily (tenant_id, tokens, requests, timestamp)
- allow/deny lists:
  - internal tenants / Redwood staff accounts
  - known load test user-agents (OpenAI-compat tags) and synthetic traffic

Outputs
- analytics.onboarding_baseline_ttfs_ttfp (tenant_id, tenant_created_at, ttfs_at, ttfs_seconds, ttfp_at, ttfp_seconds, computed_at, method_version)
- analytics.onboarding_baseline_funnel (tenant_id, step_key, completed_at, computed_at, method_version)
- analytics.onboarding_baseline_summary_daily (date, cohort, ttfs_p50, ttfs_p90, ttfp_p50, ttfp_p90, counts)

Method versioning
Add method_version string (e.g., "baseline_v1") and store job git sha / run_id in computed_at metadata columns where possible. This is important since baseline heuristics may shift.

Approx funnel proxy mapping (initial proposal)
We cannot perfectly reconstruct checklist steps. Instead produce proxies that match the dashboard funnel shape:
- api_key_created: control plane audit log / API key table first row for tenant
- env_setup_completed: cannot be reliably inferred; omit or use first auth success? (recommend omit in baseline and start funnel at first successful request)
- first_request_success: gateway first 2xx on /chat or /completions or /responses endpoints
- streaming_success: first streaming response established (streaming=true + 2xx + duration>=X seconds)
- retries_timeouts_configured: cannot infer; omit
- viewed_usage_dashboard: cannot infer; omit
- quota_or_budget_configured: control plane quota/budget config created_at
- model_pinned: policy/config table indicates model_version_pin set
- fallback_policy_set: routing policy table shows fallback configured
- observability_hook: first trace header present or first request with tracing enabled (if available in logs); otherwise omit

Acceptance criteria
- Backfill covers at least the last 90 days of tenant signups (or max within retention) and excludes internal/dogfood tenants.
- TTFS and TTFP computed for >= 95% of eligible tenants that have any inference usage in the retention window.
- Dashboard can filter by cohort (pre-checklist baseline vs post-checklist event-driven) using a consistent cohort field.
- Data quality checks:
  - no negative durations
  - TTFS <= TTFP when both exist
  - daily row counts within expected bounds (alert on sudden drop)
  - freshness (job ran in last 24h while in active rollout)
- Document known limitations and where baseline proxies differ from post-launch event steps.

Implementation notes
- Prefer a batch job (Airflow/Prefect/DBT job depending on standard) that can run incrementally by tenant_created_at partition.
- Use a single canonical tenant_id mapping (watch for legacy org_id / project_id transitions).
- Use stable time bucketing in UTC.
- Be explicit about excluding "test" traffic (load tests) by user-agent and IP ranges if available.

Open questions
- Final TTFP thresholds (N requests / M tokens / window size) pending PM-751 decision.
- Confirm whether gateway logs include streaming establishment signal reliably across regions.
- Retention: what is the maximum lookback for request logs vs metering tables; choose the limiting factor and document.

Links
- Linear: PM-751 define TTFS/TTFP calculations
- Linear: ENG-5815 onboarding KPI dashboard and alerts
- GitHub: sources/github/redwood/pr-28475-backfill-time-to-first-success-metrics.json

DRI
Mei Lin (Data Eng) with Logan Wright (Telemetry) as reviewer for schema alignment.
