title: Backfill experiment metrics after attribution schema change (add {experiment_id, variant_id})
preview:
Context
We added experiment attribution dimensions to request telemetry per ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region). During beta we discovered that the Console experiment results dashboard depends on per-variant rollups; without a backfill, results for historical windows show as empty/partial (and comparisons are misleading).

This ticket plans + implements a backfill strategy for experiment metrics after the schema change, including:
- cost estimates
- throttling/guardrails
- correctness checks (attribution integrity)
- operational runbook notes

References
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-048-experiment-metrics-attribution-schema.json
- Related work: ENG-9026 (emit dims), ENG-9027 (aggregate rollups), ENG-9028 (guardrails/auto-stop)
- GitHub: PR-28503 (rollup table changes), PR-28622 (streaming retry attribution fix), PR-28741 (reduce metrics cardinality)
- Slack: sources/slack/eng-platform/1737924411-backfill-needed-after-schema-change.json

Problem statement
We changed attribution tagging mid-beta:
1) some requests before the change have no experiment_id/variant_id
2) some requests during the change window have partial attribution due to streaming retry paths (fixed later)
3) aggregation jobs now expect experiment dimensions and therefore drop / mis-group older rows

Without a backfill plan:
- Console results charts show a data cliff around the schema deploy
- Guardrail evaluation (p95/p99 latency / errors / cost) can be undercounted per-variant
- Support sees “results not updating” complaints (ties to SUP-24910)

Goals
- Populate experiment-aware rollups for a defined historical window so Console dashboards remain usable.
- Preserve cost controls: do not overwhelm OLAP cluster / warehouse or inflate query spend.
- Provide a repeatable procedure for future schema changes (documented in release checklist).

Non-goals
- Infinite history backfill. We’ll prioritize the windows needed for beta/GA dashboards.
- Retroactively “infer” variant assignment for traffic that predates deterministic assignment (no attempt to reconstruct).

Scope / datasets
We backfill only the experiment rollup tables used by Console:
- experiment_variant_request_rollup_{5m,1h,1d} keyed by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}
- experiment_variant_token_cost_rollup_{1h,1d} keyed by same dims
- attribution_drop_rate_rollup_{1h} keyed by {route_id, experiment_id, region}

Backfill window decision
- Primary: last 14 days (covers most beta experiments and provides enough runway for GA launch comparisons)
- Secondary (best effort / if cost allows): last 30 days for 1d rollups only
Rationale: beyond 30 days, most accounts won’t have active route experiments; storing + computing variant rollups has diminishing value and higher cost.

Approach
Option A: Reprocess raw request events into rollups for the time window
- Pros: simplest correctness model (source of truth = raw events)
- Cons: expensive if scanning large raw partitions; needs careful throttling

Option B: Backfill from existing non-experiment rollups
- Pros: cheaper if rollups already aggregated heavily
- Cons: cannot recover variant dimension; only helps for attribution-drop-rate style metrics

Decision: Hybrid
1) Use raw events reprocessing for experiment-aware rollups.
2) Use existing rollups for coarse “baseline” sanity checks (totals) and for drop-rate confirmation.

Implementation details
- Backfill job implemented as a parameterized batch pipeline (time-range + granularity) running per-day partitions.
- Uses partition pruning (date/hour) and strict predicate on presence of experiment_id to avoid scanning traffic that can’t contribute to variant rollups.
- Throttling knobs:
  - max_partitions_per_run (default 6 hours worth of partitions per job invocation)
  - max_concurrent_workers (default 4)
  - rate limiter on writes to rollup tables
  - optional “dry_run” mode to estimate scanned bytes / row counts before executing
- Idempotency:
  - rollup tables are written via “INSERT OVERWRITE partition” semantics for affected partitions
  - job records a watermark in a backfill_runs table keyed by {dataset, start_ts, end_ts, job_version}

Cost estimates (beta-scale)
Estimates based on staging/prod sampling during 2025-03-18..2025-03-20:
- Raw events volume: ~250–400M request-level rows/day across all tenants (varies with region)
- Rows with experiment_id present: currently ~3–8% of total during beta (feature-flagged)
- Expected scan reduction with experiment_id predicate + partition pruning: ~10–20x vs full scan
- Compute budget target: <= 6 hours wall-clock for 14-day backfill at 1h and 1d rollups, running off-peak
- Write amplification: bounded by number of active experiments * variants; enforce cap on variant_id cardinality (matches PR-28741 direction)

We will abort/slow down if any of the following exceed thresholds:
- OLAP CPU > 65% sustained for 15m (cluster-wide)
- Rollup write latency p95 > 2s sustained for 10m
- Attribution drop-rate rollup “unknown” bucket increases > 2x vs pre-backfill baseline

Throttling + scheduling
- Run in off-peak windows (02:00–08:00 UTC)
- Execute region by region to limit blast radius and simplify rollback:
  1) us-east
  2) us-west
  3) eu-west
- Use feature flag / config knob “enable_experiment_backfill_writes” so we can run read/compute first, then enable writes.

Validation plan
Data quality checks after each day partition backfilled:
1) Totals consistency:
   - Sum(request_count) across variants for a given {route_id, experiment_id, model_id, region, day} should be <= total request_count for same route/day in non-experiment rollup.
   - If greater, indicates double-attribution or join duplication.
2) Attribution integrity:
   - experiment_id present implies variant_id present (except explicit “unassigned” bucket for eligibility failures).
3) Split sanity:
   - For known 50/50 experiments, variant shares should be within 45–55% for large enough N (N >= 50k). Smaller N only warn.
4) Known-bad window handling:
   - For the period before PR-28622 (streaming retries fix), allow higher attribution_drop_rate but ensure it is correctly surfaced.

Roll-forward / rollback
- Roll-forward: job supports extending backfill window by changing parameters; safe to rerun due to overwrite semantics.
- Rollback: disable writes flag; restore affected partitions from snapshot (daily table snapshots exist) or rerun with previous job_version.

Open questions / follow-ups
- Do we want to annotate Console with “data completeness” banner for known-bad windows (pre PR-28622) rather than trying to backfill perfection? Suggested: yes; track separately.
- Long-term: build a lightweight backfill framework template for telemetry schema evolutions (not just experiments).

Progress updates
- 2025-03-11 (Mei Lin): drafted backfill plan + cost/throttling knobs; aligned with Logan on partition strategy.
- 2025-03-18 (Caleb Johnson): confirmed raw events include experiment_id/variant_id in request context after PR-28470; verified late arrival behavior in ingestion.
- 2025-03-21 (Nadia Rahman): validated cost/token fields needed for cost rollups; flagged that backfilling token economics requires joining token_usage spans for some SDK versions.
- 2025-03-26 (Mei Lin): implemented job + dry_run estimator; ran staging backfill for 48h window; results dashboard shows continuous series.
- 2025-03-28 (Sean Gallagher): approved off-peak schedule + cluster guardrails; requested “stop switch” and runbook note for on-call.
- 2025-04-03 (Mei Lin): ran prod backfill for us-east 7-day window; monitored CPU and write latency within budget; no cardinality blowups.
- 2025-04-08 (Mei Lin): completed 14-day backfill for all regions; added validation reports to shared channel; closing.

Links / artifacts
- Dashboards: Experiment Health → Attribution drop rate / rollup job lag (see dashboard spec in sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/experiment-health-dashboards-spec.json)
- Support tie-in: SUP-24910 "Console experiment results not updating" (expected to reduce occurrences)

Acceptance criteria
- [x] Backfill job can run for configurable time window and granularity and is idempotent.
- [x] Backfill completed for last 14 days for all GA regions.
- [x] Console results dashboard no longer has a metrics cliff around schema deploy (verified on at least 2 beta experiments).
- [x] Throttling/abort conditions documented and tested in at least one prod run.
- [x] Post-run validation report posted (totals consistency + split sanity + drop rate trend).

Notes
This work is intentionally constrained to Redwood-defined metrics + Redwood eval signals. Customer-managed custom metrics ingestion is out of scope for GA (per PRD).
text:
Context
We added experiment attribution dimensions to request telemetry per ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region). During beta we discovered that the Console experiment results dashboard depends on per-variant rollups; without a backfill, results for historical windows show as empty/partial (and comparisons are misleading).

This ticket plans + implements a backfill strategy for experiment metrics after the schema change, including:
- cost estimates
- throttling/guardrails
- correctness checks (attribution integrity)
- operational runbook notes

References
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-048-experiment-metrics-attribution-schema.json
- Related work: ENG-9026 (emit dims), ENG-9027 (aggregate rollups), ENG-9028 (guardrails/auto-stop)
- GitHub: PR-28503 (rollup table changes), PR-28622 (streaming retry attribution fix), PR-28741 (reduce metrics cardinality)
- Slack: sources/slack/eng-platform/1737924411-backfill-needed-after-schema-change.json

Problem statement
We changed attribution tagging mid-beta:
1) some requests before the change have no experiment_id/variant_id
2) some requests during the change window have partial attribution due to streaming retry paths (fixed later)
3) aggregation jobs now expect experiment dimensions and therefore drop / mis-group older rows

Without a backfill plan:
- Console results charts show a data cliff around the schema deploy
- Guardrail evaluation (p95/p99 latency / errors / cost) can be undercounted per-variant
- Support sees “results not updating” complaints (ties to SUP-24910)

Goals
- Populate experiment-aware rollups for a defined historical window so Console dashboards remain usable.
- Preserve cost controls: do not overwhelm OLAP cluster / warehouse or inflate query spend.
- Provide a repeatable procedure for future schema changes (documented in release checklist).

Non-goals
- Infinite history backfill. We’ll prioritize the windows needed for beta/GA dashboards.
- Retroactively “infer” variant assignment for traffic that predates deterministic assignment (no attempt to reconstruct).

Scope / datasets
We backfill only the experiment rollup tables used by Console:
- experiment_variant_request_rollup_{5m,1h,1d} keyed by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}
- experiment_variant_token_cost_rollup_{1h,1d} keyed by same dims
- attribution_drop_rate_rollup_{1h} keyed by {route_id, experiment_id, region}

Backfill window decision
- Primary: last 14 days (covers most beta experiments and provides enough runway for GA launch comparisons)
- Secondary (best effort / if cost allows): last 30 days for 1d rollups only
Rationale: beyond 30 days, most accounts won’t have active route experiments; storing + computing variant rollups has diminishing value and higher cost.

Approach
Option A: Reprocess raw request events into rollups for the time window
- Pros: simplest correctness model (source of truth = raw events)
- Cons: expensive if scanning large raw partitions; needs careful throttling

Option B: Backfill from existing non-experiment rollups
- Pros: cheaper if rollups already aggregated heavily
- Cons: cannot recover variant dimension; only helps for attribution-drop-rate style metrics

Decision: Hybrid
1) Use raw events reprocessing for experiment-aware rollups.
2) Use existing rollups for coarse “baseline” sanity checks (totals) and for drop-rate confirmation.

Implementation details
- Backfill job implemented as a parameterized batch pipeline (time-range + granularity) running per-day partitions.
- Uses partition pruning (date/hour) and strict predicate on presence of experiment_id to avoid scanning traffic that can’t contribute to variant rollups.
- Throttling knobs:
  - max_partitions_per_run (default 6 hours worth of partitions per job invocation)
  - max_concurrent_workers (default 4)
  - rate limiter on writes to rollup tables
  - optional “dry_run” mode to estimate scanned bytes / row counts before executing
- Idempotency:
  - rollup tables are written via “INSERT OVERWRITE partition” semantics for affected partitions
  - job records a watermark in a backfill_runs table keyed by {dataset, start_ts, end_ts, job_version}

Cost estimates (beta-scale)
Estimates based on staging/prod sampling during 2025-03-18..2025-03-20:
- Raw events volume: ~250–400M request-level rows/day across all tenants (varies with region)
- Rows with experiment_id present: currently ~3–8% of total during beta (feature-flagged)
- Expected scan reduction with experiment_id predicate + partition pruning: ~10–20x vs full scan
- Compute budget target: <= 6 hours wall-clock for 14-day backfill at 1h and 1d rollups, running off-peak
- Write amplification: bounded by number of active experiments * variants; enforce cap on variant_id cardinality (matches PR-28741 direction)

We will abort/slow down if any of the following exceed thresholds:
- OLAP CPU > 65% sustained for 15m (cluster-wide)
- Rollup write latency p95 > 2s sustained for 10m
- Attribution drop-rate rollup “unknown” bucket increases > 2x vs pre-backfill baseline

Throttling + scheduling
- Run in off-peak windows (02:00–08:00 UTC)
- Execute region by region to limit blast radius and simplify rollback:
  1) us-east
  2) us-west
  3) eu-west
- Use feature flag / config knob “enable_experiment_backfill_writes” so we can run read/compute first, then enable writes.

Validation plan
Data quality checks after each day partition backfilled:
1) Totals consistency:
   - Sum(request_count) across variants for a given {route_id, experiment_id, model_id, region, day} should be <= total request_count for same route/day in non-experiment rollup.
   - If greater, indicates double-attribution or join duplication.
2) Attribution integrity:
   - experiment_id present implies variant_id present (except explicit “unassigned” bucket for eligibility failures).
3) Split sanity:
   - For known 50/50 experiments, variant shares should be within 45–55% for large enough N (N >= 50k). Smaller N only warn.
4) Known-bad window handling:
   - For the period before PR-28622 (streaming retries fix), allow higher attribution_drop_rate but ensure it is correctly surfaced.

Roll-forward / rollback
- Roll-forward: job supports extending backfill window by changing parameters; safe to rerun due to overwrite semantics.
- Rollback: disable writes flag; restore affected partitions from snapshot (daily table snapshots exist) or rerun with previous job_version.

Open questions / follow-ups
- Do we want to annotate Console with “data completeness” banner for known-bad windows (pre PR-28622) rather than trying to backfill perfection? Suggested: yes; track separately.
- Long-term: build a lightweight backfill framework template for telemetry schema evolutions (not just experiments).

Progress updates
- 2025-03-11 (Mei Lin): drafted backfill plan + cost/throttling knobs; aligned with Logan on partition strategy.
- 2025-03-18 (Caleb Johnson): confirmed raw events include experiment_id/variant_id in request context after PR-28470; verified late arrival behavior in ingestion.
- 2025-03-21 (Nadia Rahman): validated cost/token fields needed for cost rollups; flagged that backfilling token economics requires joining token_usage spans for some SDK versions.
- 2025-03-26 (Mei Lin): implemented job + dry_run estimator; ran staging backfill for 48h window; results dashboard shows continuous series.
- 2025-03-28 (Sean Gallagher): approved off-peak schedule + cluster guardrails; requested “stop switch” and runbook note for on-call.
- 2025-04-03 (Mei Lin): ran prod backfill for us-east 7-day window; monitored CPU and write latency within budget; no cardinality blowups.
- 2025-04-08 (Mei Lin): completed 14-day backfill for all regions; added validation reports to shared channel; closing.

Links / artifacts
- Dashboards: Experiment Health → Attribution drop rate / rollup job lag (see dashboard spec in sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/experiment-health-dashboards-spec.json)
- Support tie-in: SUP-24910 "Console experiment results not updating" (expected to reduce occurrences)

Acceptance criteria
- [x] Backfill job can run for configurable time window and granularity and is idempotent.
- [x] Backfill completed for last 14 days for all GA regions.
- [x] Console results dashboard no longer has a metrics cliff around schema deploy (verified on at least 2 beta experiments).
- [x] Throttling/abort conditions documented and tested in at least one prod run.
- [x] Post-run validation report posted (totals consistency + split sanity + drop rate trend).

Notes
This work is intentionally constrained to Redwood-defined metrics + Redwood eval signals. Customer-managed custom metrics ingestion is out of scope for GA (per PRD).

title: Privacy review: onboarding checklist events are PII-free + retention/compliance aligned
preview:
Context
We are shipping onboarding/progress tracking via a standardized event schema emitted primarily server-side (gateway/control plane) to drive the Console checklist and TTFS/TTFP KPIs.

This ticket is the privacy/security validation pass to ensure:
- Event payloads are PII-free (and do not accidentally capture customer prompts, outputs, headers, or identifiers like emails).
- Retention + residency are aligned with Redwood’s compliance posture and the existing data residency/retention guidance for onboarding telemetry.
- Guardrails exist in code and schema to prevent future regressions.

In scope
- Review onboarding event schema v1 fields + examples used by gateway/control plane + OpenAI-compat layer.
- Validate current emission points and payload construction do not contain PII.
- Confirm retention policy for raw events vs derived checklist state vs aggregated KPIs.
- Confirm processing location / regional storage constraints for EU tenants.
- Confirm access controls (who can query raw events; how Support uses checklist state).

Out of scope
- Full DPIA for all telemetry across product (focus is onboarding/checklist events only).
- Private deployment customer-controlled telemetry pipeline (MVP is Hosted API cohorts).

Risk summary (why this matters)
- Onboarding events fire early in the lifecycle and could include user-controlled fields if we are not strict (e.g., error_message, user_agent, request metadata).
- Support workflows and admin override tooling may expose audit trails; must avoid storing sensitive details in “reason” fields.

Findings (review results)
1) Allowed identifiers
- OK: internal tenant identifiers (tenant_id / org_id), internal project/workspace id, environment (prod/sandbox), region, cohort id.
- OK with constraints: api_key_id as an internal UUID (NOT the key string). If callers currently only have a key fingerprint, that is acceptable as long as it is irreversible and length-capped.
- NOT allowed: customer emails, end-user ids, company name, billing contact, IP addresses, raw request headers, prompt/response text, function/tool arguments, or any free-form user-provided metadata.

2) High-risk fields identified and mitigation
- error_message: must NOT be included as a free-form string due to potential leakage of prompt fragments or user identifiers. Replace with enumerated error_code (e.g., AUTH_INVALID_KEY, RATE_LIMITED, UPSTREAM_TIMEOUT) and optional error_class.
- user_agent: permitted for funnel attribution only if normalized/truncated and not stored verbatim. Action: store sdk_name/sdk_version explicitly; store a normalized ua_family and cap length to 128 chars; drop anything beyond allowlist patterns.
- endpoint/path: must be stored as a canonical route key (e.g., /v1/chat.completions) and NOT full URL including query parameters.
- request_id: OK; must be Redwood-generated correlation id (not customer-provided) and treated as operational metadata.

3) Payload examples sampled
Reviewed sample payloads from:
- API gateway emission (first 2xx, first streaming established, first 429)
- Control plane (API key created, quota/budget configured)
- OpenAI-compat layer UA tagging

Result: no prompt/response content observed in sample payloads. The one exception found during review was an early implementation path that attached error_message from upstream as-is for a small set of 5xx/timeout cases.

Decision: block free-form error_message and ship with error_code only.

4) Retention + access control alignment
Proposed/confirmed retention tiers (matches onboarding telemetry expectations):
- Raw onboarding events dataset: 30 days retention (operational debugging window). Access limited to Telemetry/Infra on-call and security-approved analysts.
- Derived checklist progress state (per-tenant step state): retained while feature is enabled + 13 months for audit/CS context; contains only step status + timestamps (no request metadata).
- Aggregated KPIs (TTFS/TTFP, funnel metrics): 13 months retention for trend analysis; no per-request identifiers.

Data residency
- EU tenants: raw events and derived datasets must be stored/processed within EU analytics boundary when available; otherwise, onboarding checklist exposure remains gated by cohort rules (feature flag) until EU pipeline is verified.

5) Support/admin override considerations
- Admin override “reason” field must be constrained to an allowlist (e.g., CUSTOMER_REPORTED_BUG, BACKFILL_CORRECTION, INTERNAL_TEST) plus optional internal-only note capped to 256 chars and explicitly disallowing customer-provided text.
- Support-facing UI should display step completion info only (timestamps, step ids) and must not expose raw event payloads.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] Schema updated to remove/ban free-form error_message; replace with error_code + error_class.
- [x] Schema explicitly documents “PII disallowed” and contains a short allowlist/denylist section.
- [x] Ingestion enforces max lengths + drops unknown fields (no pass-through JSON blobs).
- [x] Gateway/control plane emission updated to conform (no upstream error strings).
- [x] Retention documented (raw 30d, derived 13mo, aggregates 13mo) and confirmed with Security.
- [x] EU residency constraint documented + tied to rollout gating.
- [x] Support/admin override audit trail avoids sensitive notes (allowlist + cap).

Implementation notes / guardrails
- Add validation at ingestion: reject events with disallowed keys; cap all string fields; enforce enums.
- Add a CI check for schema changes: "no new string fields without explicit classification" (operational vs identifier vs user-controlled) and a reviewer requirement for Security.
- Add a sampling job that periodically scans raw events for suspicious patterns (emails, credit card regex, long base64 blobs) and alerts #security-and-compliance.

Rollout implications
- Checklist feature flag should remain off for EU cohorts until EU analytics storage is confirmed (separate tracking ticket if needed).
- Backfill job must write derived/aggregate metrics only; should not attempt to reconstruct request-level event payloads.

Deliverables / artifacts
- Updated schema + docs.
- Sign-off note from Security.
- Follow-up tasks created for any non-blocking improvements.

tags: Guided onboarding + first production launch checklist

title: Build experiment metrics aggregations (latency/cost/error) for Console by {route, experiment, variant}
preview:
Goal
Ship the aggregation datasets/jobs that power the Console “Results” tab for route-level experiments. Console needs per-variant metrics (latency, cost, errors, throughput) with stable dimensions and defensible data quality checks.

Context
- Router/policy engine emits experiment attribution fields (experiment_id, variant_id) into request context and OTel spans/metrics (ENG-9026).
- Console will call a results API that returns deltas + guardrail status. This ticket builds the underlying rollups so results queries are cheap and consistent.
- Must follow canonical schema in ADR-048 (dimensions: route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region, plus customer/account identifiers at the appropriate layer).

Non-goals (this ticket)
- No full statistical significance engine; only compute aggregates + basic deltas. (PM explicitly out of scope for GA.)
- No customer-defined metrics ingestion.

Deliverables
1) Canonical rollup tables/views
- Base fact source: attributed request spans (one row per request attempt) and token/cost spans (one row per generation stream segment), as defined in ADR-048.
- Rollup grain:
  - Primary: 1-minute buckets by {account_id, route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}
  - Secondary: 15-minute and 1-hour materializations for cheaper default dashboards
- Metrics computed:
  - Latency: request_latency_ms p50/p95/p99 (based on end-to-end server timing); include count and sum for weighted merges
  - Errors: error_count, error_rate (2xx vs non-2xx); breakdown by error_class (timeout, capacity, upstream, validation) if available without exploding cardinality
  - Throughput: request_count, tokens_out_sum, tokens_in_sum
  - Cost: cost_usd_sum, cost_per_1k_tokens_out (derived), and optionally compute “effective $/req”

2) DQ checks + monitoring hooks (hard requirements for beta expansion)
- Attribution completeness:
  - % of requests with experiment_id present but variant_id missing
  - % of requests on an active experiment route that have no experiment attribution (aka attribution drop rate)
- Cardinality / dimension hygiene:
  - route_id normalization enforcement (template route id, not raw path)
  - limit model_id/region tagging to allowed sets; emit “unknown” bucket rather than creating unbounded values
- Consistency checks:
  - variant traffic distribution sanity per experiment (detect “variant 0 traffic” / 0% variants)
  - duplication: ensure request_id + attempt_id dedupe to avoid streaming retry double-count
- Freshness:
  - end-to-end lag from request end time to rollup availability (target < 5 minutes for 1m rollups in beta)

3) Query contract for Console results API
- Provide a stable interface (SQL view / dataset contract) consumed by the results API:
  - Inputs: account_id, experiment_id, time_range, optional route_id/model_id/region filters
  - Outputs: per-variant metrics + baseline variant reference and computed deltas
- Ensure indexes/partitioning to keep p95 query < 1s at typical cardinalities; include caching guidance for Console (e.g., 30–60s cache for active experiments).

Implementation notes
- Aggregation job should treat “attempts” vs “requests” carefully:
  - For retries, only count the final successful attempt for latency + cost when attempt_id indicates a retry chain (align with telemetry conventions from Caleb/Logan).
  - Errors should count all failed attempts (since that represents real user-visible failure risk), but avoid double-counting if router reports both a failure span and a parent request error span.
- Latency quantiles:
  - Use t-digest or equivalent in rollups to enable mergeable p50/p95/p99 across buckets.
  - Explicitly document whether latency includes queueing/batching time (it should: end-to-end server latency for guardrails).
- Cost attribution:
  - Use token-level cost fields already produced by cost pipeline; do not recompute pricing inside the rollup job.
  - Store raw sums and compute derived ratios in views.

Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (beta): 1-minute + 1-hour rollups; DQ checks emitted as internal metrics; results API uses 1-hour default and can drill down to 1-minute.
- Phase 2 (GA hardening): backfill job for schema change window; tighten null handling; add “late arriving data” correction pass for last N hours.

Acceptance criteria
- Console results API can retrieve per-variant p50/p95/p99 latency, cost_usd_sum, cost_per_1k_tokens_out, error_rate, request_count for a 24h range in < 1s P95 (internal staging dataset).
- Attribution drop rate is measurable and exposed to the standard dashboards; can detect the known failure mode “streaming retries missing variant tag” as a spike.
- Rollups respect ADR-048 dimensions and do not introduce unbounded tag cardinality.
- Dedupe logic prevents double-counting for streaming retry paths (validated against SUP-24877 repro traces).
- Basic “variant 0 traffic” detection is available to SRE dashboards/spec.

Risks / open questions
- Definition mismatches between ‘request latency’ in router spans vs API gateway spans; need to pick one authoritative source and align with Allison’s observability standards.
- Late arriving spans can bias short windows; decide if we re-open buckets for the last 10–15 minutes.
- Cost pipeline lag: if cost fields arrive later than latency/error, results UI may show partial cost for very recent windows; decide UI copy vs delaying results.

Deliverables
1) Canonical rollup tables/views
- Base fact source: attributed request spans (one row per request attempt) and token/cost spans (one row per generation stream segment), as defined in ADR-048.
- Rollup grain:
  - Primary: 1-minute buckets by {account_id, route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}
  - Secondary: 15-minute and 1-hour materializations for cheaper default dashboards
- Metrics computed:
  - Latency: request_latency_ms p50/p95/p99 (based on end-to-end server timing); include count and sum for weighted merges
  - Errors: error_count, error_rate (2xx vs non-2xx); breakdown by error_class (timeout, capacity, upstream, validation) if available without exploding cardinality
  - Throughput: request_count, tokens_out_sum, tokens_in_sum
  - Cost: cost_usd_sum, cost_per_1k_tokens_out (derived), and optionally compute “effective $/req”

2) DQ checks + monitoring hooks (hard requirements for beta expansion)
- Attribution completeness:
  - % of requests with experiment_id present but variant_id missing
  - % of requests on an active experiment route that have no experiment attribution (aka attribution drop rate)
- Cardinality / dimension hygiene:
  - route_id normalization enforcement (template route id, not raw path)
  - limit model_id/region tagging to allowed sets; emit “unknown” bucket rather than creating unbounded values
- Consistency checks:
  - variant traffic distribution sanity per experiment (detect “variant 0 traffic” / 0% variants)
  - duplication: ensure request_id + attempt_id dedupe to avoid streaming retry double-count
- Freshness:
  - end-to-end lag from request end time to rollup availability (target < 5 minutes for 1m rollups in beta)

3) Query contract for Console results API
- Provide a stable interface (SQL view / dataset contract) consumed by the results API:
  - Inputs: account_id, experiment_id, time_range, optional route_id/model_id/region filters
  - Outputs: per-variant metrics + baseline variant reference and computed deltas
- Ensure indexes/partitioning to keep p95 query < 1s at typical cardinalities; include caching guidance for Console (e.g., 30–60s cache for active experiments).

Implementation notes
- Aggregation job should treat “attempts” vs “requests” carefully:
  - For retries, only count the final successful attempt for latency + cost when attempt_id indicates a retry chain (align with telemetry conventions from Caleb/Logan).
  - Errors should count all failed attempts (since that represents real user-visible failure risk), but avoid double-counting if router reports both a failure span and a parent request error span.
- Latency quantiles:
  - Use t-digest or equivalent in rollups to enable mergeable p50/p95/p99 across buckets.
  - Explicitly document whether latency includes queueing/batching time (it should: end-to-end server latency for guardrails).
- Cost attribution:
  - Use token-level cost fields already produced by cost pipeline; do not recompute pricing inside the rollup job.
  - Store raw sums and compute derived ratios in views.

Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (beta): 1-minute + 1-hour rollups; DQ checks emitted as internal metrics; results API uses 1-hour default and can drill down to 1-minute.
- Phase 2 (GA hardening): backfill job for schema change window; tighten null handling; add “late arriving data” correction pass for last N hours.

Acceptance criteria
- Console results API can retrieve per-variant p50/p95/p99 latency, cost_usd_sum, cost_per_1k_tokens_out, error_rate, request_count for a 24h range in < 1s P95 (internal staging dataset).
- Attribution drop rate is measurable and exposed to the standard dashboards; can detect the known failure mode “streaming retries missing variant tag” as a spike.
- Rollups respect ADR-048 dimensions and do not introduce unbounded tag cardinality.
- Dedupe logic prevents double-counting for streaming retry paths (validated against SUP-24877 repro traces).
- Basic “variant 0 traffic” detection is available to SRE dashboards/spec.

Risks / open questions
- Definition mismatches between ‘request latency’ in router spans vs API gateway spans; need to pick one authoritative source and align with Allison’s observability standards.
- Late arriving spans can bias short windows; decide if we re-open buckets for the last 10–15 minutes.
- Cost pipeline lag: if cost fields arrive later than latency/error, results UI may show partial cost for very recent windows; decide UI copy vs delaying results.

title: Emit onboarding checklist events from API gateway at key lifecycle points
preview:
## Context
We are building a Console onboarding checklist backed by a progress-tracking service. To keep instrumentation consistent across SDKs and reduce client-side drift, the decision (ADR-2025-02) is to emit the core onboarding events server-side wherever possible.

This ticket implements server-side emission in the API Gateway for Hosted API traffic. These events will be ingested by the onboarding progress service (ENG-5813) and used for funnel reporting (ENG-5815) + checklist step state.

## Goals
- Emit reliable, privacy-safe onboarding events for the checklist funnel.
- Ensure events are idempotent and tolerant of retries / duplicate requests.
- Keep payload cardinality constrained per schema v1.
- Cover the most common early failures (401/403, 429, streaming disconnect confusion) with enough signals to drive checklist state and Support debugging.

## Non-goals (MVP)
- Full client-side instrumentation parity across every SDK action (handled under SDK workstreams).
- Emitting per-token/per-chunk streaming telemetry (too high volume).
- Automatic remediation suggestions (follow-on).

## Event schema / naming
Use `onboarding-event-schema-v1` (see link). All emitted events MUST:
- Include `schema_version=v1` and the canonical `event_name`.
- Avoid PII (no prompts, no raw completion text, no email/IP/user-provided identifiers).
- Use stable identifiers: `tenant_id`, `project_id` (if applicable), `api_key_id` (internal), and `request_id`.
- Include `source=server.gateway` and `emitted_at` (UTC).

## Events to emit (MVP)
Emit the following from the gateway / adjacent control-plane hooks:

1) `onboarding.api_key.created`
- Emission point: control plane API key creation path (may be owned by platform; if emitted outside gateway, still route through same ingestion client).
- Required fields: tenant_id, api_key_id, key_type (prod/test), created_via (console/api).
- Notes: this is a top checklist step; ensure emitted once per key_id.

2) `onboarding.request.first_success`
- Definition: first request resulting in 2xx for a tenant (Hosted API).
- Emission point: gateway response middleware on any 2xx for eligible endpoints (chat/completions, embeddings, rerank; exclude health checks).
- Required fields: tenant_id, request_id, route, http_status, model (if resolved), is_streaming=false.
- Idempotency: dedupe using (tenant_id, event_name) at ingestion layer; gateway should still send best-effort.

3) `onboarding.request.first_stream_established`
- Definition: first streaming response where the stream is successfully established (headers flushed + first chunk sent OR explicit “stream started” hook, depending on implementation).
- Emission point: gateway streaming handler.
- Required fields: tenant_id, request_id, route, model (if resolved), transport (sse/websocket if applicable), is_streaming=true.
- Notes: do NOT emit per chunk; single event only.

4) `onboarding.request.first_rate_limited`
- Definition: first request for a tenant that receives HTTP 429 from gateway quota/rate-limit enforcement.
- Emission point: gateway rate-limit response path.
- Required fields: tenant_id, request_id, route, http_status=429, limit_type (rpm/tpm/concurrent), retry_after_present (true/false).
- Purpose: drive checklist step for “Understand quotas/budgets + implement backoff” and support analytics.

5) `onboarding.request.first_auth_error`
- Definition: first request for a tenant that receives 401/403 due to invalid/missing API key.
- Emission point: gateway auth failure handler.
- Required fields: tenant_id (if resolvable; otherwise emit with `tenant_id=unknown` and `api_key_hash_prefix` per schema guidance), request_id, http_status, auth_error_type (missing/invalid/revoked).
- Privacy: do not log full API key; only allowed safe fingerprint per schema if needed for debugging.

## Eligibility / cohorting
- Emit events regardless of feature flag exposure (flag gates UI/rollout; event data is needed for baseline + backfill validation).
- Only emit for Hosted API traffic (Dedicated/Private handled later).
- Exclude internal synthetic traffic and load tests where possible via existing `user_agent` allow/deny list and internal tenant markers.

## Idempotency + ordering
- Gateway emits are at-least-once. Consumers MUST treat them as such.
- Include `idempotency_key` where schema supports it. Proposed:
  - `onboarding.request.*` => use gateway `request_id`
  - `onboarding.api_key.created` => use `api_key_id`
- Ordering is not guaranteed across event types. In particular, `first_auth_error` may precede `api_key.created` for a given tenant.
- Coordinate with ENG-5813 to ensure the progress service uses monotonic transitions per step (see ENG-5866 follow-up).

## Failure modes / durability expectations
- Emission should be non-blocking for request path. If telemetry is degraded, gateway must continue serving traffic.
- Use bounded queue/buffer and drop-with-metric if overloaded.
- Emit internal metrics:
  - `onboarding_events_emitted_total{event_name}`
  - `onboarding_events_emit_fail_total{reason}`
  - `onboarding_events_queue_dropped_total`
  - Sampling is NOT enabled for MVP (event volume expected low); revisit if needed.

## Implementation notes
- Add a small shared helper in gateway for constructing events (to avoid divergence across handlers).
- Ensure the model field is only included if already resolved by routing (no extra lookup on request path).
- Ensure route normalization matches dashboard spec (ENG-5815) to avoid high cardinality (e.g., `/v1/chat/completions` not full URL with query params).

## Testing plan
- Unit tests for:
  - 2xx path emits `first_success` once (simulate dedupe key generation).
  - Streaming handler emits `first_stream_established` once.
  - 429 handler emits `first_rate_limited` with correct limit_type mapping.
  - 401/403 handler emits `first_auth_error` with safe payload (no key material).
- Integration / staging:
  - Use a test tenant to walk the checklist flow; validate events arrive in ingestion topic and appear in KPI dataset within expected freshness.
  - Confirm no PII in payload by log sampling (coordinate with ENG-5871).

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Gateway emits the 5 MVP events above with schema v1-compliant payloads.
- [x] No request-path blocking; emission failures do not affect API responses.
- [x] Payloads are PII-safe (no prompts/completions, no raw API keys).
- [x] Events can be ingested by the progress service and used to drive step completion (validated with ENG-5813 in staging).
- [x] Basic emitted/failed/dropped metrics are available in gateway metrics.

## Rollout
- Ship behind server config toggle `onboarding_eventing_enabled` default ON in staging, OFF in prod for 24h.
- Enable in prod for 5% tenants (randomized) for 48h; validate event volumes + data quality; then ramp to 100%.
- Coordinate timing with Console dogfood and KPI dashboard readiness.

## Notes for Support/PM
These events are intended to reduce top onboarding tickets by surfacing likely issues in checklist step state and by allowing Support to quickly identify if a customer is stuck on auth, rate limits, or never established streaming.

Owner: Amara Diallo (Gateway)
Reviewer(s): Logan Wright (Telemetry), Ethan Park (Control plane), Marcus Lin (Security)

title: Telemetry pipeline: stage-aware metrics + traces for Dedicated staged rollouts (cardinality-safe)
preview:
Goal
Emit stage-tagged metrics and traces so gate evaluation + dashboards can attribute SLO signals to a *specific rollout stage* for Dedicated pools, without blowing up cardinality.

Context
Dedicated staged rollouts introduce traffic/replica partitioning by stage (e.g., 1%/10%/50%/100%). SRE/Operators need to answer:
- Are errors/latency/saturation worse in the candidate stage partition?
- Did promotion change anything (and when)?
- Are we missing telemetry and should gates be held?

This ticket covers:
- Adding rollout/stage context to request metrics + traces across routing -> data plane.
- Cardinality guardrails (tag allowlist, per-metric tag policy, sampling).
- Making data consumable by stage dashboards + gate queries.

Non-goals
- Customer-defined arbitrary metrics for gating (v1 uses Redwood-defined queries).
- Full experiment analytics (conversion, etc.).

Proposed tagging model (v1)
We need consistent keys across metrics and traces. Prefix with `rw.` to avoid collisions.

Required dimensions (always present when a request is routed via a rollout plan)
- rw.rollout_id: UUID/string identifier of DedicatedPoolRolloutPlan execution
- rw.stage_id: stable stage identifier (e.g., "s1", "s2", ...) NOT a freeform name
- rw.variant: one of ["baseline", "candidate"]

Optional / controlled dimensions
- rw.pool_id: Dedicated pool identifier (HIGH cardinality; see policy below)
- rw.region: region code (e.g., "us-east-1")
- model: served model name/version (already present on some metrics; verify)

Tag policy / cardinality guardrails
1) Metrics MUST NOT include arbitrary string tags.
2) `rw.pool_id` is ONLY allowed on a small allowlist of metrics used for gate evaluation and the rollout stage dashboards. Everything else should aggregate at `pool_tier`/`account_tier` (if needed) or omit pool_id.
3) `rw.rollout_id` is allowed on traces/spans and on a minimal subset of metrics. (Rollout IDs can be high cardinality over time; metrics retention makes this expensive.)
4) `rw.stage_id` is limited by rollout plan validation (max stages enforced in control plane). Stage IDs are bounded per rollout.
5) Sampling:
   - Traces: sample at 1–5% baseline; increase to 10–20% for candidate partitions during rollout (feature-flagged) to improve debugging.
   - High-frequency metrics with pool_id: consider downsampling or converting to distribution/hist with exemplars rather than counters with many labels.

Implementation plan
A) Propagate stage context to data plane
- Routing layer already selects partition by stage (ENG-5950). We need a canonical way to attach context:
  - Add internal header(s) from router -> inference runtime (e.g., `x-rw-rollout-id`, `x-rw-stage-id`, `x-rw-variant`) OR attach via request context metadata if using gRPC.
  - Ensure context is present for streaming requests as well (span should keep attributes for the full stream).

B) Emit stage-aware metrics
Add `rw.stage_id` + `rw.variant` to the following metrics (initial allowlist):
- request_total
- request_error_total (by class: 5xx, 429, timeout)
- request_latency_ms (hist/distribution)
- queue_depth (if emitted per pool)
- oom_total (or oom_rate)
- gpu_saturation/headroom (only if already produced in telemetry stream for Dedicated)

Where possible:
- Prefer `rw.stage_id` and `rw.variant` for dashboards.
- Avoid `rw.rollout_id` on metrics unless absolutely needed; use traces and annotations for per-rollout debugging.

C) Emit stage-aware traces
- Add span attributes on the server span (and any relevant child spans):
  - rw.rollout_id, rw.stage_id, rw.variant, rw.pool_id (if available)
- Add log/trace event “rollout_promotion” in control plane? (Out of scope for this ticket, but we should be compatible with Console annotations.)

D) Dashboard + gate query compatibility
- Ensure metrics naming and labels line up with planned dashboards/alerts templates (observability-pack PR will consume these).
- Provide a mapping doc in the ticket comments (which metric powers which gate panel).

E) Rollout plan / feature flagging
- Gate new labels behind `dedicated_rollouts_stage_tags_v1` feature flag.
- Roll out in 3 steps:
  1) Traces only (low risk)
  2) Metrics for error/latency only
  3) Add saturation/oom/queue depth metrics with pool_id tags

Acceptance criteria
- Stage dashboards can break down p95/p99 latency + error rate by stage + variant for a single Dedicated pool.
- Policy engine gate evaluator (ENG-5941) can query metrics using stage tags without needing per-request correlation.
- Cardinality stays within budget:
  - No >20% increase in total active series in the “dedicated-serving” Prometheus cluster after full rollout.
  - `rw.rollout_id` does not appear on any high-volume metric streams by default.
- Missing/late metrics are detectable:
  - Add a “telemetry freshness” metric or queryable signal so gates can enter UNKNOWN/HOLD with an operator-friendly reason.

Testing plan
- Unit tests: tag allowlist enforcement (attempt to attach pool_id/rollout_id to non-allowlisted metrics fails/strips).
- Integration tests (ENG-5995 will cover E2E): simulate a rollout with two stages; verify metrics have correct labels.
- Load test in staging: validate series cardinality and ingestion CPU; ensure no query timeouts on stage dashboards.

Risks / open questions
- Do we need `rw.rollout_id` on metrics at all? Current recommendation: no; rely on annotations and traces. If SRE insists, limit to low-rate “gate_eval_result” metric only.
- Pool identifiers: confirm whether `pool_id` is safe to include in metrics by default for enterprise customers (data sensitivity). If not, use opaque internal ID only.
- Streaming: confirm whether we pin stage/variant for the life of stream (expected) and avoid partial relabeling if a rollback happens mid-stream.

Links
- Design spec: sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/staged-rollouts-for-dedicated-pools-design-spec.json
- Dashboard wireframe: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/dedicated-rollout-stage-dashboard-wireframe.json
- Gate query drafts: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/platform-engineering/gate-evaluation-query-drafts.json

Progress updates
2025-02-06 (Logan)
- Aligned with Allison Grant on tag schema: `rw.stage_id` + `rw.variant` are required; `rw.rollout_id` is traces-first.
- Called out series risk: pool_id * stage_id * variant * region * model can multiply quickly; plan is allowlist.

2025-02-12 (Caleb)
- Implemented plumbing to attach rollout context from router to data plane via internal headers.
- Added stage tags to request latency + error counters in staging behind flag.
- Found mismatch in region label casing between router and runtime metrics; normalized to lower-case region codes.

2025-02-18 (Sergio / Observability)
- Confirmed dashboards will primarily filter by pool_id + stage_id; rollout_id optional.
- Asked for a single “stage” dimension name across metrics/traces (no mix of `stage` vs `stage_id`).

2025-02-26 (Caleb)
- Added tag allowlist + strip behavior; attempts to add disallowed tags now emit a WARN log with metric name.
- Added telemetry freshness gauge `rw.telemetry.last_seen_seconds` per (pool_id, region) to detect staleness.

2025-03-03 (Logan)
- Early staging load test showed ~35% series increase due to `rw.pool_id` on a few high-frequency metrics (request_total and token counters).
- Decision: remove pool_id from token-level metrics and keep it only on request-level SLO metrics + a small set of capacity signals.

2025-03-10 (Caleb)
- Follow-up load test: series increase down to ~12% in staging. Query latency on stage dashboard panels stays <2s.
- Prepared rollout plan: traces-first in beta accounts, then metrics for two internal Dedicated pools.

2025-03-14 (Caleb)
- Shipped behind flag to beta pools. Monitored ingestion + series count; no anomalies.

2025-03-18 (Vanessa / Fleet)
- Requested GPU health signal be stage-breakdown capable. Added stage tagging to “gpu_unhealthy_node_count” but WITHOUT rollout_id.

Completion / shipped artifacts
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-28521-emit-stage-tags-in-metrics-and-traces.json
- Hotfix PR (cardinality regression): sources/github/redwood/pr-28644-fix-stage-metric-cardinality-regression.json

Post-ship follow-ups
- Add automated cardinality budget alert (owner: Telemetry) (tracked in new issue; not blocking this ticket).
- Align on staleness handling UX in Console (UNKNOWN gate reason copy) with Console team.

A) Propagate stage context to data plane
- Routing layer already selects partition by stage (ENG-5950). We need a canonical way to attach context:
  - Add internal header(s) from router -> inference runtime (e.g., `x-rw-rollout-id`, `x-rw-stage-id`, `x-rw-variant`) OR attach via request context metadata if using gRPC.
  - Ensure context is present for streaming requests as well (span should keep attributes for the full stream).

B) Emit stage-aware metrics
Add `rw.stage_id` + `rw.variant` to the following metrics (initial allowlist):
- request_total
- request_error_total (by class: 5xx, 429, timeout)
- request_latency_ms (hist/distribution)
- queue_depth (if emitted per pool)
- oom_total (or oom_rate)
- gpu_saturation/headroom (only if already produced in telemetry stream for Dedicated)

Where possible:
- Prefer `rw.stage_id` and `rw.variant` for dashboards.
- Avoid `rw.rollout_id` on metrics unless absolutely needed; use traces and annotations for per-rollout debugging.

C) Emit stage-aware traces
- Add span attributes on the server span (and any relevant child spans):
  - rw.rollout_id, rw.stage_id, rw.variant, rw.pool_id (if available)
- Add log/trace event “rollout_promotion” in control plane? (Out of scope for this ticket, but we should be compatible with Console annotations.)

D) Dashboard + gate query compatibility
- Ensure metrics naming and labels line up with planned dashboards/alerts templates (observability-pack PR will consume these).
- Provide a mapping doc in the ticket comments (which metric powers which gate panel).

E) Rollout plan / feature flagging
- Gate new labels behind `dedicated_rollouts_stage_tags_v1` feature flag.
- Roll out in 3 steps:
  1) Traces only (low risk)
  2) Metrics for error/latency only
  3) Add saturation/oom/queue depth metrics with pool_id tags

Acceptance criteria
- Stage dashboards can break down p95/p99 latency + error rate by stage + variant for a single Dedicated pool.
- Policy engine gate evaluator (ENG-5941) can query metrics using stage tags without needing per-request correlation.
- Cardinality stays within budget:
  - No >20% increase in total active series in the “dedicated-serving” Prometheus cluster after full rollout.
  - `rw.rollout_id` does not appear on any high-volume metric streams by default.
- Missing/late metrics are detectable:
  - Add a “telemetry freshness” metric or queryable signal so gates can enter UNKNOWN/HOLD with an operator-friendly reason.

Testing plan
- Unit tests: tag allowlist enforcement (attempt to attach pool_id/rollout_id to non-allowlisted metrics fails/strips).
- Integration tests (ENG-5995 will cover E2E): simulate a rollout with two stages; verify metrics have correct labels.
- Load test in staging: validate series cardinality and ingestion CPU; ensure no query timeouts on stage dashboards.

Risks / open questions
- Do we need `rw.rollout_id` on metrics at all? Current recommendation: no; rely on annotations and traces. If SRE insists, limit to low-rate “gate_eval_result” metric only.
- Pool identifiers: confirm whether `pool_id` is safe to include in metrics by default for enterprise customers (data sensitivity). If not, use opaque internal ID only.
- Streaming: confirm whether we pin stage/variant for the life of stream (expected) and avoid partial relabeling if a rollback happens mid-stream.

Links
- Design spec: sources/confluence/product-docs/design-specs/staged-rollouts-for-dedicated-pools-design-spec.json
- Dashboard wireframe: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/dedicated-rollout-stage-dashboard-wireframe.json
- Gate query drafts: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/platform-engineering/gate-evaluation-query-drafts.json

Progress updates
2025-02-06 (Logan)
- Aligned with Allison Grant on tag schema: `rw.stage_id` + `rw.variant` are required; `rw.rollout_id` is traces-first.
- Called out series risk: pool_id * stage_id * variant * region * model can multiply quickly; plan is allowlist.

2025-02-12 (Caleb)
- Implemented plumbing to attach rollout context from router to data plane via internal headers.
- Added stage tags to request latency + error counters in staging behind flag.
- Found mismatch in region label casing between router and runtime metrics; normalized to lower-case region codes.

2025-02-18 (Sergio / Observability)
- Confirmed dashboards will primarily filter by pool_id + stage_id; rollout_id optional.
- Asked for a single “stage” dimension name across metrics/traces (no mix of `stage` vs `stage_id`).

2025-02-26 (Caleb)
- Added tag allowlist + strip behavior; attempts to add disallowed tags now emit a WARN log with metric name.
- Added telemetry freshness gauge `rw.telemetry.last_seen_seconds` per (pool_id, region) to detect staleness.

2025-03-03 (Logan)
- Early staging load test showed ~35% series increase due to `rw.pool_id` on a few high-frequency metrics (request_total and token counters).
- Decision: remove pool_id from token-level metrics and keep it only on request-level SLO metrics + a small set of capacity signals.

2025-03-10 (Caleb)
- Follow-up load test: series increase down to ~12% in staging. Query latency on stage dashboard panels stays <2s.
- Prepared rollout plan: traces-first in beta accounts, then metrics for two internal Dedicated pools.

2025-03-14 (Caleb)
- Shipped behind flag to beta pools. Monitored ingestion + series count; no anomalies.

2025-03-18 (Vanessa / Fleet)
- Requested GPU health signal be stage-breakdown capable. Added stage tagging to “gpu_unhealthy_node_count” but WITHOUT rollout_id.

Completion / shipped artifacts
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-28521-emit-stage-tags-in-metrics-and-traces.json
- Hotfix PR (cardinality regression): sources/github/redwood/pr-28644-fix-stage-metric-cardinality-regression.json

Post-ship follow-ups
- Add automated cardinality budget alert (owner: Telemetry) (tracked in new issue; not blocking this ticket).
- Align on staleness handling UX in Console (UNKNOWN gate reason copy) with Console team.
tags: Dedicated staged rollouts for reserved pools

title: Cache recommendations batch job MVP (per-route hit-rate recommendations)
preview:
Goal
Build the MVP batch job that aggregates prefix/KV cache telemetry into per-route recommendations (internal-first; Console stub can read from the output table). This ticket covers: aggregation logic, output schema, backtesting approach, and basic monitoring/guardrails.

Why
We can ship new cache telemetry, but it’s not yet actionable for operators/customers. We need a minimal pipeline that turns: hit-rate + miss reasons + eviction/pressure + wasted prefill into concrete levers (cache knobs, routing locality, canonicalization, rollout/version pinning).

Scope (MVP)
- Nightly batch job (24h lookback; optionally 7d for context) producing a small set of recommendations per {deployment_mode, region, route_id, model_version_id}.
- Input: hourly aggregates derived from the new runtime metrics + gateway tags (no raw prompts; only buckets).
- Output: analytics table (partitioned by day) with a stable schema that can be consumed by:
  1) internal SRE/Platform report (SQL / notebook)
  2) Console stub endpoint (read-only)
- Backtesting: offline evaluation using known incidents + historical windows; report precision/false-positive notes; define safe-ship guardrails.

Non-goals
- Real-time recommendations (<5m latency).
- Fully productized UX (ranking, personalization, customer copy polishing).
- Any recommendation requiring new cache eviction algorithms (out of sprint).

Inputs (assumptions)
- Telemetry pipeline produces hourly aggregates with required join keys per the cardinality policy:
  - route_id (stable ID, not free-form path)
  - tenant_tier (for internal triage; NOT tenant_id in metrics)
  - model_id + model_version_id
  - deployment_mode (hosted | dedicated | private)
  - region
  - prompt_length_bucket (e.g., 0-256, 257-1k, 1k-4k, 4k+)
  - metrics: prefix_hit_rate, kv_hit_rate, miss_reason breakdown, eviction_count, memory_pressure gauge stats, wasted_prefill_tokens

Data privacy
- No prompt text, no prompt hashes, no request IDs in the batch output.
- Route identifiers must be the allowlisted internal route_id (not arbitrary customer-supplied strings).
- Tenant exposure: output includes tenant_id ONLY in the dedicated/private “internal operator view” dataset. Customer-facing Console reads a tenant-scoped view and never returns other tenants’ data.

Output schema (v1)
Stored in analytics warehouse table: analytics.cache_recommendations_v1 (partition: generated_date; clustering: deployment_mode, region, route_id)

Each row = 1 recommendation for a specific slice, generated for a lookback window.

Fields (v1)
- recommendation_id (string; deterministic hash of {type, route_id, model_version_id, region, deployment_mode, window_start})
- generated_at (RFC3339)
- window_start (RFC3339)
- window_end (RFC3339)
- deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated|private)
- region (e.g., us-east-1)
- route_id (string)
- route_name (string; optional, allowlisted human name)
- model_id (string)
- model_version_id (string)
- recommendation_type (enum string):
  - INCREASE_PREFIX_CACHE_BUDGET
  - ENABLE_PREFIX_CACHE_ON_ROUTE
  - ADJUST_CACHE_TTL
  - FIX_VERSION_MISMATCH_ROLLOUT
  - IMPROVE_PROMPT_CANONICALIZATION
  - IMPROVE_ROUTING_LOCALITY
  - INVESTIGATE_EVICTION_PRESSURE
- severity (info|warning|critical)
- confidence (0.0-1.0 as stringified float)
- summary (1-2 sentence)
- evidence (short, human-readable string including top metrics + deltas)
- primary_metrics (stringified JSON for convenience; MUST remain low-cardinality because it’s stored, not emitted as metrics)
- suggested_actions (string; markdown-ish bullets)
- config_keys (comma-separated; e.g., max_prefix_cache_bytes,prefix_cache_ttl_seconds,route.cache.enabled)
- runbook_link (string; can be empty)
- dashboard_link (string; can be empty)
- owner_team (runtime|platform|sre|customer)
- audience (internal|customer)
- suppressed_reason (string; empty if not suppressed)

Example (single row; simplified)
{
  "recommendation_id": "rec_7b1d...",
  "generated_at": "2025-03-20T08:10:00Z",
  "window_start": "2025-03-19T00:00:00Z",
  "window_end": "2025-03-20T00:00:00Z",
  "deployment_mode": "dedicated",
  "region": "eu-west-1",
  "route_id": "route_42kR...",
  "model_id": "llama-3.1-70b-instruct",
  "model_version_id": "mv_2025_03_17_a",
  "recommendation_type": "FIX_VERSION_MISMATCH_ROLLOUT",
  "severity": "critical",
  "confidence": "0.86",
  "summary": "Cache misses are dominated by version mismatches after the 03/17 rollout; pin or extend warmup overlap.",
  "evidence": "kv_hit_rate=0.11 (down 0.29 WoW), miss_reason.version_mismatch=62%, wasted_prefill_tokens=18.4M",
  "suggested_actions": "- Increase overlap during rollout (keep previous mv live for >= 30m)
- Consider pinning route to mv_2025_03_17_a until hit-rate stabilizes
- Validate gateway model_version tag joinability in eu-west-1",
  "owner_team": "platform",
  "audience": "internal"
}

Recommendation heuristics (MVP)
We keep templates simple and conservative to avoid spamming operators/customers.

1) FIX_VERSION_MISMATCH_ROLLOUT
- Trigger: miss_reason.version_mismatch share > 30% AND kv_hit_rate drop > 0.15 vs trailing 7d baseline
- Severity: critical if share > 50% or cost impact estimate high
- Suggested action: extend overlap window, pin model_version for route, verify gateway tags.

2) INVESTIGATE_EVICTION_PRESSURE
- Trigger: memory_pressure p95 > threshold OR eviction_rate up > 2x baseline AND wasted_prefill_tokens up
- Suggested action: check cache budget, capacity, continuous batching pressure, SRE dashboard.

3) INCREASE_PREFIX_CACHE_BUDGET / ADJUST_CACHE_TTL
- Trigger: prefix_hit_rate low (< 0.25) but reuse_depth histogram indicates repeated prefixes AND evictions/ttl_expired dominate misses
- Suggested action: increase max_prefix_cache_bytes for route; adjust ttl; validate impact on memory.

4) IMPROVE_PROMPT_CANONICALIZATION
- Trigger: miss_reason.hash_mismatch high (> 25%) AND prompt_length_bucket distribution stable
- Suggested action: canonicalize dynamic fields; remove timestamps/IDs; normalize whitespace/tool ordering.

5) IMPROVE_ROUTING_LOCALITY
- Trigger: multi-region traffic for a route AND hit-rate significantly higher in one region OR miss_reason.capacity correlated with cross-region spill
- Suggested action: route affinity policy; avoid round-robin across independent caches; revisit failover settings.

Guardrails / suppression
- Suppress if input data quality fails (missing route_id/model_version_id for >5% of rows in slice) to avoid garbage output.
- Suppress for routes below minimum volume (prefill_tokens < N over window) to avoid noise.
- Suppress if metric pipeline drop is detected (see SRE pipeline drop detection thread) for the relevant region.

Backtesting approach (MVP)
We are not doing causal inference. We are validating that templates catch known classes of regressions and do not fire constantly under normal diurnal mix.

A) Historical replay
- For last 14 days: run job daily against prior windows and count recommendation frequency by type.
- Review top 20 “critical” outputs manually with SRE (Allison/Sean) and Runtime (Omar/Samir).

B) Incident-driven validation
- Use known tickets as anchors:
  - SUP-3912 (version mismatch after model update): expect FIX_VERSION_MISMATCH_ROLLOUT
  - SUP-3920 (streaming chat + canonicalization): expect IMPROVE_PROMPT_CANONICALIZATION and/or ENABLE_PREFIX_CACHE_ON_ROUTE
- Compare the job output on those days/regions against the human RCA.

C) False positive controls
- Measure firing rate per 100 routes/day; target < 5 recommendations/100 routes/day for customer audience.
- Require confidence >= 0.65 for customer audience in MVP; internal can include lower confidence but marked info.

Operational / monitoring
- Emit job metrics:
  - cache_reco_job.success (counter)
  - cache_reco_job.duration_seconds (hist)
  - cache_reco_job.input_rows, output_rows
  - cache_reco_job.suppressed_slices
- Data quality checks (fail job or mark degraded):
  - window completeness (24 hourly partitions present)
  - required dimensions non-null
  - sanity: hit rates in [0,1]; miss_reason shares sum ~1.0 (+/- epsilon)

Deliverables
- Implementation merged in platform repo (job + SQL transforms + schema migration).
- Table created + permissions:
  - internal dataset: analytics_internal.cache_recommendations_v1
  - customer-scoped view: analytics_customer.cache_recommendations_v1 (row-level security by tenant)
- Short internal doc in Confluence: “How to read recommendations output” + links to dashboards/runbooks.

Open questions (resolved in comments)
- Whether to include tenant_id in the base table (answer: only internal dataset; customer view is tenant-scoped).
- How to represent “evidence” without nested structures (answer: short string + primary_metrics as stringified JSON).
- Baseline window for deltas (answer: trailing 7d, excluding last 12h to avoid partial ingest).

Acceptance criteria
- Nightly job runs end-to-end in staging and prod for 3 consecutive days.
- Output table populated with non-zero rows and stable schema.
- At least two recommendation types validated against known incidents (SUP-3912 and one eviction-pressure case).
- SRE sign-off that firing rate is reasonable and suppression works when input data is missing.
- Privacy review sign-off (no prompt content / no cross-tenant leakage).
