title: Emit route experiment attribution dimensions in OTel traces + metrics (incl. streaming + retries)
preview:
## Summary
We need consistent experiment attribution across traces and key metrics so Console can slice latency/cost/error by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id} with low overhead and without blowing up cardinality.

This ticket covers:
- Emitting experiment attribution fields in OTel spans (request + model execution spans)
- Emitting experiment dimensions on a minimal set of metrics (latency, errors, throughput) and ensuring cost attribution pipeline can join on the same identifiers
- Correctness for streaming, retries, and internal fallbacks
- Guardrails against high-cardinality tags / accidental per-request explosion

## Motivation / Why now
Route-level experiments are gated on trustworthy per-variant metrics. During Phase 1 integration testing we observed attribution drop in staging (esp. streaming + retry paths) and early concerns about cardinality/overhead when tagging OTel + metrics. This is a GA blocker because Console results depend on these dimensions.

## Requirements
### Canonical dimensions (per ADR-048)
- route_id (normalized template; not raw path)
- experiment_id
- variant_id
- model_id (already present in many places; ensure consistent naming)
- region (already present)

### Where to emit
1) Traces (OTel spans)
- Always set on the top-level request span and propagate into child spans where relevant.
- Must be present on:
  - router decision span (or equivalent)
  - inference execution span (for latency breakdown)
  - streaming send loop span(s) or annotate on the parent request span (avoid per-chunk tagging).

2) Metrics
- Tag a minimal, curated set of metrics with {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id}:
  - request_latency_ms (histogram)
  - request_error_total (counter)
  - request_success_total (counter)
  - request_throughput_rps (derived)
- For cost: do NOT tag per-token metrics with experiment dimensions at full fidelity if it will explode. Prefer joining in analytics using request_id -> experiment dims, or sampled expansions (see "Cardinality strategy").

### Correctness constraints
- Deterministic assignment: the experiment_id/variant_id must match the router decision for the request.
- Retries: experiment dimensions must persist across retry attempts (including streaming retries) so we don’t attribute the retry attempt to "no experiment".
- Fallbacks: when a variant triggers fallback model selection, experiment_id/variant_id should remain the assigned values (the experiment is still the policy that chose the initial variant). Model_id may differ due to fallback.
- Missing stickiness keys: if a request is bucketed via default key, we still emit attribution; however we must surface warnings elsewhere (Console validation / debug endpoint). This ticket focuses on emission.

### Performance constraints
- Additional span attributes must be constant-time and avoid allocations in hot paths.
- Any string formatting must be avoided in the request loop; use stable identifiers already present in request context.
- Avoid tagging high-volume metrics at per-token granularity with experiment dims.

## Cardinality strategy (must-have)
- Limit experiment tagging to a curated list of metrics, not all.
- Ensure route_id is normalized to route template / internal route identifier (not raw request path).
- Add safeguards:
  - max length limits for experiment_id/variant_id in telemetry fields
  - allowlist emission behind a feature flag during beta (route-experiments)
  - sampling for any detailed per-span child enrichment (if we decide to annotate additional spans)

## Implementation plan
1) Plumb experiment context
- Depend on request context plumbing work (see PR 28470).
- Router sets experiment_id/variant_id and stores them in request context.

2) OTel span attributes
- For request span: set attributes once, early.
- For child spans: propagate via context; do not re-compute.

3) Metrics tagging
- Tag only request-level metrics with experiment dims.
- Ensure streaming response metrics are attributed to the same request context.

4) Edge cases
- Streaming retries: ensure context survives into retry pipeline (fix expected; see follow-up PR referenced below).
- Async spans: ensure we don’t lose attributes when spans are created after request context is moved across goroutines/threads.

5) Validation
- Add tests:
  - unit test: for a request assigned to variant A, all expected spans include experiment_id/variant_id
  - integration test: streaming request with retry emits consistent experiment attribution
- Add staging smoke test dashboard: attribution drop rate and per-variant request count sanity.

## Acceptance criteria
- p95 attribution drop rate (missing experiment_id OR variant_id when experiment is active) < 0.5% in staging under streaming + retry workloads.
- No material increase in metrics ingestion cost or cardinality alerts after enabling beta allowlist.
- Traces show experiment_id/variant_id on request spans for 99%+ of routed requests in enabled accounts.
- Documented conventions for attribute names (match existing OTel naming conventions thread) and metric tag keys.

## Notes / Open questions
- Naming conventions: align exact attribute keys with existing OTel conventions (see Slack thread "otel-span-tagging-conventions").
- Token-level cost: likely join in analytics instead of tagging per-token metrics. Need explicit sign-off from Nadia/Mei on join strategy.
- How to represent "no experiment": omit experiment_id/variant_id vs explicit "none". Current recommendation: omit to reduce cardinality and avoid misleading values.

## Rollout
- Behind feature flag route-experiments (beta allowlist).
- Enable in staging first, then 1-2 beta accounts, watch:
  - attribution drop rate
  - propagation lag correlation
  - metrics ingestion cardinality
- Expand allowlist once dashboards stay green for 48h.
text:
## Summary
We need consistent experiment attribution across traces and key metrics so Console can slice latency/cost/error by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id} with low overhead and without blowing up cardinality.

This ticket covers:
- Emitting experiment attribution fields in OTel spans (request + model execution spans)
- Emitting experiment dimensions on a minimal set of metrics (latency, errors, throughput) and ensuring cost attribution pipeline can join on the same identifiers
- Correctness for streaming, retries, and internal fallbacks
- Guardrails against high-cardinality tags / accidental per-request explosion

## Motivation / Why now
Route-level experiments are gated on trustworthy per-variant metrics. During Phase 1 integration testing we observed attribution drop in staging (esp. streaming + retry paths) and early concerns about cardinality/overhead when tagging OTel + metrics. This is a GA blocker because Console results depend on these dimensions.

## Requirements
### Canonical dimensions (per ADR-048)
- route_id (normalized template; not raw path)
- experiment_id
- variant_id
- model_id (already present in many places; ensure consistent naming)
- region (already present)

### Where to emit
1) Traces (OTel spans)
- Always set on the top-level request span and propagate into child spans where relevant.
- Must be present on:
  - router decision span (or equivalent)
  - inference execution span (for latency breakdown)
  - streaming send loop span(s) or annotate on the parent request span (avoid per-chunk tagging).

2) Metrics
- Tag a minimal, curated set of metrics with {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id}:
  - request_latency_ms (histogram)
  - request_error_total (counter)
  - request_success_total (counter)
  - request_throughput_rps (derived)
- For cost: do NOT tag per-token metrics with experiment dimensions at full fidelity if it will explode. Prefer joining in analytics using request_id -> experiment dims, or sampled expansions (see "Cardinality strategy").

### Correctness constraints
- Deterministic assignment: the experiment_id/variant_id must match the router decision for the request.
- Retries: experiment dimensions must persist across retry attempts (including streaming retries) so we don’t attribute the retry attempt to "no experiment".
- Fallbacks: when a variant triggers fallback model selection, experiment_id/variant_id should remain the assigned values (the experiment is still the policy that chose the initial variant). Model_id may differ due to fallback.
- Missing stickiness keys: if a request is bucketed via default key, we still emit attribution; however we must surface warnings elsewhere (Console validation / debug endpoint). This ticket focuses on emission.

### Performance constraints
- Additional span attributes must be constant-time and avoid allocations in hot paths.
- Any string formatting must be avoided in the request loop; use stable identifiers already present in request context.
- Avoid tagging high-volume metrics at per-token granularity with experiment dims.

## Cardinality strategy (must-have)
- Limit experiment tagging to a curated list of metrics, not all.
- Ensure route_id is normalized to route template / internal route identifier (not raw request path).
- Add safeguards:
  - max length limits for experiment_id/variant_id in telemetry fields
  - allowlist emission behind a feature flag during beta (route-experiments)
  - sampling for any detailed per-span child enrichment (if we decide to annotate additional spans)

## Implementation plan
1) Plumb experiment context
- Depend on request context plumbing work (see PR 28470).
- Router sets experiment_id/variant_id and stores them in request context.

2) OTel span attributes
- For request span: set attributes once, early.
- For child spans: propagate via context; do not re-compute.

3) Metrics tagging
- Tag only request-level metrics with experiment dims.
- Ensure streaming response metrics are attributed to the same request context.

4) Edge cases
- Streaming retries: ensure context survives into retry pipeline (fix expected; see follow-up PR referenced below).
- Async spans: ensure we don’t lose attributes when spans are created after request context is moved across goroutines/threads.

5) Validation
- Add tests:
  - unit test: for a request assigned to variant A, all expected spans include experiment_id/variant_id
  - integration test: streaming request with retry emits consistent experiment attribution
- Add staging smoke test dashboard: attribution drop rate and per-variant request count sanity.

## Acceptance criteria
- p95 attribution drop rate (missing experiment_id OR variant_id when experiment is active) < 0.5% in staging under streaming + retry workloads.
- No material increase in metrics ingestion cost or cardinality alerts after enabling beta allowlist.
- Traces show experiment_id/variant_id on request spans for 99%+ of routed requests in enabled accounts.
- Documented conventions for attribute names (match existing OTel naming conventions thread) and metric tag keys.

## Notes / Open questions
- Naming conventions: align exact attribute keys with existing OTel conventions (see Slack thread "otel-span-tagging-conventions").
- Token-level cost: likely join in analytics instead of tagging per-token metrics. Need explicit sign-off from Nadia/Mei on join strategy.
- How to represent "no experiment": omit experiment_id/variant_id vs explicit "none". Current recommendation: omit to reduce cardinality and avoid misleading values.

## Rollout
- Behind feature flag route-experiments (beta allowlist).
- Enable in staging first, then 1-2 beta accounts, watch:
  - attribution drop rate
  - propagation lag correlation
  - metrics ingestion cardinality
- Expand allowlist once dashboards stay green for 48h.

title: Quickstart Templates: KPI + telemetry requirements (events, tags, dashboards)
preview:
Goal
Define a minimal, privacy-safe telemetry spec for Quickstart Templates (RAG + agents) so we can measure adoption + activation ("time-to-first-success"), and so Console/Analytics can segment template-originated traffic.

This ticket produces:
1) Event + tag definitions (what we capture, where it’s emitted, cardinality rules)
2) Source-of-truth definitions for KPIs (especially TTFS)
3) Implementation guidance for SDK/templates + gateway allow-listing
4) Dashboard requirements for Console/Analytics

Context
DevEx is shipping language templates + a small CLI (init/doctor) and wants to measure:
- template adoption (created projects, active installs)
- activation (successful first run)
- retention (active installs after N days)
- support deflection (docs/troubleshooting effectiveness)

Constraints
- Must not leak prompts, user content, PII, or customer identifiers.
- Must avoid high-cardinality dimensions in metrics.
- Tagging must work behind restrictive corporate proxies; telemetry should be opt-in and degrade gracefully.

Non-goals
- Full product analytics instrumentation for all SDK usage.
- Any per-user tracking requiring customer consent flows.
- Capturing document contents, embeddings payloads, or tool outputs.

Proposed measurement model (high level)
We measure template success primarily via:
A) CLI/template-local events (init, doctor, deploy) -> sent to analytics via a lightweight endpoint (opt-in)
B) API request tagging (template tags on inference calls) -> server-side derived KPIs (first successful completion)
C) OTel spans/metrics (for operational visibility) -> not used as the primary source for product KPIs

KPI definitions (v1)
1) Activation rate: % of installs that reach a first successful completion within 60 minutes of init
- numerator: installs with a "first_success" within window
- denominator: installs with init

2) Time-to-first-success (TTFS)
TTFS is computed per install_id as:
- start: template_init timestamp (CLI) OR first tagged API request timestamp (fallback if CLI telemetry disabled)
- end: first API response that meets "success" criteria
- report median / p75 / p90 by template_name + language

Success criteria (for the API-derived end event):
- HTTP 2xx from inference endpoint
- streamed responses must complete normally (no client disconnect before final chunk) OR non-streaming returns a complete response
- response has > 0 output tokens
- not classified as a retry-exhausted/network failure

3) Template retention (D7)
% of installs that have >= 1 tagged successful completion on day 7 (UTC) after init

4) Support deflection proxy metric
Reduction in template-related tickets per 100 active installs (requires Support tagging alignment; tracked separately)

Telemetry: required tags (used for segmentation)
These are the only tags we intend to use for KPI dashboards.

Tag keys (stable):
- rw.template.name: e.g. "rag-agent"
- rw.template.lang: "python" | "typescript"
- rw.template.version: semantic version from template release (e.g. "0.3.1")
- rw.template.install_id: UUID v4 generated on init (stored locally in .redwood/template.json)
- rw.template.channel: "cli" | "github" (set by scaffolder; github = user cloned/copied)

Optional tags (allowed but not required for v1 KPIs):
- rw.template.deployment: "local" | "docker" | "kubernetes" | "fly" | "render" (bounded set)
- rw.template.vectorstore: "local" | "hosted" | "none" (bounded set)

Rules
- install_id must be random (UUID v4) and never derived from user/device identifiers.
- Do not include repo name, file paths, hostname, email, org name, customer_id, IP address, prompt text, retrieved chunks, or tool output.
- Values must be from bounded enumerations where possible. template.version is the only semi-unbounded field; OK for event analytics, NOT for metrics cardinality.

Where tags are emitted
1) API request tagging (primary)
Templates must attach the tags to inference requests via a Redwood-supported mechanism.
Implementation guidance (v1):
- Prefer a single allow-listed header carrying encoded key/value pairs (validated at gateway)
  - Header name: "x-redwood-template-tags"
  - Format: semicolon-separated "k=v" pairs; keys restricted to the list above; values length-limited (<= 64 chars per value)
- If header is blocked by a corporate proxy, templates must allow users to disable it via env var:
  - REDWOOD_TEMPLATE_TELEMETRY=off
- For users who disable headers, we still compute partial TTFS using first tagged API request if available; otherwise only CLI funnel metrics (if enabled)

2) CLI events (secondary)
CLI emits minimal events to Redwood analytics endpoint (opt-in). These events are useful for funnel drop-off analysis that the gateway can’t see.

CLI event schema (all fields optional except event_name + timestamp):
- event_name
- timestamp
- rw.template.name/lang/version/install_id/channel
- outcome: "success" | "failure" (for doctor/deploy)
- error_class (bounded; see below)

Event list (v1)
A) CLI events
1. template_init
- emitted when CLI scaffolds a project successfully
- includes: name/lang/version/install_id/channel

2. template_doctor_run
- emitted at start and end (two events or one with outcome)
- includes outcome and error_class if failure

3. template_deploy_attempt
- emitted when user runs deploy command (if we ship it in v1) OR runs guided deploy script

4. template_deploy_success

B) API-derived “activation” events (server-side)
These are derived by Analytics from tagged traffic; templates do not emit explicit “success” events.
5. template_first_tagged_request
- first time we see install_id on an API request

6. template_first_success
- first time we see a successful completion per install_id

Error taxonomy (bounded values)
Used ONLY for CLI outcomes + (optionally) template-local error reporting; not required for API-derived KPIs.
- auth_invalid_key
- auth_missing_key
- quota_rate_limited (429)
- quota_insufficient_balance
- network_timeout
- network_dns
- network_tls
- streaming_disconnect
- tool_schema_validation
- retrieval_empty
- retrieval_backend_error
- config_invalid
- otel_exporter_misconfigured
- unknown

Dashboard requirements (Console/Analytics)
1) Templates overview
- Active installs (7d)
- Activation rate (60m)
- Median TTFS (by template + lang)
- Top failure classes from CLI doctor (last 7d)

2) Funnel dashboard
init -> first_tagged_request -> first_success -> deploy_success (if available)
- drop-off at each step
- breakdown by lang and channel (cli vs github)

3) Operational correlation (link-out)
From a segment (template_name/lang) provide links to:
- template-tagged traffic filters in Console usage analytics
- SLO dashboard from observability-pack (latency, error rate, streaming disconnect rate)

Open questions / decisions
- Decision: make header-based tagging opt-in and documented due to known proxy header restrictions. Default remains ON for CLI-created projects, but README must include “turn off telemetry tags” option.
- Decision: do not use OTel as KPI source-of-truth (sampling + customer-controlled collectors make it unreliable for adoption metrics).

Acceptance criteria
- Written spec (this ticket) reviewed by DevEx + Observability + Privacy/Security.
- Analytics can compute TTFS and activation rate using:
  - CLI init events when available
  - API-derived first_success from tagged requests
- Cardinality guidelines documented and agreed (no freeform tags).
- Console/Analytics owners confirm dashboard fields and filtering requirements.

Links / related work
- PR: redwood-quickstarts #115 (template telemetry tags)
- PR: redwood monorepo #18421 (gateway allowlist for telemetry header)
- PR: redwood monorepo #18439 (Console filter for template-tagged traffic)
- Thread: #devex "template-telemetry-tagging-and-privacy" (opt-in + redaction)

Notes for implementers
- SDKs should expose a safe, explicit hook for passing template tags without requiring userland header mutation.
- Ensure request-id propagation: log fields must include Redwood request_id and install_id (install_id only if telemetry is enabled).
- Any telemetry endpoint for CLI must accept anonymous, unauthenticated events OR use API key with strict rate limits; do not require user identity.

title: Telemetry: tag Hosted requests with rollout_id + cohort and expose baseline vs canary metrics (cardinality-safe)
preview:
Problem
- Hosted canary rollouts require cohort-level observability so customers + SRE can compare baseline vs canary and auto-stop/rollback on SLO regressions.
- Today we can attribute metrics by route/model/region, but not by rollout cohort. We need cohort tagging end-to-end (data plane -> telemetry ingest -> aggregates -> dashboards/alerts) without causing a metric label cardinality explosion.

Scope
1) Request tagging
- Add request-scoped attributes for active rollout assignment:
  - rollout_id (string UUID)
  - rollout_cohort (enum: baseline|canary|none)
  - rollout_route_id (internal route key; should already exist as route_id)
- Ensure attributes are set consistently for:
  - streaming + non-streaming
  - retries (client retries + internal retries)
  - multi-region routing (cohort should be computed once per request and stay sticky)

2) Metrics emitted (cohort-aware)
- Emit/extend the following metrics with a low-cardinality cohort label:
  - hosted_requests_total{route, region, model_version, cohort}
  - hosted_request_errors_total{route, region, model_version, cohort, error_class}
  - hosted_request_timeouts_total{route, region, model_version, cohort}
  - hosted_request_latency_ms_bucket{route, region, model_version, cohort}
  - hosted_ttft_ms_bucket{route, region, model_version, cohort} (streaming)
  - hosted_tokens_out_total{route, region, model_version, cohort}
- Notes:
  - cohort is required for baseline vs canary comparisons.
  - rollout_id is NOT included on the core metrics above (see Cardinality Controls).

3) Rollout-specific drilldown (controlled rollout_id)
- Provide a dedicated metric namespace (or a controlled tag pathway) for per-rollout drilldown needed by Console timeline + SRE debugging:
  - hosted_rollout_requests_total{rollout_id, cohort, region}
  - hosted_rollout_latency_ms_bucket{rollout_id, cohort, region}
  - hosted_rollout_errors_total{rollout_id, cohort, region, error_class}
- Constraints:
  - Only emitted while rollout state == RUNNING or PAUSED (not historical forever).
  - TTL/retention aligned with rollout timeline needs (14 days default).
  - Enforced cap on concurrent active rollout_id series per tenant per region (see below).

4) Dashboards + alert templates wiring (engineering-owned portion)
- Ensure the telemetry side produces everything needed for:
  - Baseline vs canary comparison view (p50/p95, error rate, timeouts, TTFT)
  - Auto-stop/rollback stop conditions (ENG-9158)
- Dashboards and alert templates themselves are tracked separately (ENG-28910 / observability pack), but this ticket must validate the metrics are queryable and performant.

Cardinality controls / budgets
- Guiding rule: cohort is allowed on the normal Hosted metrics; rollout_id is only allowed on a small dedicated set, with TTL + caps.
- Implementation requirements:
  - Add rollout_id to the telemetry tag allowlist ONLY for the dedicated hosted_rollout_* series.
  - Enforce an "active rollout series" budget:
    - max_active_rollouts_per_tenant_per_region = 5 (configurable)
    - if over budget, drop rollout_id dimension and emit cohort-only metrics + add a structured log event "rollout_metrics_dropped" with tenant_id, rollout_id, reason=over_budget.
  - Ensure route/model labels remain normalized to stable IDs (no free-form route names).
  - Ensure error_class is bounded to a fixed set (e.g., upstream_5xx, timeout, overload, validation, unknown).

Acceptance criteria
- For a staged rollout with a known split (e.g., 90/10), cohort metrics reflect expected volume within tolerance after a 5–10 minute window.
- Console can query baseline vs canary for the same route/model_version/region using cohort label, without requiring rollout_id.
- Auto-stop logic can compute:
  - error rate regression (canary vs baseline)
  - p95 latency regression (canary vs baseline)
  - timeout rate regression
  using the new cohort metrics.
- No significant increase in TSDB series count outside the controlled hosted_rollout_* metrics.
  - Explicitly: rollout_id does not appear on hosted_* core metrics.
- Load test in staging shows ingest + query latency within existing SLOs for cohort dashboards (no >20% regression in query time for the key panels).

Implementation notes
- Data plane / request context
  - cohort assignment comes from Smart Routing (ENG-9123). We expect a resolved (rollout_id, cohort) in the routing decision. Telemetry must treat that as the source of truth.
  - For retries, propagate the original cohort decision via internal headers so telemetry stays consistent.

- Telemetry pipeline
  - Add new attributes into trace spans and structured request logs (always safe).
  - For metrics:
    - add cohort label to existing aggregates.
    - add new dedicated hosted_rollout_* aggregates gated by rollout active-state + series budget.

- Testing
  - Unit: label normalization + budget enforcement (over-budget behavior).
  - Integration: run a 90/10 split in staging, validate volume + p95 values differ predictably.
  - Regression: verify no cardinality blow-ups when creating many rollouts in a single tenant (simulate 20 rollouts; ensure only cohort metrics remain after budget).

References
- Cardinality analysis: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/rollout-cohort-metrics-cardinality-analysis.json
- SLO/alerts guidance: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/hosted-rollouts-slo-metrics-and-alerts.json
- Auto-stop/rollback wiring: ENG-9158
- Data plane split enforcement: ENG-9123

Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag: telemetry.rollout_cohort_metrics_enabled (default on in staging, off in prod)
- Enable for beta tenants first, monitor TSDB series + query latency, then ramp to GA.

Out of scope
- Automated quality metrics as a hard gate (may be added later as separate signals).
- Dedicated/Private cohort metrics parity (schema should not block it, but not required for Hosted GA).
text:
Problem
- Hosted canary rollouts require cohort-level observability so customers + SRE can compare baseline vs canary and auto-stop/rollback on SLO regressions.
- Today we can attribute metrics by route/model/region, but not by rollout cohort. We need cohort tagging end-to-end (data plane -> telemetry ingest -> aggregates -> dashboards/alerts) without causing a metric label cardinality explosion.

Scope
1) Request tagging
- Add request-scoped attributes for active rollout assignment:
  - rollout_id (string UUID)
  - rollout_cohort (enum: baseline|canary|none)
  - rollout_route_id (internal route key; should already exist as route_id)
- Ensure attributes are set consistently for:
  - streaming + non-streaming
  - retries (client retries + internal retries)
  - multi-region routing (cohort should be computed once per request and stay sticky)

2) Metrics emitted (cohort-aware)
- Emit/extend the following metrics with a low-cardinality cohort label:
  - hosted_requests_total{route, region, model_version, cohort}
  - hosted_request_errors_total{route, region, model_version, cohort, error_class}
  - hosted_request_timeouts_total{route, region, model_version, cohort}
  - hosted_request_latency_ms_bucket{route, region, model_version, cohort}
  - hosted_ttft_ms_bucket{route, region, model_version, cohort} (streaming)
  - hosted_tokens_out_total{route, region, model_version, cohort}
- Notes:
  - cohort is required for baseline vs canary comparisons.
  - rollout_id is NOT included on the core metrics above (see Cardinality Controls).

3) Rollout-specific drilldown (controlled rollout_id)
- Provide a dedicated metric namespace (or a controlled tag pathway) for per-rollout drilldown needed by Console timeline + SRE debugging:
  - hosted_rollout_requests_total{rollout_id, cohort, region}
  - hosted_rollout_latency_ms_bucket{rollout_id, cohort, region}
  - hosted_rollout_errors_total{rollout_id, cohort, region, error_class}
- Constraints:
  - Only emitted while rollout state == RUNNING or PAUSED (not historical forever).
  - TTL/retention aligned with rollout timeline needs (14 days default).
  - Enforced cap on concurrent active rollout_id series per tenant per region (see below).

4) Dashboards + alert templates wiring (engineering-owned portion)
- Ensure the telemetry side produces everything needed for:
  - Baseline vs canary comparison view (p50/p95, error rate, timeouts, TTFT)
  - Auto-stop/rollback stop conditions (ENG-9158)
- Dashboards and alert templates themselves are tracked separately (ENG-28910 / observability pack), but this ticket must validate the metrics are queryable and performant.

Cardinality controls / budgets
- Guiding rule: cohort is allowed on the normal Hosted metrics; rollout_id is only allowed on a small dedicated set, with TTL + caps.
- Implementation requirements:
  - Add rollout_id to the telemetry tag allowlist ONLY for the dedicated hosted_rollout_* series.
  - Enforce an "active rollout series" budget:
    - max_active_rollouts_per_tenant_per_region = 5 (configurable)
    - if over budget, drop rollout_id dimension and emit cohort-only metrics + add a structured log event "rollout_metrics_dropped" with tenant_id, rollout_id, reason=over_budget.
  - Ensure route/model labels remain normalized to stable IDs (no free-form route names).
  - Ensure error_class is bounded to a fixed set (e.g., upstream_5xx, timeout, overload, validation, unknown).

Acceptance criteria
- For a staged rollout with a known split (e.g., 90/10), cohort metrics reflect expected volume within tolerance after a 5–10 minute window.
- Console can query baseline vs canary for the same route/model_version/region using cohort label, without requiring rollout_id.
- Auto-stop logic can compute:
  - error rate regression (canary vs baseline)
  - p95 latency regression (canary vs baseline)
  - timeout rate regression
  using the new cohort metrics.
- No significant increase in TSDB series count outside the controlled hosted_rollout_* metrics.
  - Explicitly: rollout_id does not appear on hosted_* core metrics.
- Load test in staging shows ingest + query latency within existing SLOs for cohort dashboards (no >20% regression in query time for the key panels).

Implementation notes
- Data plane / request context
  - cohort assignment comes from Smart Routing (ENG-9123). We expect a resolved (rollout_id, cohort) in the routing decision. Telemetry must treat that as the source of truth.
  - For retries, propagate the original cohort decision via internal headers so telemetry stays consistent.

- Telemetry pipeline
  - Add new attributes into trace spans and structured request logs (always safe).
  - For metrics:
    - add cohort label to existing aggregates.
    - add new dedicated hosted_rollout_* aggregates gated by rollout active-state + series budget.

- Testing
  - Unit: label normalization + budget enforcement (over-budget behavior).
  - Integration: run a 90/10 split in staging, validate volume + p95 values differ predictably.
  - Regression: verify no cardinality blow-ups when creating many rollouts in a single tenant (simulate 20 rollouts; ensure only cohort metrics remain after budget).

References
- Cardinality analysis: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/rollout-cohort-metrics-cardinality-analysis.json
- SLO/alerts guidance: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/hosted-rollouts-slo-metrics-and-alerts.json
- Auto-stop/rollback wiring: ENG-9158
- Data plane split enforcement: ENG-9123

Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag: telemetry.rollout_cohort_metrics_enabled (default on in staging, off in prod)
- Enable for beta tenants first, monitor TSDB series + query latency, then ramp to GA.

Out of scope
- Automated quality metrics as a hard gate (may be added later as separate signals).
- Dedicated/Private cohort metrics parity (schema should not block it, but not required for Hosted GA).

title: TTL enforcement + purge job for request/response logs store
preview:
Goal
Implement TTL enforcement (scheduled purge) for the request/response logs data store, driven by the retention policy evaluation result for each tenant. Must be idempotent, observable, and safe under peak load.

Context
This is part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation (“Retention controls + compliance reporting pack”). Request/response logs are one of the primary data classes called out in security reviews; we need predictable deletion semantics and evidence (metrics + exports) that retention is being applied.

In-scope
- Add TTL/purge enforcement for request/response logs store ("request_logs" service DB/table/collection).
- Wire purge behavior to evaluated retention policy (per tenant, per region where applicable).
- Support safe backfills (existing rows) and ongoing incremental purging.
- Emit verification metrics and hook into standard dashboards/alerts.

Non-goals
- Per-field redaction or selective deletion (handled separately).
- Changing upstream log capture behavior (this ticket assumes logs are already being written when enabled).

Requirements / acceptance criteria
1) Correctness
- For tenants with request logging enabled: delete records older than configured retention TTL (as evaluated by control plane) with no tenant cross-contamination.
- For tenants with request logging disabled: no purge work should be performed for the request_logs store (or it should be a no-op); policy evaluation should return a disabled state and purge job should skip.
- Purge semantics: hard-delete (not tombstone) unless store-specific constraints require soft-delete; if soft-delete is used, include a follow-up task to guarantee physical deletion within a bounded window.
- Idempotency: re-running the job for the same time window does not error and does not create inconsistent state.

2) Performance and safety constraints
- Purge job must not cause sustained write amplification or lock contention on the request_logs store.
- Must operate with bounded work per run:
  - limit rows per batch
  - limit time per run
  - pacing between batches to avoid saturating DB
- Prefer index-friendly predicates (created_at < cutoff) and avoid full scans.
- Provide configuration knobs (via service config) for max rows/run, max runtime/run, and concurrency.
- Must degrade gracefully when lag/backlog exists (job keeps making progress; surfaces backlog; does not crash-loop).

3) Observability and verification
Emit (at minimum) per-store metrics:
- retention_ttl_lag_seconds{store="request_logs", tenant_tier, region} (distribution or gauge; avoid high-cardinality tenant labels)
- retention_purge_rows_deleted_total{store="request_logs", result="success|error"}
- retention_purge_failures_total{store="request_logs", error_class}
- retention_purge_backlog_seconds{store="request_logs"} (if backlog is computed; otherwise document how ttl_lag is interpreted)

Dashboards/alerts:
- Add panels for request_logs TTL lag and purge failure rate.
- Alerting guidance: page on sustained purge failures; ticket on increasing TTL lag over threshold.

4) Operational readiness
- Runbook updated/linked with specific request_logs details (where data lives, query predicate, batching knobs, how to pause/resume, how to validate).
- On-call friendly: include “DB pressure” mitigation steps (throttle, reduce batch size, disable concurrency, temporarily pause job).

Implementation notes / proposed approach
- Use the generic purge job framework (ENG baseline) with a request_logs-specific “deleter” implementation.
- Determine cutoff = now - evaluated_ttl for each tenant (or tenant group) and delete in chunks.
- Group work to avoid per-tenant loops where possible:
  - If store schema supports tenant_id + created_at index, perform deletes by tenant in bounded loops but ensure we don’t open unbounded transactions.
  - If we need to avoid tenant-cardinality in job scheduling, batch tenants into “shards” based on hash(tenant_id) and process shard ranges.
- Enforce upper bound: do not attempt to delete more than N rows/run; stop and report partial progress.

Testing plan
- Unit tests: cutoff calculation, idempotency, skip behavior when disabled.
- Integration tests in staging:
  - Create synthetic logs across time windows; verify deletion after job runs.
  - Induce transient DB errors; verify retry behavior and failure metrics.
  - Verify job respects throttles and does not exceed configured runtime.
- Load/perf checks:
  - Run job against a dataset approximating production cardinality and confirm no sustained DB CPU spike beyond agreed guardrails.

Rollout plan
- Stage 1: internal tenants only (feature flag / allowlist), 48h monitoring.
- Stage 2: design partners (Dedicated + Private where applicable), 1 week monitoring.
- Stage 3: broad enablement with SRE sign-off and dashboards live.

Open questions
- Confirm request_logs store partitioning strategy (if partitioned by day/week, deletion can drop partitions for faster performance).
- Confirm whether we should compute and store “oldest_remaining_timestamp” for lag computation vs deriving from table scans (preferred: derive from job progress + cutoff, avoid expensive queries).

Owner notes
Primary owner: Telemetry (Logan Wright). Secondary: SRE guardrails (Sean Gallagher / Allison Grant). Control plane policy evaluation owner for wiring: Jordan Lee.

title: Add metrics + dashboards for residency blocked requests (per-region + per-policy breakdowns)
preview:
Context
- As part of Hosted data residency enforcement, we need auditable + actionable telemetry around policy blocks.
- We need to answer: “Who is being blocked?”, “In which region?”, “By which policy scope (org/project/key)?”, “Is this a misconfig vs a routing fallback attempt?”, and “Is enforcement adding latency?”.

Goal
- Add first-class metrics and dashboards for residency enforcement decisions, especially BLOCK outcomes, with per-region breakdowns.
- Provide an SRE review checklist + alerting guidance for rollout (avoid paging storms on early allowlist).

Non-goals
- Full tracing rollout changes (we will emit structured log fields but not mandate end-to-end trace propagation work here).
- Deep customer analytics in-product (Console views are tracked elsewhere).

Proposed telemetry (authoritative names; align with Observability naming conventions)
1) Counters
- residency.enforcement.decisions_total
  - Incremented at gateway (authoritative) for every request with a residency decision.
  - Tags/labels:
    - outcome: allow | block | bypass
    - reason: none | no_policy | region_not_allowed | cross_region_fallback_disallowed | unknown_region | policy_service_error | config_invalid
    - surface: chat | completions | embeddings | rerank | other
    - org_tier: free | pro | enterprise (if available)
    - pinned_primary_region: <internal_region_id> | unset
    - selected_region: <internal_region_id> | unset
    - request_region: <internal_region_id> | unknown
    - enforcement_version: v1

- residency.enforcement.blocks_total
  - Convenience counter (or derived query) where outcome=block.
  - Same tags as above but keep cardinality controlled (see below).

- residency.enforcement.cross_region_attempts_total
  - Incremented when routing attempted to select a region outside allowed set (even if later blocked upstream).
  - Owner: Smart Routing (emitted from routing layer); used to detect regressions.
  - Tags:
    - from_region
    - attempted_region
    - fallback_reason (capacity | health | model_unavailable | policy)

2) Histograms
- residency.enforcement.decision_latency_ms
  - Time to evaluate policy + compute decision context in gateway.
  - Tags:
    - outcome
    - policy_source: org | project | api_key | none

- residency.policy_service.lookup_latency_ms
  - Time to fetch policy from control plane (if remote call).
  - Tags:
    - cache: hit | miss
    - result: ok | not_found | error

3) Gauges (optional, if cheap)
- residency.policy_service.cache_size
- residency.policy_service.cache_hit_ratio (if we have a canonical place; otherwise compute in dashboards)

Cardinality constraints (must-follow)
- DO NOT tag metrics with org_id/project_id/api_key_id/policy_id.
- If we need customer drill-down, rely on structured logs + log-based analytics with explicit sampling and access controls.
- Region IDs are bounded and OK as tags.

Structured log fields (for correlation; emitted on block and sampled on allow)
- residency.outcome
- residency.reason
- residency.allowed_regions (redacted list of region ids only)
- residency.selected_region
- residency.request_region
- residency.policy_source (org/project/api_key)
- residency.policy_id_hash (HMAC w/ internal key; allows grouping without exposing identifiers)

Dashboards (Observability pack)
1) Overview
- Block rate (%) over time
- Blocks by reason (stacked)
- Decisions by selected_region + request_region
- Decision latency p50/p90/p99

2) Rollout safety
- Blocks by surface (chat/completions/embeddings)
- Cross-region attempts (routing) vs blocks (gateway)
- Error contract success: count of blocks returning residency error code vs generic 5xx (guardrail)

3) Per-region health
- Block rate by request_region
- Decision latency by request_region

Alerting recommendations (initial thresholds; tuned during allowlist)
- Page (SEV2): residency.enforcement.blocks_total spikes > 5x baseline for 10m AND total requests > 1k/10m in a single region
- Page (SEV2): residency.enforcement.decision_latency_ms p99 > 50ms for 15m (policy service degradation)
- Ticket: cross_region_attempts_total > 0 for a pinned allowlist org in production (requires log drill-down; do not page unless sustained)

Acceptance criteria
- Metrics are emitted in production from gateway for streaming + non-streaming endpoints.
- Metrics are emitted in production from routing for cross-region attempts (with controlled tags).
- Dashboards exist in the standard folder: “Security & Compliance / Data Residency Enforcement”.
- SRE review checklist completed and attached in comments.
- Verified on staging with feature flag on:
  - Allowed request increments decisions_total (outcome=allow)
  - Blocked request increments blocks_total (outcome=block) with correct reason tag
  - Latency histograms populate

Implementation notes
- Gateway is source-of-truth for blocks (consistent error contract).
- Routing metrics help detect “attempted cross-region fallback” even if gateway blocks.
- If policy lookup errors occur, outcome should be “block” with reason=policy_service_error (per enforcement stance) and surfaced clearly to customers via error contract.

Links
- Drive draft metrics list: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/residency-enforcement-metrics-draft.json
- Dashboard/alerts spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/data-residency-enforcement-monitoring.json
- Related gateway error contract: ENG-4833
- Routing enforcement: ENG-4827

SRE review checklist (copy/paste)
- [ ] Cardinality reviewed (no org_id/policy_id tags)
- [ ] Metrics emitted on both success + failure paths (including streaming)
- [ ] Decision latency measured around policy eval only (not full request)
- [ ] Dashboard queries validated in staging + prod
- [ ] Alert thresholds set to non-paging during allowlist (or explicitly guarded by rollout flag)
- [ ] Runbook link added to alert annotations
- [ ] Privacy review: no customer content in metric labels; logs are redacted and access-controlled

title: Audit exporter: Datadog Logs destination adapter (API key, tag mapping, rate-limit/backoff)
preview:
## Context
We need a Datadog Logs destination for the SIEM-ready audit export beta (initial sinks: Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs, generic S3). This ticket covers the Datadog adapter implementation in `audit-log-exporter` including:
- API key handling + secure config plumbing
- Mapping audit event fields into Datadog-friendly tags/facets
- Rate limit detection and backoff strategy (avoid noisy retries / runaway lag)

Datadog should be usable by Security Ops teams who already centralize logs there, and should preserve audit semantics (stable `event_id`, categories, actor/resource metadata) while minimizing PII.

References:
- Confluence: `audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json`
- ADR: `adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency.json` (at-least-once delivery)
- ADR: `adr-audit-export-filtering-model.json`
- PII policy: `pii-redaction-and-sensitive-fields-policy-for-audit-events.json`

## Requirements / Acceptance Criteria
1) Destination type `datadog_logs` implemented behind SIEM export feature flag.
2) Auth: Datadog API key provided via secrets reference (no plaintext persistence in control plane DB; exporter receives resolved secret at runtime).
3) Transport: HTTPS to Datadog Logs intake endpoint(s), region-aware.
4) Payload: Each audit event exported as a single Datadog log entry, JSON-encoded, with consistent top-level fields and a stable message.
5) Mapping:
   - Add tags for `tenant_id`, `workspace_id` (if present), `category`, `action`, `outcome`.
   - Add facets/attributes for `actor.*`, `resource.*`, `request.*` with redaction policy applied.
   - Ensure `event_id` and `occurred_at` preserved for downstream correlation/dedup.
6) Rate limits:
   - Detect 429s and apply exponential backoff with jitter.
   - Respect `Retry-After` when present.
   - Cap retries per batch and dead-letter after threshold per delivery semantics.
7) Observability:
   - Per-destination counters for sent/failed/retried, plus latency and status-code breakdown.
   - Export lag should not grow unbounded due to tight retry loops.
8) Testing:
   - Unit tests for mapping + request building.
   - Integration test via stub endpoint in e2e harness (don’t require real Datadog org).

## Design Notes
### Destination configuration (exporter internal)
- Destination: `datadog_logs`
- Required:
  - `api_key_secret_ref`
  - `site` or explicit `intake_url` (default based on site)
- Optional:
  - `service` (default `redwood-inference`)
  - `source` (default `redwood.audit`)
  - `tags` (static tags list from customer, merged with Redwood-generated tags)

### Mapping details (Datadog)
Datadog log entry fields:
- `message`: concise string like `"audit_event"` (avoid stuffing data into message)
- `ddsource`: `redwood.audit`
- `service`: `redwood-inference`
- `hostname`: omitted
- `timestamp`: use `occurred_at` (RFC3339) when available; fallback to exporter enqueue timestamp for operational events.
- `attributes` (JSON):
  - `event_id`, `category`, `action`, `outcome`, `tenant_id`, `workspace_id`
  - `actor.type`, `actor.id`, `actor.email` (ONLY if allowed per policy; email should be optional and generally excluded unless Security approves)
  - `resource.type`, `resource.id`, `resource.name` (name optional; beware PII)
  - `request.ip` (optional; policy-controlled)
  - `request.user_agent` (optional; policy-controlled)
  - `export.destination_id`, `export.config_id` (for debugging)

Tagging strategy:
- Tags are low-cardinality only. Proposed tags:
  - `redwood_tenant:<tenant_id>`
  - `redwood_workspace:<workspace_id>` (only when present)
  - `audit_category:<category>`
  - `audit_action:<action>` (note: may be too high-cardinality depending on taxonomy; we added guardrails to cap/normalize)
  - `audit_outcome:<success|failure|unknown>`

Facet strategy:
- Everything else stays as attributes for querying.

PII minimization:
- Adapter must never attach request/response bodies.
- Ensure any freeform strings from upstream audit payload are either absent or explicitly allowlisted. If an event contains `metadata` blob, we export only allowlisted keys (handled upstream by audit schema v2 emitter; adapter adds a second layer of defense: drop unknown keys).

### Rate limit + backoff
- On HTTP 429:
  - If `Retry-After` present, sleep that duration (bounded by max backoff).
  - Else exponential backoff base 1s, factor 2, jitter +/- 20%, max 60s.
- On 5xx/timeouts:
  - retry with exponential backoff (same max), classify as transient.
- On 4xx auth errors (401/403):
  - treat as non-retryable (fail fast to surface misconfig); increment `adapter_auth_error_total` and mark destination unhealthy.

At-least-once semantics:
- Batches may be retried; downstream may see duplicates. Ensure `event_id` always present to enable dedup/correlation.

## Implementation Plan
- Implement `DatadogLogsAdapter`:
  - request building + batching (Datadog intake supports arrays)
  - gzip compression (match Splunk approach, reduce bandwidth)
  - status code classification
- Add config validation:
  - validate `site/intake_url` against egress allowlist hooks
  - validate API key secret ref is resolvable
- Add mapping helpers for tags and attributes:
  - ensure stable keys and normalization
- Integrate with exporter retry/DLQ infrastructure
- Add metrics:
  - `audit_export_adapter_requests_total{destination_type="datadog",status_code}`
  - `audit_export_adapter_retries_total{destination_type="datadog",reason}`
  - `audit_export_adapter_batch_size` histogram
  - `audit_export_adapter_request_duration_seconds` histogram

## Links
- GitHub PR (implementation): sources/github/audit-log-exporter/pr-1080-add-datadog-logs-adapter-and-tag-mapping.json
- Follow-on: ENG-5168 (metrics/tracing), ENG-5164 (DLQ/replay tooling), ENG-5189 (threat model)

## Rollout / Feature flag
- Destination exposed only when tenant is allowlisted for SIEM export beta.
- Default disabled; create destination via internal tooling / config API behind feature flag.

## QA Notes
- Added stub HTTP server to test:
  - 200 success path
  - 429 with Retry-After
  - 500 transient
  - 403 auth failure
- Verified gzip encoding and payload structure matches Datadog docs (JSON per line / JSON array mode).

## Comments / Updates
### 2025-02-14 (Caleb Johnson)
Drafted adapter skeleton + mapping proposal. Question: do we want `audit_action` as a tag? Might be too many unique values across product surfaces.

### 2025-02-17 (Marcus Lin)
+1 to keeping tags low-cardinality. `category` is fine; `action` should remain an attribute unless we cap/normalize. Also: do not export `actor.email` by default; only include `actor.id` + `actor.type`.

### 2025-02-19 (Logan Wright)
Observability request: ensure we can break down adapter errors by status class and expose "destination unhealthy" state to status endpoint.

### 2025-02-26 (Caleb Johnson)
Implemented 429 handling with `Retry-After` support and exponential backoff. Added auth error fast-fail path (403/401) -> marks destination unhealthy.

### 2025-03-03 (Sean Gallagher)
For beta, please cap max backoff at 60s and ensure we don’t busy-loop on repeated 429s. Also ensure exporter lag alerting won’t page on a single tenant misconfig (ticket-level vs page policy).

### 2025-03-05 (Caleb Johnson)
PR merged. Adapter ships behind flag. Added guardrail: `audit_action` is an attribute; only `category/outcome/tenant/workspace` are tags by default. Marking Done.

## Follow-ups
- Consider supporting EU intake sites explicitly in config UI copy (customer confusion risk).
- Confirm with Security whether IP/user agent fields are enabled for any beta cohort (policy default is off; might be a per-tenant toggle).
- Add docs snippet to Security FAQ about Datadog duplicates (at-least-once) and using `event_id` for correlation/dedup.

- Mapping audit event fields into Datadog-friendly tags/facets
- Rate limit detection and backoff strategy (avoid noisy retries / runaway lag)

Datadog should be usable by Security Ops teams who already centralize logs there, and should preserve audit semantics (stable `event_id`, categories, actor/resource metadata) while minimizing PII.

References:
- Confluence: `audit-event-schema-v2-and-siem-mapping.json`
- ADR: `adr-audit-export-delivery-semantics-and-idempotency.json` (at-least-once delivery)
- ADR: `adr-audit-export-filtering-model.json`
- PII policy: `pii-redaction-and-sensitive-fields-policy-for-audit-events.json`

title: Emit audit log events for residency policy create/update/delete + admin overrides
preview:
## Context
Hosted data residency enforcement introduces a new residency policy object (org/project/API key scope) and optional admin override mechanisms for incident mitigation / support workflows. Enterprise customers and auditors expect a complete, immutable audit trail for:
- policy lifecycle changes (create/update/delete)
- changes to policy enforcement status (enabled/disabled)
- admin overrides that temporarily relax residency enforcement or allow cross-region fallback

This ticket adds control-plane emitted audit log events and wires them into the audit-log-exporter pipeline.

Links:
- PRD: Hosted data residency enforcement (Confluence)
- Design/Model: Residency policy model + admin UX (Confluence)
- ADR-021: Region pinning enforcement boundaries (Confluence)
- Related work: ENG-4821 (policy schema + validation), ENG-4827 (routing enforcement)
- Implementation PR (target): sources/github/redwood/pr-21561-add-audit-log-events-for-residency-policy-updates.json

## Goals
1) Emit audit log events for residency policy lifecycle changes:
   - create
   - update
   - delete
2) Emit audit log events for admin override actions (if/when enabled behind feature flag):
   - override enabled (relaxation granted)
   - override updated (scope/expiry changed)
   - override disabled/expired
3) Ensure events are:
   - consistent with Redwood audit log conventions
   - safe (no customer prompt/content in payload)
   - attributable (actor identity + auth context)
   - exportable via audit-log-exporter (customer SIEM / log archive)

## Non-goals
- Backfill historical policy changes from before event emission.
- Customer-facing UI changes (tracked under Console work).

## Proposed event types + naming
Audit log event names (string constants):
- residency.policy.created
- residency.policy.updated
- residency.policy.deleted
- residency.policy.override.enabled
- residency.policy.override.updated
- residency.policy.override.disabled

Rationale: namespace aligns with other security control-plane events and stays stable for customer SIEM rules.

## Event schema (fields)
All events must include the standard Redwood audit envelope fields (existing):
- event_id
- event_name
- event_time
- actor_type (user | service | api_key)
- actor_id (internal principal id)
- actor_email (when actor_type=user; omit otherwise)
- org_id
- ip_address (best-effort; from admin request context)
- user_agent (best-effort)
- request_id / trace_id (to correlate with API gateway/routing logs)

Event-specific fields (encoded into the audit event "details" string per current audit-log service constraints):
- policy_id
- scope_type (org | project | api_key)
- scope_id (e.g., project_id, api_key_id)
- allowed_regions (comma-separated internal region IDs; e.g., eu-west-1, eu-central-1)
- primary_region
- enforcement_mode (enforce | monitor)  // monitor used during rollout/dogfood
- allow_cross_region_fallback (true|false)
- change_summary (human-readable short diff summary; safe)
- changed_fields (comma-separated; e.g., allowed_regions, primary_region)

For updates, include previous values where safe and practical:
- previous_allowed_regions
- previous_primary_region
- previous_enforcement_mode
- previous_allow_cross_region_fallback

Override fields:
- override_id
- override_reason (required; validated; no customer content)
- override_expires_at
- override_scope (policy_id or org_id)

### Data handling notes
- Do NOT log API key material, tokens, prompts, completions, embeddings, or request payloads.
- api_key_id is acceptable (internal identifier only). If there is any risk of the ID being used as a lookup by unauthorized parties, hash it before emission (confirm with Security).
- policy configuration is considered "customer configuration metadata" and is allowed cross-region per ADR-021, but audit events should still be emitted and stored in-region when possible (hosted audit log store is region-aware; confirm exporter behavior).

## Implementation plan
1) Control plane: residency-policy-service
   - On create/update/delete of residency policy, publish audit event to audit-log service.
   - Ensure events include actor identity from auth context (user vs service vs api_key).
   - Update path must compute changed_fields and include previous_* fields.

2) Admin override path
   - Add audit events to the override API handler(s).
   - Enforce override_reason required (minimum length + denylist patterns) and include in event.

3) audit-log-exporter wiring
   - Confirm exporter includes new event names and doesn’t filter them out.
   - Add mapping tests / snapshot tests for event serialization.

4) Testing
   - Unit tests for:
     - create/update/delete emits correct event_name and required fields
     - update with no-op changes does NOT emit event (or emits with changed_fields empty) — decide and document
     - override enable/disable emits events with expiry + reason
   - Integration test (staging) to verify events appear in exported stream.

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] Creating a residency policy emits residency.policy.created with actor/org/policy/scope and new config.
- [x] Updating allowed_regions and/or primary_region emits residency.policy.updated with changed_fields and previous_* values.
- [x] Deleting a policy emits residency.policy.deleted with policy_id + scope.
- [x] Enabling/disabling/updating an admin override emits corresponding residency.policy.override.* events.
- [x] No customer content is present in payload (validated via code review + test assertions).
- [x] Events are visible in audit-log-exporter output in staging.
- [x] Security review sign-off on event fields (Marcus / Aisha delegate).

## Open questions
1) Do we treat enable/disable of enforcement_mode (monitor->enforce) as an "update" event only, or also a distinct event name (residency.policy.enforcement_enabled)?
   - Proposal: keep as residency.policy.updated to limit event taxonomy. Include changed_fields=enforcement_mode.
2) Should we include full allowed_regions list on delete events?
   - Proposal: include it if readily available at delete time; otherwise policy_id + scope is sufficient.
3) Export residency events to customer SIEM by default or gated by enterprise plan?
   - Assume: audit export is enterprise; events should still be emitted internally regardless.

## Rollout
- Behind feature flag: audit_residency_policy_events (default ON in staging, progressive in prod).
- Dogfood: internal orgs with pinned EU-only policies.
- Monitoring: count of emitted events per event_name; alert on sustained emission failures.

## Progress / updates
- 2025-02-13 (Logan Wright): Drafted event names + field list; sent to Marcus Lin + Olga Petrov for GRC expectations.
- 2025-02-18 (Marcus Lin): Confirmed no customer content; configuration metadata allowed. Requested api_key_id hashing check.
- 2025-02-21 (Logan Wright): Implemented create/update/delete emission in residency-policy-service; added changed_fields diff.
- 2025-02-24 (Felix Schneider): Confirmed Console "configuration history" can consume audit stream; no additional fields needed.
- 2025-02-26 (Rafael Mendes): Asked for staging validation + exporter verification before enabling in prod.
- 2025-03-02 (Logan Wright): PR up with exporter wiring + tests. Staging verified: events appear with request_id correlation.

## Code / artifacts
- PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-21561-add-audit-log-events-for-residency-policy-updates.json

## Notes for Support / Security
- Audit events can be used to answer "who changed residency policy settings" and "when was an override applied".
- For incident response, correlate audit request_id with gateway/routing logs to confirm whether blocks were caused by policy change or enforcement rollout.

title: Reconciliation report: unit economics aggregates vs billing totals
preview:
Build a reconciliation view/report that compares the dashboard’s blended $/1M output tokens numerator/denominator components against Billing/RevOps totals to detect discrepancies early.

Why:
- Finance signoff requires we can explain (and quantify) gaps between platform metering aggregates and billed usage/spend inputs.
- SRE/oncall needs a fast way to determine whether a spike/drop is real vs a data pipeline issue.
- Product needs guardrails so exec readouts don’t get derailed by “these numbers don’t match billing”.

Scope (v1):
- Warehouse views that compute comparable aggregates over a shared time window:
  1) Metered tokens (output tokens as primary; include input tokens for debugging)
  2) Metered request counts (streaming vs non-streaming)
  3) Allocated cost numerator used by blended cost (GPU + overhead/idle allocations per ADR)
  4) Billing totals (token-based charges / invoice usage totals) at the closest matching granularity we have internally
- A single reconciliation table/view that outputs deltas and delta% by day and by tier (hosted/dedicated/private/enterprise), with drilldown by region and model as “debug mode”.
- Document known expected differences and make them explicit in the report output (e.g., tax/credits excluded, billing time zone boundaries, free-tier credits, retries).

Non-goals (v1):
- Perfect parity across all historical periods; goal is to make gaps visible and actionable.
- Customer-facing reporting.
- Re-deriving invoice line items exactly (Billing remains source of truth for invoiced amounts).

Success criteria:
- Finance can use the report to sign off on the metric definition for blended $/1M output tokens (within agreed tolerance bands).
- Oncall can answer: “Is this dashboard change due to metering or due to rate card/cost allocation changes?” within 30 minutes.

Deliverables:
- Warehouse objects:
  - finance.recon_unit_econ_vs_billing_daily (primary)
  - finance.recon_unit_econ_vs_billing_daily_debug (optional, heavier view)
- Console/internal link: add a link from the unit economics dashboard to the reconciliation view (can be a query link / saved Looker/Grafana panel in v1).
- Short runbook section: how to interpret common mismatch patterns and who to page.

Reconciliation logic (draft):
- Time window: UTC day by default; add a column showing Finance reporting day boundary (America/Los_Angeles) to catch boundary issues.
- Join keys:
  - customer_tier (derived via ENG-8423 logic; keep raw tier_source for debugging)
  - region (normalized via ENG-8424 mapping; include “unknown” bucket)
  - product_surface (hosted api vs dedicated vs private) where billing supports it
- Measures:
  - metered_output_tokens (from unit economics hourly aggregates)
  - billed_output_tokens_or_equivalent (from billing usage tables; may be “total tokens” depending on plan)
  - metered_usage_usd (if available; otherwise compute from rate card only for directional parity)
  - billed_usage_usd (pre-tax, pre-credits by default; include credits as separate columns)
- Deltas:
  - token_delta = metered_output_tokens - billed_tokens
  - token_delta_pct = token_delta / NULLIF(billed_tokens,0)
  - usd_delta = allocated_cost_usd - billed_usage_usd (explicitly label as “cost vs revenue” if comparing across concepts)

Known expected differences to encode:
- Billing may include/minus customer credits; dashboard cost metric explicitly excludes credits (cost-side only).
- Billing may be rounded/aggregated per request; metering is token-granular.
- Dedicated/private contracts may be capacity-based; usage tables can be incomplete or not meaningful. For these tiers, reconciliation focuses on token counts parity, not $ parity.

Tolerances (proposal; pending Finance signoff):
- Hosted (token-billed): daily token delta within ±1.5% and within ±0.5% on 7-day trailing average.
- Dedicated/Private: token delta within ±2.5% daily, and no sustained drift > 1% over 7 days.

Open questions:
1) For tiers where billing is capacity-based (Dedicated/Private), what is the authoritative “billing total” to compare against? (Likely: token parity only + optional cost numerator sanity vs rate card inputs.)
2) Which billing dataset is stable enough for automated daily reconciliation (RevOps export vs internal billing DB)?
3) How do we handle late-arriving billing adjustments (credits/reversals) without creating alert noise?

Links:
- ADR: adr-034-unit-economics-cost-attribution-model
- Metric dictionary: unit-economics-dashboard-metrics-and-definitions
- Related: ENG-8426 (hourly aggregation job), ENG-8429 (data quality checks/anomaly alerts)

title: Unit Economics: data quality checks + anomaly alerts (completeness, drift, token drops)
preview:
Goal
Ship the v1 data quality guardrails for the Unit Economics pipeline so Finance/Product/Eng can trust blended $/1M output tokens. Specifically:
- Completeness checks for hourly aggregates and key dimensions (tier/region/model/quantization_profile)
- Drift/anomaly checks (sudden output token drops, spikes in unknown dimension values)
- Alerts routed to the right channels with runbook links and clear severity

Context
We have known fragility around:
- Streaming path output token under-counting (see ENG-8421)
- Region normalization null/unknown mapping (ENG-8424)
- Tier derivation edge cases (ENG-8423)

This ticket adds the monitoring layer so we catch regressions quickly and prevent exec-facing reporting from silently degrading.

Scope (v1)
1) Hourly completeness checks
- Pipeline freshness: hourly partition exists for the last N hours (N=2 for warning, N=4 for critical)
- Hourly coverage by required dims:
  - region: % rows with region='unknown' <= threshold
  - customer_tier: % rows with customer_tier='unknown' <= threshold
  - model_identifier: % rows with model_identifier='unknown' or NULL <= threshold
  - quantization_profile: allow 'unset' for non-quantized routes, but cap unexpected values
- Missing-dimension detection: if a dimension suddenly disappears for a common slice (e.g., no 'eu-west' rows for hosted tier in last hour)

2) Drift/anomaly checks
- Output token drop detector:
  - Compare total output_tokens (all tiers) for hour H to a baseline (median of same weekday/hour over last 2 weeks, or rolling 7d median if sparse)
  - Alert when drop > 25% (warn) or > 40% (critical), gated by minimum baseline volume
- Token-per-request drift (sanity): detect implausible changes in avg_output_tokens/request (helps catch counting bugs that keep request volume stable)
- Unknown dimension spike:
  - If unknown region/tier spikes by > 2x compared to 7d median, alert (usually mapping/ingestion regression)

3) Alerting + routing
- Emit check results as metrics + a small "dq_check_failures" table for debugging (so we can render a simple internal view)
- Create alert rules:
  - Severity: page only on critical freshness/token-drop issues; warnings go to Slack
  - Route:
    - Slack: #eng-sre, #eng-platform (and #eng-oncall for critical)
    - PagerDuty: "Telemetry & Analytics" oncall (critical only)
- Include direct runbook links in alert payload (ENG-8435) and the exact failing check name + the affected window/dimension.

Implementation notes
- Checks run as part of the hourly aggregation DAG tail (after materialized views complete) to avoid false positives.
- Use a small config to centralize thresholds:
  - freshness_lag_hours_warn=2
  - freshness_lag_hours_crit=4
  - token_drop_warn=0.25
  - token_drop_crit=0.40
  - unknown_region_warn=0.01 (1%) and crit=0.05 (5%) (tune after first week)
  - unknown_tier_warn=0.005 (0.5%) and crit=0.02 (2%)
- Ensure checks are dimension-aware but cardinality-safe (no per-customer breakdowns; limit to top-level slices only).

Acceptance criteria
- [x] If an hourly partition is missing or delayed beyond thresholds, an alert fires with the missing hour(s) and the affected table/view name.
- [x] If streaming output token metering regresses and total output_tokens drops materially, alert fires within 1 hour.
- [x] If region/tier mapping breaks and unknown spikes, alert fires with the suspected dimension.
- [x] Alerts include runbook link + owners + remediation hints (backfill, rerun job, verify mapping tables).
- [x] Noise is tuned: no paging for transient single-hour blips during planned maintenance; warnings are rate-limited.

Links
- Confluence: Unit economics pipeline SLO: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/unit-economics-pipeline-slo.json
- Confluence: Metric dictionary / definitions: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/unit-economics-dashboard-metrics-and-definitions.json
- Related PRs:
  - sources/github/redwood/pr-19383-add-data-quality-checks-for-economics-pipeline.json
  - sources/github/observability-pack/pr-812-add-unit-econ-dashboards-and-alerts.json

Rollout plan
- Dogfood week: warnings only (no paging) while we establish baselines and tune thresholds.
- Week 2: enable critical paging for freshness + token-drop only.
- Week 3: enable unknown-dimension spike paging only if it correlates with dashboard breakage (otherwise Slack warning).

Open questions
- Baseline method: lock to same weekday/hour vs rolling median? (initial: rolling 7d median; revisit with Finance once seasonality patterns are clearer)
- Separate "live" vs "finance" views: checks should run against the finance-aligned aggregates (so Finance sees the same reality).

title: Warehouse view performance optimization for Unit Economics drilldowns
preview:
Context
The internal Unit Economics dashboard (Console) relies on hourly aggregates and drilldown queries (top-N by model/region/tier; time series w/ group_by). In dogfood, several drilldowns are timing out or returning in 8–25s for common filters (7d / all regions / hosted + dedicated), which is too slow for the intended workflow (Finance + Eng diagnosing regressions).

Problem statement
Warehouse views are currently computed via on-the-fly GROUP BY over the hourly fact table for most endpoints. The fact table is large and growing quickly; common queries scan too many partitions and spill during sort/aggregation. Additionally, some group_bys (model_version + quant_profile) are forcing wide aggregations that defeat caching.

Goal / success criteria
- P95 dashboard query latency in the backend <= 2.0s for:
  - 24h and 7d time series grouped by {model, region, tier}
  - top-N (N<=50) breakdowns by {model, region, tier, quant_profile}
- P99 <= 5.0s for the above under typical concurrent dashboard usage (10–20 req/min internal).
- No material changes to metric correctness vs the canonical hourly aggregates.
- Freshness preserved: new hours available <= 90 minutes after the hour closes (same as pipeline SLO).

Proposed approach
1) Partitioning
- Ensure hourly fact table is partitioned by ds (date) and clustered by hour + (region, customer_tier) to reduce scan volume for default dashboard filters.
- Verify query planner is pruning partitions for ds/hour filters coming from the API.

2) Pre-aggregation / materializations
Create additional materialized views for the highest-volume drilldowns so the API does not have to group on the fly:
- mv_unit_econ_hourly_by_model_region_tier
- mv_unit_econ_hourly_by_model_version_quant_region_tier (limited dimensions; see cardinality guardrails)
- mv_unit_econ_daily_by_model_region_tier (for Finance-oriented ranges >= 30d)

3) Indexing / clustering keys (warehouse-specific)
- Add clustering on (model_id, model_version, quantization_profile) for the pre-aggregated tables used in top-N.
- Add secondary index / sort key on (ds, hour) for the main hourly tables if supported (otherwise rely on partition pruning + clustering).

4) API-side query shaping
- Ensure API always includes ds/hour bounds.
- For long ranges, route to daily materializations ("finance" view) vs hourly ("live" view) to avoid huge scans.
- Cap group_by combinations that create pathological cardinality; return a validation error with guidance.

Out of scope
- Changing the cost attribution model or token metering definitions.
- Adding new dimensions beyond what’s already in the v1 data model.

Investigation notes (current bottlenecks)
- Top-N by model across 7d is scanning full hourly fact and doing a global sort; this is avoidable.
- Grouping by model_version + quant_profile is high-cardinality for some models with experimental profiles; needs both guardrails and pre-agg that keeps dimensions limited.
- Several queries were missing ds filter due to default time range being applied in the app but not consistently passed through to the SQL generator for comparison mode.

Acceptance criteria / deliverables
- Materialized views created + backfilled for last 30 days.
- Query plans verified (partition pruning and no full table scan for default filters).
- Benchmarks recorded for key endpoints (before/after) and added to the engineering notes in this ticket.
- Rollout plan: ship behind internal feature flag; monitor warehouse load and dashboard latency; adjust refresh cadence if needed.

Links
- PR (expected): sources/github/redwood/pr-19405-warehouse-view-performance-optimizations.json
- Related performance thread: sources/slack/eng-platform/1737501201-warehouse-view-slow-queries.json
- SQL scratchpad: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/sql-prototypes-unit-economics-views.json
